WOMAN'S EXPONENT
The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations
SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JUNE 15, 1882			No. 2.


AFTER THE SAD NEWS.

May 12th, 1882.

No! little one.  No! love; not now, not now!
Let mamma's hand lay gently on your brow,
And nestle your soft curls against my cheek:
So rest, and do not move abour nor speak.
Sweet, Willie sleeps to-night without a song,
And so can you, my precious Lee, ere long.
The sky is dark, oppressive seems the air,
with melancholy shadows everywhere;
and sadness breathes in all the sounds of Spring;
And mamma's vaioce would break--she cannot sing!
"Why does poor grandma bend her head so low?"
Hush! darling, don't disturb her--"Sleeping?" No!
Grandma is weary, and she bows her head
In sorrow, for dear  grandpa who, a month to-day,
bade us goodbye, and started on his way,
Soon to return; and now, how strange it seems,
And dark as though our lives were troubled dreams!
Oh! can it be, that he will come no more,
To cheer and bless us, as so oit before!
Half chiding thus, my cherub's restless mood,
watching my mother, plae and grief subdued,
My own heart burdened with its weight of carcs,
Grasps Lee's next words--"I have not said my prayers!"
Happy reminder! When in Jesus' name
The Holy Comforter's blest aid we claim, 
The memory of his death and sufferings,
Takes from our sorrows all their keenest stings;
We feel, with his great victory in view,
That we and ours shall live and triumph too.
							LULA.


A REPRESENTATIVE WOMAN.
MARY ISABELLA HORNE.
Continued.
It is not anticipated that in this brief narra-
tive one can describe all the trying scenes and 
hardships through which Mrs. Horne passed
during the persecutions and drivings of the
Saints; but merely to state a few facts that the
world may be informed in regard to the afflic-
tions and patient endurance of those who were
the sufferers from mobs and drivings in the
early days of the church. To tell it all would
be a task for more than mortal, only the angels
who watched over and guarded the people
then, could do that.
In the month of August of 1938, before Mrs.
Horne had fairly recovered from her recent
illness, and while her babe was less than a
month old, she removed with her family to Far
West, where the largest body of the Saints were
located; and from the exposure took chills and
fever, which prostrated her for some time. At
one time on the route, they stopped at night on
a prairie where they could neither obtain wood
or water, and were tormented with mosquitoes
beyond endurance; Mrs. Horne was compelled
to keep a handkerchief waving all night over
herself and children, and the horses were so
restless, on account of being bitten by these
insects, that, in the middle of the night, Mr.
Horne was obliged to harness them and pro-
ceed on the journey in the dark. It was
8 o'clock in the morning before they reached
water, and then it made them so sick that they
were obliged to discontinue the journey for
the day. When they arrived at Far West
they could find no suitable place to live in
but were obliged to go into a log house with-
out doors or windows; it was built on blocks of
wood and was so high from the ground that

the sheep would lie under the building bleat-
ing all night, which was a very great annoy-
ance. In this miserable log cabin Mrs. Horne
lived during the time of the persecutions of
the Saints. Here her eldest daughter was
taken very ill, and her life  despaired of, in
fact it seemed impossible for her to get better.
The mother of the Prophet, Mrs. Lucy Smith,
came and blessed the child, and said she should
live. This was something new in that age, for
a woman to administer to the sick, but the
Lord blessed the administration and manifested
his power in behalf of the child, and she
speedily recovered. It was about this time
that the excitement in Missouri raged, and the
persecution was at its height. Mrs. Horne
was left much alone nights, as well as days,
with her little ones, her husband being de-
tained on guard; she knew not at what moment
the mob would break in and destroy them all.
But in all these trying scenes she never fal-
tered, or feared, but trusted in God for deliver-
ance.
 The mob threatened women and children
with all sorts of dreadful things, and in many
instances carried their inhuman threats into
execution. They wanted to get possession of
the Prophet and the leaders of the people, as
the Jews did of Jesus and the Apostles; and
they hunted Joseph down, upon one pretext
and another, until they accomplished their
wicked design. Mrs. Horne says, "I shall never
forget the  night when our Prophet and his
brethren were taken to the enemy’s camp. If
all the fiends of the lower regions had been let
loose they could not have made a more hideous
noise than was made by the mob that night.
They were clamoring and exulting over our
brethren during the whole night. The next
day, while I was standing in the door of the
house, several of the mob rode up and said,
'Bid your prophet good-bye, for you will not
see him again until you see him in hades,’
but not using so mild a word. The Saints sor-
rowed and mourned for him, and the Lord
eventually delivered him out of their hands.”
Mrs. Horne soon after removed with her hus-
band and family, and the family of her hus-
band's father, to Plattsburg, about twenty miles
from Far West, where they spent the winter;
ten in number in one room. Mrs. Horne was
sick with chills and fever and her life despaired
of—her babe too was afflicted with erysipelas,
but the Lord healed them and brought them
safe through all these trying scenes. In the
Spring of 1839 Mrs. Horne and family left
the state of Missouri as exiles, and sought an
asylum in Illinois, and arrived in Quincy, on 
the Mississippi River, in the early part of May.
Here, for a time, they found peace and rest 
from their oppressors. Mrs. Horne assisted her
husband in supporting the family, by taking
in fine sewing. She has ever been a helpmeet
in very deed, and her hands are never idle.
Many of the beautiful sayings of Solomon in
the thirty-first chapter of Proverbs, might
well be applied to her:
"She looketh well to the ways of her house-
hold and eateth not the bread of idleness."
"Her children arise up, and call her blessed:
her husband also, and he praiseth her."
"Her husband is known in the gates, when
he sitteth among the elders of the land."
	There words are truly applicable to Mrs.
Horne for she has labored diligently in her 
own household and her family have profited
by her teaching and example.

In Quincy her children had scarlet fever,
and, notwithstanding the careful nursing and
the medical attendance of Dr. Williams, a well
known physician there, the eldest little girl
died on the 26th of November, 1839. Mrs.
Horne’s own health was poor, and her cares
and anxiety were very great. On the 16th of
July 1840, another son was born to them.
They named him William Joseph. In the
Fall Mrs. Horne visited Nauvoo, meeting there
with many of her old friends and acquaintan-
ces, who, also, had passed through the
trials and persecutions in Missouri. The next
year, 1841, Mr. and Mrs. Horne went to
Nauvoo to the celebration of the 4th of July.
Returning home they encountered in the night
a most terrific storm of thunder and lightning,
the darkness was intense, except when the vivid
flashes of lightning illimined the surroundings.
They had no covers to the wagons, or protec-
tion from the storm, and were thus exposed to
the rain, which fell in torrents, and they were
drenched through and through. The exposure
was too great and Mrs. Horne and  babe were
both taken sick. Again, she says "we employed
a doctor, at the earnest solicitations of our
friends;" but the baby died on the 26th day of
July. The mother felt so grieved that she
promised the Lord she would never employ
another doctor to  please anybody, but would
exercise faith in the ordinances of the Church,
and leave the result with Him. This covenant
she has faithfully kept up to the present time.


COMMENTS.

		BY ONE WHO KNOWS.

	An article headed "The origin of Mor-
monism," read by Mrs. Dr. Horace Eaton, of
Palmyra, N. Y., who  says she has resided in
that place for the last thirty years, speaks not
from what she knows, but from what she has
heard against the Prophet Joseph Smith, and
family. she says, "They went out from us,
but they were not of us. The deceived were
elsewhere."
	Now if Mrs. Dr. Horace Eaton  was ever
familiar with the Holy Scriptures she must
have forgotten this passage, "A man is not
without honor except in his own country," and
the Lord said, "I will take one from a house
and two from a city," etc.
	Having been born in the State of New York
myself, and connected with the Church of Lat-
ter-day Saints from the beginning, and acquain-
ted with the mother of the Prophet from my
childhood up, I am able to testify, from actual
knowledge, (which statement ought to be re-
lied on), that this woman speaks falsely against
the Prophet Joseph Smith and his parents, and
hundreds are still living who are able to testify
that they were among the noblest and most
choice speciments of God's handiwork; and the
inventors of such wicked, malicious and bare-
faced falsehoods, what can be said of them?
"that they know not what they do!" We could
with quite as much truth and propriety say
that the mother of the late President Garfield,
because she was poor, was ignorant and dis-
honest, moved in the lowest walks of life; and
that "an evil spirit had inspired both mother
and son;" it would be as true in this case as the
other; to work as they had to for years, endur-
ing many hardships and privations; and that he
was taught to restrain the instincts of child-
hood, impressing this idea upon the boy that


[Column 1]
he must be diligent in study, to obtain a suit-
able education to prepare him for what he was
destined to become: President of the United
States. This would be no more absurd than
the contemptible stuff read by Mrs. Eaton of
Palmyra to blast the character of the honored
dead, supposing by this dishonest means, that
she can break up and destroy "Mormonism."
	We know from the history of many of the
greatest men and women, that those of the high-
est intellectual order whose minds soared above
the common cares and drudgeries of life, and
their ideas and aims were the most pure, noble
and exalted, with the dreamers and visionary
minded who were most richly endowed with
spiritual gifts, have, we may say, with scarcely
an exception, been looked down upon, and
made the butt of ridicule for the more worth-
less, ignorant and faithless portion to pick upon,
who invariably set them down as silly, super-
stitious fools, or lazy and good-for-nothing
drones.
	When Noah, in obedience to the command of
the Almighty, told the people that He would
send a flood to drown the world, they only
ridiculed and called him a fool; and Joseph,
who was a dreamer, was hated by his brethren,
who sold him into Egypt, where they were
afterwards humbled before him. He was des-
tined to become the greatest and a temporal
savior to his father's house.
	Columbus, who discovered America, was de-
nounced as a fool or a most demented being.
These are only a few cases, and we could men-
tion many who have made the greatest discov-
eries and were similarly treated, winning no
praises nor recompense in this life, but though
they lived and died in obscurity, and many of
them in the most wretched poverty, their works
have followed them. I am not only thankful,
but proud to be able to say that my parents
were among the few who first received the gos-
pel, as taught by Joseph Smith, the Prophet of
latter-days; and that I, with them. was gath-
ered out from among the "rank weeds" of
"Western New York," because we were not of
them; and that, with a few other choice tendrils,
we were transplanted upon the rich and fertile
soil of the "Far West;" where under a more
congenial clime, though we were moved about
from place to place because of wicked usurpers,
who scattered and killed many; and sought to
destroy us all both root and branch; but hav-
ing been watched over and continually pruned,
weeded and watered from the pure fountain,
we, with thousands more who have been gath-
ered from the east and west, from the north and
south and grafted in, have thriven and grown
together and taken root in these once barren
but now fruitful vales of the Rocky Moun-
tains, and, in spite of the anxious prayers
and opposing influences of the enemy, have
borne much fruit, and the sight of the numer-
ous little branches which are putting forth and
spreading over the land has so enraged the ad-
versary, that He is going about like a "roaring
lion seeking whom he may devour;" and though
his agents are filled with jealousy, envy and
bitter hatred towards one another, they are
willing to unite their forces to see if they can
possibly put a stop to this increase, break up
the union, tear down and destroy the "master's
vineyard;" but they must learn that "The race
is not to the swift nor the battle to the strong."
	As J. M. Coyner has so generously offered
the privilege to some of our teachers of correct-
ing him if he writes aught that they know is
untrue, I, though not professing to be a teacher,
can detect the falsehoods, and understand the
spirit with which his articles, and many others
were written and published. Some of them
are the most vile, wicked and barefaced, which
have been resurrected to blast the characters of
the leaders and prominent men of this Church;
firing the public mind with tales of insurrec-
tion, murder and crimes which were never com-

[Column 2]
mitted. The Latter-day Saints have never
ustified the wicked deeds of ungodly men. It
is contrary to the spirit of our religion and
the constant teachings of Joseph Smith, Brig-
ham Young, John Taylor and the Apostles.
"Vengeance is mine and I will repay," saith the
Lord, is ever the teaching of the Elders.
	The author of an article headed "A Beauti-
ful Picture," drawn from what he learned con-
cerning a certain polygamist family in the
southern part of Utah, while he was stopping
(as he says) "long enough to dine and change
horses," is quite in keeping with other
statements which the Christian ministers
have gathered up and published, to prej-
udice the outside world against the favored
people of God. This story of an attorny is on
a par with some other extravagancies which
malicious hack-drivers have imposed upon
the credulity of tourists who make only flying
visits to Utah.
	They do it out of pure mischief, to see how
much the stranger can be made to swallow.
Many a one has gone away with the idea that
the whole block east of the Temple was owned
by President Brigham Young, and they were
told that his wives were numbered by the
chimneys in the houses; when their informers
well knew that most of them were the private
property of other men; including Kimball and
Whitney, who own nearly half of said block
two of the houses belonging to my husband.
They will afterwards amuse themselves by re-
lating their experience, laughing and chuck-
ling over the fun they have enjoyed at the ex-
pense of others. In connection with these
falsehoods they have published (though unin-
tentionally) some excellent truths, which, in-
stead of doing us harm, will do an immense
deal of good.
	The Lord has said that all things shall
work together for the good of those who fear
him and keep his commandments; and those
who are exposing "Mormonism" are only doing
what the Elders of this Church have been
laboring for many years to accomplish: send-
ing the Gospel truths where the presence of a
"Mormon" would not be allowed, opening the
eyes of the honest of all nations, and at the
same time showing themselves up in their
true light. One of the writers says, "The
nature of the organization of the Mormon
Church tends to hold the people together;
it is one of the best organized systems in the
world." True, nor can we separate our tem-
poral and spiritual affairs, for with all our
daily cares and vocations of life is mingled the
spirit of this one great and all important object
—the advancement and eternal progress and
exaltation of poor fallen man, and we know
that this is why the enemy of all righteous-
ness is so busily engaged in stirring up the
hearts of men who allow themselves to be
blinded by prejudice and priestcraft instead of
being led by the light and truth of the pure
Gospel of the humble Nazarene, who was also
accused of every crime imaginable and who
said, "Blessed are ye when all men revile you,"
etc. "I have chosen you out of the world,
therefore the world hates you, if you were of
the world the world, would love its own."
	Among what the writer says is the most bar-
barous of our doctrines, is the belief that God
possesses a body, parts and passions and that
we claim a right to revelation and prophecies;
to the healing of the sick, by the laying on of
hands and the guidance of the holy spirit, all
of which is contained in the Gospel of Christ.
He says: "It would be hard to find more self-
conceit and self-righteousness than among the
Mormon people." It would be impossible, I
think, to find more ignorance of the holy
scriptures, from which we derive our faith and
doctrines, than he betrays in every sentence
uttered against what is called "Mormonism."
Satan, with all his cunning, so betrays his folly

[Column 3]
and short sightedness in every move that his
agents have made to expose Mormonism, that
it is really pitiful.
	The spirit could not be perfect without the
body, else why was our Savior resurrected?
The Latter-day Saints believe in both the Old
and New Testament (though they try to prove
to the contrary) and we accept of all that is
good, no matter from what source it comes
Revelation is not confined to our leaders, but
it is the privilege of every person—yes, even "a
young 'Mormon' Elder," who cannot put to-
gether three sentences in a grammatical way—
may receive revelation and have a better under-
standing of religious truths generally, than
thousands who have had sixty or even a hun-
dred years of careful study without the assist-
ance of the Holy Spirit; for study would not re-
veal the scriptures. It is the privilege of every
Saint to know the will of God concerning them-
selves, and no one who lives up to the light of
the Gospel of Christ need to grope long in dark-
ness, or be dependent on the testimony of an-
other; the Lord has said, "Cursed is he who
maketh flesh his arm." "If any of you lack
wisdom, ask of God, who giveth to all freely
and upbraideth not." These words show plain-
ly that this is the privilege of every one high
or low, bond or free.
			To be Continued.


10	WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


MRS. SCOTT.

	DEAR FRIEND—We take the liberty of
calling you friend because you are willing to
accord us the privilege of speaking for our-
selves.
	A great many years ago Prest. John Taylor
edited a paper in Washington, D. C., called The
Mormon; at the head of said paper were the
words "It is Better to Represent Ourselves
than to be Misrepresented by Others." There
is a great deal in this statement, and we as a
people can appreciate it. You speak in your
last letter about the injustice of reward and
punishment; and of your heart being hard and
unbelieving. It is a sweet consolation, in the
hour of trial, to approach our Heavenly Father
knowing that He will hear and answer our
prayers; this is how we Lattter-day Saints pass
through this world of trial. In regard to re-
ward and punishment, let me ask one question.
Can our Heavenly Father ask us to pass
through more than He did his only begotten
Son? Can we possibly descend lower than He
did, born in a manger; suffered the pangs of
hunger and want; was scoffed at and even spit
upon; and at last crucified between two thieves?
Can any of us pass through more than this?
And if we would share his glory, is it not just
that we should taste of the bitter cup that He
drank to the dregs? Again, if we would be as
gold seven times purified. we must bear with
patience that which our Heavenly Father sees
is for our good.
	While I am writing I may tell you that I
am a plural wife and have been for some
twenty-six years; would I change my position
for that of an only wife? I would not. In the
period named I have attended a very high
school; I have made intimate acquaintance with
my God. He has taught me many valuable
lessons, some of them may have been bitter,
but they are priceless. As a people we enjoy
solid peace and satisfaction, which the world
can never give nor take away. We fear no
outside element, but trust in the living God
who we know is at the helm.
	We have another consolation, we know that
our husbands are true to their marriage vows;
we know that they and our children are pure,
and that virtue is dearer to them than life.
And this we prize more than all the glittering
gold of the world.
	Hoping to hear from you again,
		Most respectfully,		M. L. M.


﻿	WOMAN’S	EXPONENT.


THOUGHTS.
SUGGESTED BY LOOKING OVER THE CONTENTS OF TWO
	BOOKS OF POETRY, "AUTOBIOGRAPHY AND POEMS OF
	MRS. HANNAH CORNABY," AND "WILD FLOWERS OF
	DESERET," BY AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.

				Humbly, in a pensive mood,
				I said I'll seek the good,
		My life shall not be worthless, I shall aim
		To live that all may love and bless my name.
				A monument I'll build
		Within the hearts of men.  Each little child
		Shall say, "God bless his name and deeds;"
		I'll scatter sunshine, sow the seeds
		Of love, and truth and justice everywhere,
		I'll save the helpless ones from want and care.

				And never in my life
				Will I engage in strife:
		Unless it be to help the poor and weak;
		Then, word of sharp reproof I'll plainly speak,
				My wrongs I'll quick forgive,
		In peace with all mankin, content I'll live.
		Our Savior's life so good and pure
		Will imitate each day and hour.
		God's holy word shall ever be my guide,
		Should fortune smile or woeful ill betide.

				I shall not choose a text,
				And there on build a sect.
		My rule of faith--the Golden Rule shall be,
		"Unto all do, as they should do to me."
				At our dear Savior's birth
		The song the angels sang was, "Peace on earth,
		Good will to men."  My life shall be
		Responsive to the melody--
		That when my days are done, the glad refrain,
		At mention of my name, shall sound again.

				Life is worth the living,
				Good words worth the giving,
		If we but speak them for the joy they'll give,
		And not await an answer to receive,
				The little flower seeds
		Oft for months, o'er grown with poisonous weeds,
		Lie dead.  Then springing from the ground
		They bloom, and shed sweet fragrance 'round.
		One enjoys the planting--others the bloom,
		Tis joy to both--a blessing to each one.

				Plant not for self alone,
				Enjoy it as 'tis done,
		Shall be my daily rule.  Nor shall the weeds
		Of envy, lust or malice, choke the seeds
				I plant along life's way.
		But symphathetic tears, and love's bright ray
		Shall call them forth to brightly bloom,
		And fill all life with sweet perfume.
		I may be gone from life, but they will be
		A joy, an incense to my memory.			E.S.D.


TWO BOOKS.

   Across the plains there came to me the notes
of a Western song bird, and the perfume of
sweet flowers.  Wild flowers of Deseret.  The
song bird's notes had a plaintive sound at times
stirring my soul to pensive sadness.  Some-
times there was a note of jubilee, and at all
times the glad refrain said o'er and o'er again,
"My heart doth trust my Savior's love."  Mrs.
Cornaby's songs, so full of love, trust and a
woman's quick sympathy with all that is good
and beautiful, awakened all my best resolves
to be hopeful and trustful.  Her little book
should be on the parlor table of every Chris-
tian mother.
   The book,  "Wild flowers of Deseret,"
brought to my mind a childhood school song,
recalling all the pleasant remembrances of
happy school life.  "Flowers, wildwood flow-
ers, in a sheltered dell they grew."  The last
verse of the song was,
	Flowers, wildwood flowers,
		Do they not tell of heaven?
	Oh! man may garnish his garden bowers,
	But who hath tended these wildwood flowers?
	Is it not He who rules, who rules above
	Sending these tokens of his love?
	  'Tis God's own hand hath given
	All these wildwood flowers,
	Sweet wildwood flowers.


"Wild flowers of Deseret" is a sweet name
for so pretty a cluster of poetic blossoms, and
one feels while reading the little book that
God's own hand hath tended the sheltered dell
of Mrs Crocheron's mind and heart, the verses
are so fragrant with love, faith, hope and kind-
ness.
	'Tis God's own hand hath given these tokens
of his love. One need never feel lonely with
two such good companions in the house, "Mrs.
Cornaby's Poems," and "Wild Flowers of
Deseret."
					EMILY SCOTT.


[Column 1]
A PSALM OF PRAISE.

	I stand in Zion, and rejoice in the God of
my salvation! My home, my cherished home
is among the Saints of God; therefore do I re-
joice. I sit beneath the voice of the Prophets
of God, and the light of inspiration is reflected
from them even into my heart and brain; there-
fore do I rejoice. Holy men are around us
who bear the Priesthood of the Holy One, and
magnify the same; and therefore do I rejoice.
Holy women also are around and about us,
that minister to their sisters in holy places;
that go about doing good; whose lives are a
"labor of love," nursing the sick, comforting
the afflicted, strengthening the weak, and in
all things fulfilling the law of love. Therefore
does my heart rejoice, l am made glad, and am
comforted for the trials I have under gone.
In a far distant land was the Gospel brought
to me, by a handmaid of the Lord. My father
and my mother were near me, and a host of
loving and beloved ones! My home was the
nest of love! The Gospel did not find me des-
titute, or desolate, or alone, but it found me
rich; my cup full to overflowing with good
things, with sweet and loving spirits around me.
Yes, I had all I desired!—Then I heard the
Gospel of Jesus Christ, and I had enough of
the Spirit of God to know it was truth. Then
also came the knowledge that I must sacrifice
on the altar of my God much that was so dear,
to me.
	I halted, I had "great possessions," and then
came a voice saying, "whoso loveth father or
mother more than me is not worthy of me; or
son or daughter more than me and my Gospel
is not worthy of me; one who is not ready to
forsake all if need be for my sake cannot be my
disciple." 'Twas the Spirit of my God that en-
abled me to pass through the fiery trials of that
grand epoch of my life—'twas often the bitter-
ness of death, and the tenderest fibres of my
soul were stretched to their utmost tension; but
I passed the severe ordeals, and now rejoice
that I stand as one among the Saints of Latter
days.
	Here are my brethren and my sisters, the
friends of my soul—and the children whom
God has given to me. He hath given me a
home in Zion, food and raiment, peace of mind,
love and unity to be round and about me, and
his Spirit He hath not taken from me. There-
fore my spirit rejoices all the day long, and
often in the silent watches of the night have
I risen to thank and bless Him for his good-
ness to me. He hath upheld me in every trial,
in severe sickness, and through faith and the
administration of his servants, and the ordin-
ances of his church I have been healed.
	When poverty and privation have been
around me, He has inspired friends to help me,
and supplies have come. I gave up my friends
in my native land, but never for a moment have
I been without one. I gave up my brother
and my sister, that I so loved; but I have found
brothers and sisters fond and faithful, in the
gathering place of the Saints; and my richly
appreciated parents that were among the most
valued of my treasures—their places even have
been in a measure supplied. I have realized

[Column 2]
the words of our Savior, that whatsoever we
sacrifice for his sake and the Gospel shall be
restored to us a hundred fold, even in this life.
Shall I not then exultingly break forth? I
stand in Zion, and rejoice in the God of my
salvation! Here I "raise my Ebenezer," for
truly "thus far hath the Lord helped me."
Therefore I will sing unto him a song of praise,
and proclaim his goodness in the courts of Zion.
He hath dealt with me as a loving father; He
hath corrected me, but not in his anger; He
hath afflicted me to purify and sanctify me.
	He has taken some that were dear to me as
my own soul, but they were his before He gave
them to me, and He will reserve them for me
till we meet again. "He hath done all things
well," and my soul is satisfied. How shall I shew
forth my gratitude, how make known the
thanksgivings of which my heart is full? I
will listen to the instructions of my teacher—
I will be obedient to the counsels of God's ser-
vants— I feel as a child in school, and will try
to learn the lessons set before me; I will try to
school myself, teaching my spirit to be passive
where action is not available. I have never
eaten the bread of idleness, now I will be "a
working bee in the hive of Deseret;" industry
becomes the Saints of Latter-days. I will labor
with my hands, even as a menial; I am content
to do it, I will also examine my mind, and if
there is good in it I will lay it out for the
benefit of others. That, that I have found
good, by my own experience, I will lay before
others, that they may also turn the same to
good account. I will do all the good I can in
the station into which God has seen fit to call me
I will try to bear affliction patiently. When I
am in happiness Thou my God knowest how
grateful I will be, for happiness is heaven to
me; and it is an emanation of the Spirit of
God. Where it exists the principle of evil
can have no power, for the soul is basking in
a fulness and is satisfied. Then let us cultivate
this atmsophere of heaven; it will enlarge our
hearts, expand our minds, increase our intel-
ligence, multiply our usefulness and teach us,
above all other things, to praise our God. Let
us help others to be happy, for by our works
shall we be judged; by the "deeds done in the
body," will the verdict be given, and in this
way only have I any right to say, or any hope
in saying, I stand in Zion, and rejoice in the
God of my salvation.
					HANNAH T. KING.

Salt Lake City, June, 1882.


THE SPHERE OF WOMAN..

Dr. Napheys, in treating of the '“Physical
Life of 'Woman,” incidentally gives his opinion
of the “Sphere of Woman:”
	The mental faculties of man and woman
are unlike, but not unequal. Any argument- 
to the contrary, drawn from the somewhat less
weight of the brain of woman, is met by the 
fact that the most able men are often under-
sized, with small heads. The subordinate
place which woman occupies in most States. 
arises partly from the fact that the part which
she plays in reproduction prevents her from 
devoting her whole time and energies to the
acquisition of power, and partly from the fact..,.,
that those faculties in which she is superior to.
man have been obscured and oppressed by the
animal vigor and selfishness ot the male, As
civilization advances, the natural rights of
woman will be more and more conceded, until
the sexes become absolutely equal before the
law; and finally, her superiority in many re-
spects will be granted, and she will reap the
benefits of all the advantages it brings, without
desiring to encroach on those avocations for
which masculine energy and strength arc im
peratively needed.—Ex.
