SALT LAKE CITY.	-	JUNE 15, 1882.


[Column 1]
		DESERET HOSPITAL.
	Every man and woman who sees this title
will certainly feel a thrill of gladness that steps
have been taken to inaugurate such an institution
for the sick and afflicted. Long has this need
been felt, and efforts have several times been
made to obtain the desired result, yet in the past
they have proved ineffectual, but at this time 
all things seem propitious. It has been taken
hold of with a determination that should merit
success. The Relief Society organized in each 
ward often finds itself laboring under great
difficulties for lack of the facilities necessary in
case of accident and those emergencies of various
kinds incidental to human life that require
special attention.
	It is sincerely to be hoped that the people
evreywhere will appreciate the needs for a
hospital, and the benefits which will accrue to 
the sick and distressed, and will lend their
influence and donate liberally of the means
which is in their possession,for the establishment
and permanency of the hospital, that they may
receive the blessing of the Lord upon the sub-
stance over which they are made stewards.
This Hospital is under the direction of able
women, women of integrity and of faith, women
who have proved themselves capable of manag-
ing business affairs economically. Their names
are a guarantee that all will be done practically
with a view of the Hospital becoming self-sus-
taining to its utmost extent.
	Circulars have been published and are now
ready to distribute, throughout the country in
all the Stakes of Zion, setting forth the rules
and regulations, giving the names of officers and
including an appeal addressed to the Presidents of
Stakes, Bishops, Presidents and members of the
Relief Society, Superintendents and Presidents
of Y. L. and Y.  M. I. A.
	One of these circulars it is expected will as
soon as possible be put into the hands of the
persons named and it is earnestly desired they
will use their influence to aid in this benevolant
undertaking. And to all those who desire to
follow in the footsteps of the blessed Savior of
the world, this work will be assuredly, most ac-
ceptable. His time upon the earth was mostly
devoted to  healing the sick, the lame, the dumb,
the blind and those afflicted with all manner of
diseases. those who take upon themselves his
name should emulate his example; and it is well
known that He went about doing good.
	Officers of Deseret Hospital Association, Salt
Lake Cite--Pres. E. R. Snow Smith,: Vice-Pres.
Zina D. H. Young. Executive Board,M. Isabella
Horne, Marinda N. Hyde, Phebe Woodruff,
Bathsheba W. Smith, Jane S. Richards, Ellen
B. Ferguson M. D. Sec. Emmeline B. Wells,
Treas. Matilda M. Barratt, Chairman Visiting
Committee, Elizabeth Howard. Finance Com-
mittee--Mesdames Sarah M. Kimball, Priscilla
M. Staines, Priscilla Jennings, Annie G. Sharp,
Sarah Jane Cannon, Hannah T. King, Elizabeth

[Column 2]
Groosback, Ellen Dinwoodey, Elizabeth H.
Goddard, Sophia W. Taylor.  Resident Physi-
cian and Surgeon, Dr. E. B. Ferguson. Visiting
Board Dr. Seymour B. Young, Dr. W. F. Ander-
son.  Visiting Surgeon Eye and Ear, Dr.
Romaina B. Pratt. Clinical Registrar and Dis-
pensary Clerk, Mary E. VanSchoonhoven.
Mafron, Mary Ann McLean. The Stake
Presidents of the Relief Society are members of
the Honorary Committee, each one to represent
the interests of the Hospital in her respective
locality.
	Any lady member of the Church of Jesus
Christ of Latter-day Saints, in good standing,
may become a member of this Association by
the payment of one dollar annually; such name
and amount to be sent to any member of the
Committee,to be voted upon at the next month-
ly meeting. The board of managers expect
to establish in connection with the Hospital,
school for the purpose of educating midwives
and training nurses for the sick, in order that
the people in all the Stakes of Zion may be
better supplied with efficient help in the hour of
extreme need.


﻿CONVENTION.
				-----
	The Constitutional Convention met pursuant
to adjournment on Tuesday June 6 at 2 o'clock
P. M., in the City Hall, Hon. Joseph F. Smith
in the chair. Among other business transacted
the report of the vote on the Constitution was
read accepted and ordered to be placed upon the
minutes. Votes cast in its favor were 27,814 and
those against it numbered only 498, A commit-
tee was appointed to nominate delegates to go to
Congress present  the Constitution and ask for
its ratification.
	The Committee were Messrs. D. H. Wells, A.
O. Smoot, L. J. Nuttal, G. W. Thatcher, F. A.
Hammond, E. G. Woolley, Mrs. E. B. Wells.
There was some discussion as to whether
the delegates should go now and lay the
matter before the present session of Congress or
wait until the next session. The report of the
Committee on Memorial was submitted by
the Chairman, Gen. D. H. Wells.  Memorial read
and laid on the table.
	The following morning at 10, the Committee
convened, when the Memorial was taken up
read again discussed and amended, and was
adopted by unanimous vote.  The President
announced that 5000 copies of the Constitution
had been printed with the names of the dele-
gates attached, and how they had been distri-
buted.  The committee on nominations reported
the following names as delegates: Wm. H.
Hooper, John T. Caine, James Sharp, W. W.
Riter, Salt Lake Co., Franklin S. Richards,
David H. Peery, Weber Co., W. D. Johnson Jr.
Washington Co. Report adopted.  Some ex-
pression was given by the members in regard to
the time when the delegates should go to Wash-
ington, and the prevailing sentiment seemed
to be in favor of presenting the Constitution
to the present session of Congress. Minutes were
read amended and approved, and the Convention
adjourned until October 9, 1882, at 2 p. m.,
unless sooner called by the President.


[Column 1]
﻿MEMORIAL.

To the Honorable the Senate and House of Represen-
tatives of the United States, in Congress assembled:

	Gentlemen—The citizens of the United States
in the Territory of Utah, through their dele-
gates in Convention, respectfully present this
Memorial, asking for their admission into the
Federal Union as a free and sovereign State, on
the same footing as the other States, under the

[Column 2]
name and title of the State of Utah; and here-
with submit the Constitution which they have
adopted.
	When the people who opened this vast region
to civilization, and rendered possible its settle-
ment by the advanced races, first planted their
feet upon the virgin soil of these valleys, it was
part of the domain of the Republic of Mexico,
five hundred of their stalwart men were in the
field aiding in the war with that Power, and
they occupied this part of the country as loyal
citizens of the United States, and unfurling its
flag to the mountain breeze, proceeded to
organize a provisional government, preparatory
to becoming a part of the Federal Union. When
by the treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo, this portion
of Mexico was ceded to the United States, they
sought at once, by proper means, to secure the
rights and privileges of statehood. California,
the settlement of which by citizens of the United
States, was largely due to the people of this
Territory, made application at the same time as
they for admission into the Union, and was
successful. To Utah was granted a territorial
form of government. For thirty-two years,
the citizens of this Territory have been kept out
of the Union, and under the disadvantages of a
system which in many respects, is foreign to the
principles of democratic republicanism. They
now, for the fourth time, approach your Honor-
able Body, with their respectful petition for that
republican form of government which Congress
is required by the Constitution to guarantee to
every State in the Union.
	And they submit that Utah has outgrown the
conditions, if they ever existed, which justified
the establishment of that imperfect political
system which has been so long continued. Her
population numbers at least 150,000. Her agri-
cultural, stockraising, manufacturing, mineral
and other resources and industries, are of such
magnitude that she is abundantly able to bear
the burdens and meet the expenses involved in
independent statehood. Her cities towns and
villages exhibit all the marks of thrift, prosperity
and order that indicate capacities for self-govern-
ment. Unaided by any extraneous assistance,
schools for the education of her children flourish
in every settlement within her borders. The
local laws protect life and property with more
than common safety, and extend all possible
liberty to persons of every party, creed and race,
without distinction. Railroads and telegraph
lines connect her people with each other and
with all parts of this great nation, and they are
familiar with the common interests and public
questions which blend the various States in pol-
itical unity. There is nothing to prevent Utah
from taking an active part in the Federal com-
pact but those barriers of prejudice which have
been built up chiefly through misrepresentation
aided by unfamiliarity with the facts.
	We further submit that the exercise of absolute
and unlimited power over the great body of
citizens inhabiting this Territory, which is
claimed by Congress as a right, is anti-republi-
can in theory, and most oppressive in practice.
We have no voice in the election of the national
legislators who, without understanding our con-
dition, occasionally make laws to govern us, or
of those authorities who appoint officials to con-
duct our affairs. The Governor, placed over us
without our consent, has the power to render
void all the acts of the legislature elected by the
people, by simply withholding his signature
thereto. This absolute veto power is added to
the supreme authority of Congress to abolish or
make null all or any of our local laws. The
essential principles of American republicanism,
as enumerated in the Declaration of Indepen-
dence, are thus grossly violated, and the people
of Utah are kept in a condition of vassalage alike


unworthy of a free government, irksome to those
who are compelled to endure it, and subversive
of the inallenable rights of man.
	We direct the attention of your honorable body
to the pledged word of the nation, in the treaty
before mentioned, agreeing that the people resid-
ing in this region at the time of its ratification, 
should be "incorporated into the Union of the 
United States, and be admitted at the proper
time to the enjoyment of all the rights of citizens
of the United States according to the principles
of the Constitution, and meanwhile should be
protected in the free enjoyment of their liberty
and property, and secured in the free exercise of
their religion without restriction." Abstaining
from any inquiry as to whether  the proper time
has not now arrived when her people should be
admitted to the full rights and privileges of
citizens of the United States, the same conditions
being required as of other communities seeking
the status of independent statehood?
	We present with confidence the Constitution
framed by this Convention, and ratified by the
people of this Territory at the polls, the election
being held May 22d, 1882, when 27,814 ballots
were cast in its favor, and but 498 against it-
We think it will be conceded that it provides
for the State of Utah a republican form of govern-
ment; and we urge that this being guaranteed
beyond dispute, the constitutional requirement
is complied with, and nothing stands lawfully
in the way forbidding Congress to authorize the
admission of Utah into the Union.
	In conclusion, we respectfully suggest that by
granting what we believe to be our rights under
the Constitution and the treaty made with
Mexico, benefits will accrue not only to the
new State but to the nation at large, in the set-
tlement of questions that have frequently pro-
duced great and unprofitable agitation: in the ad-
dition of one more vigorous and promising com-
monwealth to the Federal Union; and in the
security which will be the consequence to every
interest in this important section of the country 
which is calculated to add to the wealth, power
and prosperity of the glorious Republic in which
we desire to become incorporated.
	And as in duty bound your memorialists will 
ever pray. 
	Adopted in Convention at Salt Lake City Utah
Territory, on the seventh day of June A D 1882, 
by unanimous vote, and ordered to be signed by
the President and Secretary.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 13


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	THE Quarterly Conference of the Relief So-
ciety, Y. L. M. I. A. and Primary Associations
of this Stake of Zion, will be held in this city,
Friday and Saturday June 16 and 17. A repre-
sentation from each branch of these organiza-
tions is specially required, as there will be no
written reports.

	WOMEN'S CONFERENCES: Sisters E. R. S.
Smith, Zina D. H. Young, E. S. Taylor and Dr.
E. B. Ferguson, attended the Ladies Conference
of Weber held in Ogden, on the 8, 9, and 10th of
this month Sisters E. R. S. Smith, Z. D. H.
Young, E. S. Taylor and Mattie Horne, Box
Elder Stake held in Brigham City 13, 14, and
15, and Sisters Phebe Woodruff and Presendia
Kimball, Tooele Stake held at Grantsville 10th,
and 11th of June.

	THE OLD Folks Excursion has been arranged
to take place on the 28 and 29 of June. It is
anticipated that this occasion will be something
extraordinary. The Committee who have
undertaken the arrangements, have shown them-
selves equal to any enterprise of this kind by


their efficiency in times past. It will be some-
thing new for many of the old people, to come
to Salt Lake City from all the surrounding
counties.

	DR. R. B. Pratt who has been in New York
ever since last October, studying in her profession
and as a specialist in the diseases of the eye, ear,
nose and throat, returned home on the 4th, inst.
She has been hard at work during her absence, in
the Infirmary under the best Professors in these
departments, and as she is well and fovorably
known in Utah as a physican, her many friends
and patrons will doubtless be glad to hear that
she has opened an office again, in the city,
next to J. M. Benedict's office.

	MMS. Elvira S. Barney who went East three
years ago to study Medicine and Surgery, has
completed her course and returned home, pre-
pared to enter upon the practice of her profession.
Mrs. Barney is widely known as an active and
able worker among the people of Utah, having
traveled considerably from place to place, in the
interests of the organizations, and will without
doubt soon build up a reputation as a practising
physician. Dr. Barney has opened no office as
yet, but may be found by those wishing her
service[-] at the residence of James P. Freeze, 11th
Ward.
	We have received a copy of "Questions and
Answers on the Life and Mission of the Prophet
Joseph Smith," published by the Deseret Sun-
day School Union. The book has been carefully
prepared for the use of the Sunday Schools. It
will place some important facts and data before
the children in a way that will make a lasting
impression upon their minds. The price is
only 10cts. and we hope to see it widely
circulated. Every child should have one of
the books. For sale at the Juvenile Instructor
Offices.


THE WORD OF WISDOM.

	I firmly believe that but very few of the Saints
realize the great importance of this word of coun-
sel, that was revealed through the Prophet Jos-
eph, for the benefit of the Saints; and is adapted
to the capacity of the weakest of those who may
or can be called Saints. So says the revelation.
It further says, "In consequences of evils that do
and will exist, in the minds of designing men
in the last days, this commandment is given."
Now what these designs are, we are left to con-
jecture, but it is certainly something that would
bring trouble and sorrow upon the Saints, or
the Lord would not have considered it neces-
sary, to give a special revelation concerning it.
It is very probable that some deadly ingredient,
will be worked insidiously into the tea and
coffee also tobacco and whiskey, that will work
its deadly venom into the system, thereby weak-
ening its powers, that when disease, and that
overwhelming scourge that has been predicted,
should come forth upon the earth in this genera-
tion, it will be impossible to fight against it.
	Again it says, "If you will give heed to these
sayings and neglect them not, I the Lord will
cause the destroying angel to pass by you, as it
did the children of Israel, and it shall not slay
you." Now what a glorious promise is this, by
merely exercising the powers of self denial,
denying ourselves of what is merely a gratifica-
tion, of a depraved appetite, (which is not neces-
sary to be gratified, either for food or drink.)
What great and inestimable blessings are prom-
ised us. How can we be so foolish and short-
sighted as to persist in a practice that will bring
destruction and sorrow upon ourselves and o[--]
spring, when the way to escape these evils is so
plainly pointed out. Is is not suicidal in its
effects, to continue in this practice? And how


can we exercise faith in God, when our children
are laid low by sickness, if we realize that we
have been trampling under our feet, and con-
sidering as worthless those very laws, and rules,
that if we had obeyed, would have preserved
them from the power of the destroyer. If the
Lord had not decreed, and foreseen, that great
destruction was coming upon the inhabitants of
the earth, why did He give this warning, and
at the same time provide a way for his Saints to
escape these dire calamities? The Lord would
not trifle with his people, although a great many
are inclined to trifle with his words, but his
words are too sacred to be trifled with. Those
things that have been predicted, shall as surely
come to pass, as the world stands. And may
the Lord give each and every one of his Saints
a portion of his Holy Spirit that we may realize
the necessity of obeying the "Word of Wisdom,"
in its fullest sense, for I do not think that merely
the avoiding the use of tea and coffee, and other
stimulants, is all that is comprised or compre-
hended, in the "Word of Wisdom."
	We should ever pray for wisdom to direct us
in all the affairs of life. In governing our child-
ren and training them in an honest and upright
course, and instil in their minds principles of
truth and virtue, that they may have an abhor-
ence of everything which leads to evil, and love
that which is pure and good; that they may grow
up to be an honor to their parents, and to the
Church and kingdom of God. Then when sick-
ness and disease visits our households we can
come before the Lord in faith and claim the
promises He has made to us, if we obey his laws
for his word failsnot.
	And that the Lord will help us so to live, is
the earnest wish and prayer of,
						SARAH M. POMEROY.


RESOLUTIONS.

	The following resolutions of respect to our
young and much beloved Sister, Julia V. Pack,
who departed this life May 22d, 1882; in the
Seventeenth year of her age, were unanimously
adopted by the Y. L. M. I. Association of Kamas,
Summit Stake of Zion.
	Whereas God in his divine wisdom has seen
proper to call from our midst, to a higher sphere,
one of our members, who was greatly beloved
and respected by us all, for her kind and gentle
spirit, her pure and virtuous life, her faithful
labors in our Society and many good qualities of
a saint which she possessed, and for the love
which we have for her, and the respect for her
parents, brothers, sisters and friends,
	Therefore, be it resolved that we as members
of the Y. L. M. I. A., do deeply mourn the loss of
our beloved Sister and co-laborer in this Society,
and that we condole with her parents and rela-
tives in their great loss intermingled with the
joy and hope of a glorious resurrection of the
departed,
	And be it resolved, that we present a copy
of these resolutions to her parents, and that a
copy be sent to the WOMAN'S EXPONENT for
publication, and that they be spread upon our
record book, that we may be reminded of and
emulate her virtues and inestimable good
qualities in our lives, that we may meet her in
heaven.

Dearest sister mild and lovely,
	May thy virtues we possess;
Farewell, sister may we meet you
	And be numbered with the blest.
		MARGARET A. LAMBERT, Prest.
		URSULA V. SMITH, Sec'y.

	We the members of the Kamas Primary As-
sociation, do most heartily endorse the above
resolutions of respect to our beloved sister and
Counselor in our Association.
				ELIZA J. ATWOOD, Prest.
				PAMILLA A. HARDER, Sec'y.


14 WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. AND PRIMARY
REPORTS.

MOAN COPY, ARIZONA.

	The Relief Society of this place was organ-
ized May 11th, 1882, under the direction of
President Wilford Woodruff, with sixteen
members, of whom five only remained at the
end of a few months. Consequently we have
accomplished but little. We hope to do better
in future, as other settlers have now moved in;
we were reorganized April 6th, of the present
year, by Bishop A. L. Farnsworth and Coun-
selors; with the following officers: Harriet M.
Gardner, President; Elisabeth D. Gardner,
Caroline E. Walker, Counselors; Mary A.
Farnsworth, Secy.; Annie E. Farnsworth,
Treasurer. We have twenty-one members,
twelve of whom live near enough together to
attend meetings regularly. We intend meeting
once a month at present; working meetings as
often as necessary. We are few in number,
without either the affluent or the absolutely
needy in our midst. Our situation is isolated
in the extreme; the country, for many miles on
either side of us, is an irreclaimable desert,
with this small oasis in the midst, where the
people live by the blessings of the Lord, and
their own untiring industry. We are striving
to do all the good we can among the Lamanites
and fill our mission acceptably before the Lord.
We raise plenty of good corn here, but no
small grain as yet. The climate is rather too
warm for vegetables to do well. But the
people, in general, enjoy excellent health, for
which we are thankful. We have no regular
mail here, and the nearest post office is seventy-
five miles distant, at Lee's Ferry on the Big
Colorado. We have waited sometime for a
favorable change in our mail affairs, and are
tired of living without our invaluable Expon-
ent. We will now subscribe for two copies
for our Society. Hoping ere long to chronicle
something more creditable to ourselves as a
Relief Society, we remain your sisters in the
Gospel of peace.
				HARRIET M. GARDNER,
					MARY A. FARNSWORTH.

RICHMOND.

	Minutes of the third Anniversary of the
Primary Association of Richmond, held June
1st, 1882. President Mary Allsop presiding.
Singing, "Gather them into the Fold." Prayer
by Bishop Wm. L. Skidmore. Singing, "We
are the children of the Saints." The pro-
gramme for the afternoon was as follows:
	Isabella Thompson, a recitation, entitled
"Good Morning." Select reading, entitled
"How to make Sunshine," by Reuben Traveller.
Mary Thompson sang a song, entitled "Sweet
hour of Prayer," followed by a comic recitation
by Amos Merrill. Abba Burnham recited
"Dedication to Brigham Young." Anna Stod-
dard sang, "Joseph Smith's first prayer." Jo-
seph Webb recited, "How to succeed." Lizzie
Allsop recited, "Ourselves and our enemies."
Alice Brower recited, "Children do you love
each other!" Marinda Monson sang, "Our
Baby." Recitation by Lorin Merrill, entitled
"Grandma always did." Maggie Thopson
read an original essay. on "Brigham Young."
Hattie Skidmore recited, "Helping Paper and
Mamma." Hyrum Monson sang, "The Mormon
boy." Sina Funk recited, "I love to attend
the Primary." Lewis Merrill recited, "Louie's
Secret." Jane Hyer sang, "O, Birdie, I am
tired now." Emma Nibley recited, "To Brig-
ham Young." Charles Skidmore recited, "John
O'Nory." Sina Beck sang, "O, Boatman, row
me o'er the stream." Nellie Bullen recited,
"Jesus blessing little children." Nettie Lewis
sang, "You are false, but I'll forgive you."
Ella Shapard recited, "I'll do as Mother did."
Julia Olsen sang, "Tis meeting day." Millie

[Column 2]
Traveller recited "I'll be a little Mormon."
Sister Hannah Brower sang a song, entitled
"Lines to Brigham Young;" composed by her-
self. President Mary Allsop read an original
essay, entitled "A play with the children."
	Sister Jane E. Molen, President of the
Primary Associations of Cache Valley, gave
some very good advice. Her Counselor, Sister
Adeline Barber, also gave some good in-
structions. Bishop Wm. L. Skidmore, C. Hyer
and A. C. Brower each made a few remarks.
Sister Myra Merrill, President of the Relief
Society, and her Counselors met with us. A
good spirit prevailed.
	Singing, "In our lovely Deseret." Benedic-
tion by the children in concert.
	Annual report, beginning June 1st, 1881, and
ending June 1st, 1882: Number of names en-
rolled, one hundred and forty-seven. Average
attendance, seventy-five. Number of meetings
held, thirty-six. Financial report: Amount re-
maining in treasury from previous year, $5.20.
Received during the year, from the members,
$6.48. Total, $11.68. Disbursed, $4.45.
Amount in treasury, $7.23.
		MARY A. WEBB, Secretary.
		ELIZABETH MERRILL, Asst. Secretary.

SMITHFIELD.

	The yearly meeting of the Primary Associ-
ation of Smithfield, Cache Co. was held on
April 25th, 1882. A number of the Relief
Society sisters were present. During the meet-
ing a great many encouraging and instructive
remarks were given. Sister Adeline Barber,
in behalf of the mothers, presented Pres. Mar-
tha Williams with a handsome hat and cape;
also read a memorial of respect, composed by
the mothers for the Association, as a token of
respect and love toward her for her labors, as
she had been faithful in teaching their children
the ways of truth and righteousness. Prest. M.
Williams, with a few appropriate words,
thanked all for the kindness they had shown
her.
	On April 15th Sister Molen, Bishop Farrell
and Sister Barber being present, the Associ-
ation was reorganized, with the following
officers: Martha Williams, President; Lillian-
Jones and Emma Farrell, Counselors; Emma
Nelson, Secretary; Ghena Folsom, Assistant
Sec.; Hannah Christensen, Treasurer; Ida F.
Harper, her assistant.
	Sisters Williams, Molen and Barber, also Bp.
Farrell spoke encouragingly to the children.
	The Primary Association of Smithfield is
doing well, and great improvements have been
made since its first organization. The children
have held three concerts during this year; also
paid $12.00 to the emigration fund and have
wheat and beans stored away for a famine.
The Association proves to be a blessing to this
ward. A book of Questions and Answers, by
Sister Snow Smith, proves to be a great help
to the President.
					EMMA NELSON.
							Secretary.


[Column 1]
UNION.

	What a glorious and eternal principle is
union! How much can be accomplished where
this exists, whether it is in a family, a church
or a nation! If existing in a nation, that na-
tion must, of necessity, become invincible; for
what foe could stand against a people of one
mind and with concerted action? surely that
kingdom, or that republic, would attain to
honor and glory above all other nations or
countries. When this spirit of union pervades
a family, how lovely it makes that family ap-
pear! No matter how rudely the cold winds
of adversity may blow, or what the outside in-
fluences may be, that surround them, in that
home there is peace; and in that family circle

[Column 2]
is a haven of rest. But how much of this
glorious principle of union is there upon
the earth at the present day? Do we find it
existing in the assemblies of the nations; in
the family circle, or in the churches? No; for
the seeds of desolation are in their midst;
and the nations are sick; and old dynasties
are tottering to their foundation; dissension,
disunion and strife prevail to an awful extent,
and confidence seems almost a thing of the
past, and there is a fearful looking for the
things that are coming upon the earth. Signs
in the heavens above, and in the earth below;
surely no thinking mind can doubt that we are
living in the latter days.
	But what are the Christian ministers doing?
Who profess to be followers of the meek and
lowly Jesus; of Him who had not where to lay
his head, and who said, if ye are my disciples,
love one another. Who wept over Jeruselem
because he would have gathered its inhabitants
and they would not. Are these same Chris-
tian ministers faithfully warning the people?
Are they using their influence to bring about
a better state of things? Are they seeking for
that which was lost, and striving to bind up
that which was broken? Alas! No; too many
of them use their influence to fan the flames of
dissent and strife, and even condescend to fab-
ricate falsehood in order to bring persecution
upon an innocent people, whose greatest fault
is that they are too united. Whatever may be
made the excuse for the crusade against the
Latter-day Saints, this is the real secret. It
is not in Utah that Rev. Mr. Newman should
look for children born without homes or paren-
tal care. No, rather let him seek them where
they will be found, in and around the place
honored by his most profound teachings;
where he advocates the bayonet and the knife
to be used against an innocent people, and
where he dares to pray to the God of mercy
that the glorious valleys that the people of
Utah have made beautiful, and the fields of
waving grain, which they have planted, should
be wrested from them and be given to their
oppressors. Let him search where they will be
found in the sewers and cisterns of our great
cities in the east; where civilization reigns trium-
phant. Let him petition Congress for special
legislation to abate the social evil; and its mem-
bers will find abundant use for all the time they
can spare, and the philanthropy they are capable
of exercising. Let him that is without sin
cast the first stone, and Utah will be left face
to solve her own problems, the parents to raise
their own children; and the foundling hos-
pital and the workhouse will be unnecessary
and unknown in their midst. Surely the men
who profess to be ministers of the Gospel of
peace must have mistaken their vocation, when
they sow the seeds of dissension and strife; and
instead of trying to convince those who see
differently, and whom they may imagine to be
in error, by the divine spirit of love, stir up
instead, a spirit of hate and war. Yes, they
must have mistaken their vocation and war
should have been their profession. "Ichabod
the glory is departed!" might be written on the
very walls of the places where they preside;
for after all the fuss that has been made about
the sad fate of the women and children of
Utah, they are beginning to admit that that
is not the true reason they seek to bring
trouble upon us. It is our union that must be
destroyed, and the question settled forever; just
as though it were a sin for a people to be united,
when the spirit of true religion is unity, peace
and love! And the great fathers and foun-
ders of our own country advocated it above
all other principles; they well knew the ad-
vantages to be derived from its practice, and
could foresee the evils that would be the result
of disunion. Then let us, as Latter-day Saints,
not suffer our own union to be broken up; and-
if trouble and sorrow invade our ranks, let it


have the effect of making us more united. Let
us not return evil for evil, but rather, by our
good works, prove that we seek to honor our
Father in heaven. Then the question will in-
deed be settled speedily, and in a different
way than that which our defamers anticipate.
Oh! what a vision opens to my mind, when
contemplating the blessings and glory that
awaits a united people; but my pen fails me,
and I veil my eyes in wonder, and view, as in
a mist, the rest that remains for the people of
God.
	When we are enabled, through faith, to
pierce the dark clouds that obscure the pres- 
ent, and fix our eyes on the glorious promises
given of the future destiny of the people who
are united in keeping the commandments of
God, the trials and labors of the present are
but light, notwithstanding they sometimes
seem greater than we can bear. "For at
evening time it shall be light. And they shall
all see eye to eye, when the Lord shall bring
again Zion."
					PHOEBE C. YOUNG.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 15


[Column 1]
THE TRUE CHURCH.

	Why are all the churches in Christendom
offended with us? Is it because a young man
only fourteen years of age who, being anxious
to know which church possessed the greater
portion of the Spirit of the Lord, and the true
principles of religion, asked in faith believing
he could know for himself? believing also the
word of the Apostle James, chap. iv, ver. 5:
"He that lacketh wisdom let him ask of God!"
Accordingly received an answer, to his astonish-
ment, saying there were none on the earth or-
ganized as Jesus left it. None according to
this pattern. But the time had come when
there must be such a church and such a people.
This being the fact, I would ask who is to
blame?  Is any one to blame? We were all
doing the best we could before this message ar-
rived. And what now can be done only to in-
vestigate the truth and see if it will better com-
pare with Christ’s teachings when he was here
upon the earth. Did not the Savior come at
a certain period of time, to accomplish a work
which was ordained to be from before the
foundation of the world, to rescue us from the
fall and bring to pass the resurrection? Yet
his brethren, the Jews, rejected him, as St.
Paul says, blindness in part happened to Israel
until the fulness of the Gentiles has come in.
This time having arrived, is it strange that
this young man should be led to make so fer-
vent a prayer to God to know the right way. 
Is it not probable he was fore-ordained to do
this great work, this strange work which we
that have embraced it once looked upon as 
false? I well remember exclaiming false pro-
phet, when I first heard of the work; because
the Savior said there should be such in the
last days. But how unwise, to judge a people
unheard.
	Well what did I hear? It was this: that the
Lord, knowing our capacities, gave us the writ-
ten word, or Bible, just as he meant it, and
said his word was so plain that the wayfaring
man, though a fool, need not err therein.
This looked reasonable and fatherly. Why
should He do otherwise? The "Mormon" Elder
then took the bible in its plainness, and how in-
teresting. It indeed seemed like a well of
water springing up into everlasting life. Be-
cause he taught that Christ’s sermon on the
Mount must be observed to the very letter.
Nothing must be neglected, if we would be
prepared for the coming of the Savior. Was
there danger in this kind of teaching? No; far
more on the other side of the question, with the
idea that you have only to come to Jesus and
you are saved. Most assuredly. But how
must we come? The Savior says to observe all
things whatsoever I have commanded them

[Column 2]
And Oh! the plan of redemption with the
different changes that earth must pass through
before it can be the abode of its Creator! The
study is great and glorious, even as shown to
Daniel in vision down to the end of time. Also
to John, the Revelator, the same vision, only
by different figures, when the word came that
time shall be no longer! How I wish the
clergy of the different sects could see and un-
derstand this great and glorious work. Then
their minds would be filled with that light and
intelligence which would take all envy and
hatred and malice from their bosoms. Light and
joy and peace would take possession and fill the
place of the former; and they would find the
words of the Savior verified, when he told the
woman of Samaria saying, the water He would
give, would be a well of water springing up
unto everlasting life. With this water of life
or this light and intelligence, what great at-
tainments are made in arts and sciences, by
those who are not full of gall and bitterness;
those who give their attention to the principles
of study, and advancement for the benefit of
mankind. But should we become so proud
of our attainments that we cannot look beyond
our own sphere of action? Should we not re-
member that we are, although different, yet
members of one body or, in other words, all
children of one Father? Is it wise to be proud?
Rather let us remember the Lord resists the
proud, but gives grace to the humble. But again
I ask, why do the clergy rage against the truth?
I will answer—all do not. As soon as they
learn its simple and plain facts, many have
thrown away prejudice and especially if they
were devoted to the teaching of Christ. Many,
very many have done this. Even President
Taylor, who with another divine was praying
to understand some passage of scripture when
Elder Parley Pratt came in with the message
of salvation. Read also Spencer’s letters, you
will see true faith in God. The author was a
Baptist minister, and had a large congregation,
and a good salary. He gave up all for the truth,
when he heard it, and he never repented of it,
but rejoiced in it to the day of his death.
	Can it be a reality that the whole of Chris-
tendom are determined to stamp us out of
existence? Dare they, in the pride of their
hearts, attempt this? Have they no fear of
God before their eyes? I think it would be
well for them to learn of our faith and doc-
trine before they begin, lest our exit should
be to our advantage, and their efforts to their
sorrow. The measure they mete to others will
most assuredly be meted out to them again.
The word of the Lord has spoken it through
the Savior of the world.
						M. E. KIMBALL.


[Column 1]
THE MODERN HERO.

	Reading Carlyle's "Hero as Divinity," start-
ed the train of thought about the modern hero.
All nations, all people have a portion of di-
vinity within them. Children of the Heaven-
ly Father; how can it be otherwise? No matter
how dense the darkness that enshrouds them,
they seek for something to worship, to look up
to, to aid them to ward off evil, and to bring
them good. A higher power than theirs is
sought blindly, but devoutly. The traditions
of the ancient fathers came down through cen-
turies, mingled with obscurity; and that prob-
ably constituted the Gods of the ancient Norse-
men. The evil one was abroad, falsehood was
mingled with truth. Anything to lead the
minds of the children of men from the truth;
anything to satisfy the inherent longing for a
higher life, save the eternal truths of heaven.
The Priesthood was driven from the earth.
Idolatry and false forms of religion held sway.
Darkness swept over the earth. Popery and
and Paganism rose, advanced and partially de-

[Column 2]
clined. From Popery sprang her many
daughters, the Protestant Churches.
	There was no Divine Hero upon the earth.
None sent from the heavens to proclaim the
everlasting truth. No ambassador from on
high, for heretofore every ambassador, even to
the Son of God, had been destroyed, translated
or driven to the uttermost parts of the earth.
	The time had come for a new era. Blest,
ordained and set apart for his high mission,
Joseph was sent unto the earth. As a babe he
began his earthly life; angels watched over
their precious charge. While still a boy the
Lord and his Son, Jesus Christ, appeared to
him and unfolded the truth from heaven.
"Hear ye him" said the Father, and the Son
proclaimed the truth. Then beamed light up-
on the darkness of the world—light that was
destined to dispel it—to revolutionize the
theories of man-made religion; to bring life
again, eternal life, to the hungry children of
the Father, dwelling in shadows and fanciful
forms.
	This is a hero to be proud of! Who with-
stood persecution; who manfully maintained the
right against the wrong; who alone, in his
youth, unaided by mortal man, maintained his
integrity. The hosts of heaven watched, aye,
and guarded him too. Sorely was he tested,
but he shone forth as pure gold; he began a
new era of light and life, to mankind, the
brethren and the sisters, children of the same
Father he had lived with before he came to
this world, before he descended from the courts
of heaven.
	This hero came, Joseph was his name. His
life testified of his heroism; a struggle continu-
ally with the powers of darkness, that tried to
overwhelm him but could not. Through trial
and persecution Joseph led the chosen people
gathered from many nations. What is life to
him but sorrow and gladness. Sorrow when
wickedness triumphed, and gladness for the
power given in the last days over the evil one.
	After a life of self-denial, of good wishes, of
miracles, of hope, of troubles innumerable from
the hands of wicked men; of privations, of
imprisonment, falsly accused, this great hero,
this wonderful man, this son of the Father was
martyred; laid down his life, and sealed his
testimony with his blood, which has never
been effaced from the prison floor. It is a
strong testimony of the innocence of the mar-
tyred modern heroes, Joseph Smith and his
brother Hyrum.
					EMILY B. SPENCER.

INTELLIGENCE.

	In a revelation to Joseph Smith in the Doc-
trine and Covenants we find the following: "Seek
ye earnestly the best gifts, always remembering
for what they are given; for, verily I say unto
you, they are given for the benefit of those who
love me, and keep my commandments;" then
follows several paragraphs explaining what
those gifts are. And, "He that receiveth of
God, let him account it of God, and let him
rejoice that he is accounted of God worthy to
receive;" and we are told to "live by every
word that proceedeth forth from the mouth of
God," and to "search these commandments for
they are true and faithful."
	"The glory of God is intelligence, or, in
other words, light and truth." Doc. and Cov.
page 246, First European Edition. "And if
your eye be single to my glory, your whole
bodies shall be filled with light, and there
shall be no darkness in you, and that body
which is filled with light comprehendeth all
things." page 100; again he says "cast
away your idle thoughts and your excess of
laughter far from you." A description of
Jesus Christ says he was never known to laugh.
And it is certain that the more we think idle


[Column 1]
thoughts the less light and truth there is in us,
this certainly reflects much discredit on writers
and readers of fictitious literature; who give so
much of their time and thoughts to nonsense.
	Joseph Smith says: that whatever principles
of intelligence we attain unto in this life, it
will rise with us in the resurrection. And if a
person gains more knowledge and intelligence in
this life, through his diligence and obedience,
than another, he will have so much the advan-
tage in the world to come.
	God further says, "Seek not for riches but for
wisdom, and behold the mysteries of God shall
be unfolded unto you and then shall you be
made rich. Behold he that hath eternal life is
rich." Riches here evidently mean supera-
bundance or wealth, not necessary to comfort
or happiness; for in other commandments God
signifies his will that we should have neces-
saries, and something to spare, for our tithing
was to be of our "surplus properties," and it is
not right that any man should be in bondage
one to another. Doc. and Cov. It is wisdom
from the foregoing that our exaltation behind
the vail (when preceded by a pure life here) is
in accordance with our intelligence, hence those
who die ignorant, though equal in purity, will
enjoy a far less degree of happiness there; for
as intelligence is the glory of God it follows
that the more we possess the more our glory will
assimilate to his, and we cannot any way take
with us earthly riches, hence our knowledge
and wisdom is all we can lay claim to from
this side, so it is all our wealth when we reach
that. Though we have no contempt for money,
for a portion is absolutely necessary to the
acquirement of knowledge, of which a good
share is obtained by reading the "best books;"
"seek learning even by study and also by faith,"
Doc. and Cov., and "you shall live by every
word that proceedeth forth from the mouth of
God." Doc. and Cov. page 85.
	The highest ambition of every saint is to be-
come like Jesus Christ, as we are to be joint
heirs with him.
	From the foregoing we learn that intelligence
is glory, and is essential to future happiness;
then the question arises, which is the best pos-
sible way to gain it? Intelligence is spirit, em-
bodied, mind, skill, understanding or knowledge,
which implies truth ascertained, information,
learning.
	Desire spiritual gifts, 1st Cor., xiv chap.
"Every good gift and every perfect gift is from
above, and cometh down from the Father of
lights, with whom is no variableness, neither
shadow of turning." James 1st chap., 17th
verse; and they are instituted for "the perfect-
ing of the saints" Eph. 4th chap. for "where
there are no visions the people perish" and "the
manifestation of the Spirit is given to every
man to profit withal." For to one is given by
the Spirit the word of wisdom; to another the
word of knowledge by the same Spirit. 1st Cor,
12th chap.
	The Lord told Joseph Smith that to those
that feared Him and served Him in truth and
righteousness to the end, to them He would re-
veal all mysteries, the wonders of eternity;
they should know "their wisdom shall be great
and their understanding reach to heaven	*	*
for by my spirit will I enlighten them" and
make known unto them "things which eye has
not seen, nor ear heard, nor yet entered into
the heart of man."
	Jesus Christ says: "I am the spirit of truth,"
and truth is knowledge of things as they are,
and as they were, and as they are to come. Doc.
and Cov. page 245. Jesus Christ received not
a fulness at first, but continued from grace to
grace until he received a fulness. "The spirit
of truth is of God." Prest. Brigham Young
said, "Every member has the right of receiving
revelation for himself, both male and fe-
male. It is the very life of the church of the
living God in all ages of the world."

[Column 2]
	Apostle George Q. Cannon says, "There is an
influence born with every person that to a cer-
tain extent is a spirit of revelation," so as we
all possess more or less of this faculty and by
cultivation could no doubt develop it to a con-
siderable degree, then our minds would be im-
pressed in regard to passing events and enlight-
ened on all subjects we undertook to study or
think about.
	Brother Calkin, in Mill. Star, says: "The Di-
vine Will has ordained that the self-exertion
of his creatures, rightly directed, shall be the
proper source of blessings and the means to
bring about their redemption, salvation and
eternal exaltation."
	Though study and practice are indispensable,
any one whose mind has been inspired to un-
derstand things, to write by inspiration or im-
pression, or produce anything mechanical by
the same power will readily perceive the beauty
of the thing and the ease with which it is accom-
plished, so far beyond anything we could do by
our own common unaided ability, that were we
assisted by this power and benefited by the ex-
perience of those who have gone before; through
the "ministry of angels" our means of acquir-
ing knowledge would be infinitely greater; and
through continuing from "grace to grace" we
could not fail to receive a fulness in far less
time than by our present comparatively slow
process, to say nothing of the indescribable
happiness felt by the close proximity of the
Holy Spirit, or the knowledge that our
minds were enlightened and our future bliss
enhanced by continued revelation from Him
that "doeth all things well."
						ANN FELLOWS.


16 WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	GOOD manners are made up of petty sacrifices.

	UNDER all speech that is good for any-
thing there is a silence that is better. Silence
is as deep as eternity; speech is shallow
as time.

	HOPE is like the sun, which as we journey
toward it casts the shadow of our burdens be-
hind us.

	MISS Edith Simcox is spoken of by some of
the English people as "the inheritor of the
mantle that dropped from George Eliot's
shoulders."

	MISS Lillie C. Darst was elected an
alternate delegate to the Republican State
Convention of Ohio. The lady is the editor
of a paper at Circleville, Ohio. The papers
speak of it as "a woman's triumph."

	MRS. Helen M. Slocum, of New York
city, peacefully passed away on Tuesday, May
16th. Mrs. Slocum was born in Vermont, and
spent her girlhood among the Green Mountains.
She married in her early youth Dr. Volney G.
Slocum. Her own family were Friends, be-
longing to one of the best old Quaker stocks
of this country. She was brought up in the
school of reform. In her girlhood she was an
ardent anti-slavery advocate; later she took up
the cause of temperance; but she devoted the
prime of her life to the advocacy of woman
suffrage.
	Mrs. Slocum was chairman of the executive
committee of the New York Suffrage Associ-
ation, and first vice-president of the city so-
ciety—Ex.
	For several years past her voice has been
heard at every meeting of the friends of suf-
frage in New York city and vicinity. Her
style of speaking was peculiarly earnest and
impressive, her pathetic appeals often bringing
tears to the eyes of her auditors. Her last
speech was made at the convention held in city
in Chickering hall on Feb. 1st and 2d.—Ex.

AND
