SALT LAKE CITY.	-	OCTOBER 1, 1882.


[Column 1]
GOING TO THE CONVENTION.

	Saturday morning, up with the lark, hur-
ried preparations for starting, bade good bye
for a week or so to home and loved ones,
and took the early morning train for
Ogden, chatting pleasantly all the way with
old acquaintances. The day was a glorious
one, and every thing fresh in the morning sun.
At Ogden, we went on board the Union Pacific
train, new car, KEARNEY, very elegant, parlor,
dining-room and bed-rooms all combined. Here
we found ourselves in the company of some
strangers, who had a great curiosity about Mor-
mons, and anxious to see a man with more
wives; one very sweet young girl, with a pretty
face and golden curls, longed to see a man with
two wives, another wanted to see one with eight
or ten wives. The scenery, however, was much
more attractive than the chatter and gossip
about "our people," and gazing out upon the
hills and fields one found abundant food for re-
flection.
	The mountains were even more beautiful now
than when robed in the bright, green, robe of
summer; the tints were growing mellower and
the landscape more picturesque. Beautiful
mountain vales, and shady nooks near limpid
streams where soft willows drooped fringing the
water’s edge so gracefully. The little brown
birds flitted in and out, and chirped merrily
in the sunshine, bending the long branches
down to the water's surface. High mountains
towered loftily towards the blue sky, and afar
in the distance the while caps glistened in the
sunlight. Here and there great tufts of velvety
locking low bushes contrasted with the barren
rocky cliffs, where occasionally a straight pine
tree rose apparently from out the solid rock;
and with the gorgeously tinted shrubbery, made
a picture so charming that one might well
wonder if this were the land of sage-brush, sun-
flowers and cat-tails. The scenery was diversi-
fied, making a pleasing contrast to the traveler
if seeking rest from business and bustle. The
immense rugged and rough heights of red rock
stood like giant mansions, old fortifications, or
the ruined castles of a departed race. Irregular
and fantastical, yes even wierd enough to satisiy
the most imaginative fancy that in some of
these quaint old crags and cliffs, robbers might
have had their strongholds, and could the
mountains speak, what wonderful legends might
be unfolded. But passing from the unreal to
the positive, here were the Rocky Mountains,
sublime in their grandeur. Below were the
habitations of men in every available nook and
corner, and groan fields of lucerne, wheat just
stacked, and sometimes a "meadow sweet with
hay" new mown and fragrant. And further on
away from the haunts of men, and where the
brooks lay in the low grass at the foot of the
mountains, ornamented, shapely elderberry trees
were clustered together, the luscious fruit purply

[Column 2]
blue lay in flat rich heavy bunches, on the grace-
ful, drooping boughs.
	Carried away mentally with the charms of
mountain scenery, one could not help forgetting,
for the time being, the errand upon which we
were going, and the importance attached to the
special question about to be discussed on the
public platform. The blue hills stretching away
In the distance were gently bathing in the pur-
ple haze, and the gray shadows were gathering
as the sunset faded in the West. One could
never tire of such natural beauties though it was
easy to be dreamy and pensive.
	On Sunday morning, between Rawlins and
Laramie, as we were steaming along, we saw a
small herd of antelopes not more than a half a
mile away, it was quite a sight as those pretty
creatures are now very seldom seen so near the
railroad track. At Laramie we got off to get a
cup of tea. It was easy to see it was Sunday, for
the people were in their Sunday attire. Little
children with their Sunday School books just
starting towards the meeting house. As we
traveled farther East on the road, the country
grew flatter and less to see—the mountains were
still blue in the immense distance, and a few still
wore their snowy caps. As we neared Cheyenne
we came to Dale Creek bridge, quite a curiosity;
every body went out to look, even Mr. Jennings.
It is very slenderly built, though It may be just
as strong as is necessary. The rocks on both
sides, as we passed down through the ravine,
were very handsome and in the morning sun
they looked like the coarse rough specimens of
amethyst. The snow sheds are a great nuisance
when one Is writing, for you no sooner get
started than darkness blinds you.
	At Sherman there was a curiosity shop or sort
of museum, specimens of various kinds, but we
no sooner arrived at the door than the conductor
shouted "all aboard," and we were obliged to
turn back. There Is a monument erected there,
and inscribed "To Oliver and Oakes Ames." It
is 60 feet high, square in form and tapers up
from the base, until the top is small; it is built
to stand for ages, and is perfectly solid, cost a for-
tune to erect it; no doubt those who built it had
the pyramids of Egypt In mind, yet we have
not been informed that there is any historic
chronology contained therein. Shortly after
leaving Sherman we came to a grove of cedars,
very beautiful in this desert waste. A perfect
picture in the sunlight, and made one feel as if
they were not quite outside vegetation.
	Perhaps this is enough, for once, of the jour-
ney to the Convention, and next time we shall
be able to tell what happened there, who we saw
and what transpired. Which will be far more
interesting to most of our readers than the
description here given of the Union Pacific
road.


THE WOMAN SUFFRAGE LAW.

	In this number of the EXPONENT it will be seen
that the law of this Territory, giving to women
the right of suffrage has been fully sustained
from the charges made against it by political trick
sters, and discord creators, who have an exist-
ence and are kept before the public only by their
continual harangues and false and vehement
assertions. Both Judges Hunter and Emerson,
sustained the law in every particular, and in the
strongest language at their command, main-
tained the right of the Territorial Legislature to
pass the act which it was attempted to over-
throw, in the interest of a meager and unscru-
pulous minority. Judge Twiss also asserted the
validity of the law, but held that women must
be taxpayers before they could vote. In view,
however, of the rulings of the two Judges first
mentioned, it is most probable that this portion


of Judge Twiss' decision will be ruled against by
the Supreme Court, and that the right of wo-
men to vote under the existing law will remain
undisturbed. The result is no more than was
anticipated, but it is gratifying to know that
we have men on the bench who are not afraid to
breast the waves of wild and unreasoning
clamors of a party frantic for power to which
they are in no way entitled.


HOME AFFAIRS.

	NEW COMPENDIUM—We are enabled to state
that a work entitled “A Compendium of the
Doctrines of the Gospel," compiled by Apostle
Franklin D. Richards and Elder James A. Little,
is from the press, and is now for sale.

	ON Friday next the General Semi-Annual
Conference of the Church of Jesus Christ of
Latter-day Saints will commence. In view of
the early harvest and generally favorable
weather, it is believed the attendance will be
unusually large. The interest of the present
times will also be an important factor in draw-
ing together large numbers of the Saints, to be
advised by the authorities.

	ON Monday, Oct. 9th, the Territorial Conven-
tion, for the nomination of a Delegate to Con-
gress, will be held. All the county conventions
have been held and all delegates chosen, and
there is little doubt but a man well qualified to
represent the best interests of the people will be
chosen. Quite a number ot prominent gentle-
men are mentioned in connection with the posi-
tion, any one of whom would be satisfactory to
the people.

	THE National Woman’s Suffrage Convention
met at Omaha on Tuesday, Sept. 26, with a large
delegation of the leading suffrage ladies from
all parts of the United States. Utah was repre-
sented by Sister Emmeline B. Wells, editor of
the WOMAN'S EXPONENT, and by Sister Zina
D. Young. Neither of the ladies has yet re-
turned, but are expected back in a day or two.
In the next number of the EXPONENT, a de-
tailed account of the proceedings will be given.

	THE season now drawing to a close seems to
have been a very happy one for these who fol-
low the foundation industry—farming. Every-
where the reports are of abundant and excellent
harvests, second only to those of the preceding
year, which was perhaps without a parallel in
the history of the Territory. Large and excellent
crops make the heart of the farmer glad, and
send a ray of cheerfulness unconsciously
through the hearts of the whole community; for
what is bleaker and more dismal than a poor
and unsatifactory harvest?

	THE Governor has made a large number of ap-
pointments to various offices in the Territory,
under the authority which he assumes to have
derived from Congress. In Salt Lake County,
nearly all the parties have applied for their
offices but the applications have without excep-
tion been denied. On Wednesday two individ-
uals applied to the courts to force the present
occupants to give up their offices, and the case
will be argued before Judge Hunter on the 10th
of October. The parties are Arthur Pratt, who
wants to be made Sheriff of Salt Lake County,
and one George C. Douglass, who aspires to be
Territorial Auditor. The ablest lawyers have
been secured to defend the rights of the people as
against the pretended rights of these official as-
pirants; and the result is looked forward to with
confidence.

	ON Saturday, Sept. 30th, the County Conven-
tion of the People’s Party of Salt Lake County
was held In the County Court House. There
was a full attendance of delegates, among them


several ladies. The proceedings were com-
menced in the usual manner, and G. M. Ottin-
ger made temporary chairman, and Francis
Cope temporary secretary, who, after the organ-
ization, were made permanent officers. A com-
mittee on credentials consumed some time in
examining the certificates of delegates, during
which time C. W. Penrose, Esq., made a stir-
ring speech. A committee of seven was ap-
pointed to make nominations for delegates to the
Territorial Convention, which meets on Mon-
day, the 9th inst. The committee nominated
sixteen delegates and they were acted on sep-
arately, and all the nominees were sustained by
the convention.
	ELSEWHERE will be found an advertisement
of Dr. Pratt’s, stating that she will resume her
class in obstetrics about the 15th of October.
She states that during the course, lectures will
be given by Drs. Anderson, Benedict and other
prominent and experienced physicians. This is
an opportunity which we would urge all inter-
ested in such matters to avail themselves of. It
is gaining in an easy manner that which Mrs.
Pratt spent great labor and expense in at-
taining. Her large practice is steadily increas-
ing, and the cognizance and assistance of the
leading local physicians are guarantee sufficient
to entice anyone to grasp Mrs. Pratt’s generous
offer. May she have a large and studious class
to reward her for her pains.


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


RECIPES.

	GREEN TOMATOES PICKLED: 4 quarts of tom-
atoes sliced, 4 oz. salt, 1 tablespoonful of allspice,
3 of cloves, 2 of pepper, 4 of black mustard seed-
3 onions sliced. Put about half the salt into a
kettle with some vinegar. Put the sliced toma-
toes and onions into this and let them boil a few
minutes, skim them out, and let them drain.
Then put them into a jar with remainder of salt
and spices, and cover them with scalding vine-
gar; horseradish root improves them, making
them crisp.
	GREEN TOMATOES PICKLED SWEET: 2 gal. of
tomatoes sliced, 2 qts vinegar, same of sugar, 2
table spoonfuls of salt, 2 of pepper, 1 of allspice, 1
of cloves. Method: Put enough water in a
kettle with a teacup of vinegar, to cover the
tomatoes. Let them scald, then put in a jar,
then take spices and vinegar and let them come
to a boil, and pour over tomatoes. Horseradish
root placed on top of a jar of pickles makes them
crisp. All tried and good recipes.
	SWEET PICKLE PEACHES: 8 lbs of fruit, 4 lbs.
of sugar, 1 pt. of cider vinegar, 1 oz. each of
cloves, allspice, cinnamon. Method, make syrup
with vinegar, sugar and spices, except cloves
which you stick in the peaches, two to each
peach. Put in the fruit and boil till easily pricked
with a straw, take the fruit out, and boil the
syrup 20 minutes longer, then pour it over the
fruit, boil up the syrup three mornings, pour over
fruit and then seal up.
	APPLE JAM: 10 lbs. sugar, 10 lbs. good sour
apples; chopped fine. The grated peel of three
lemons, and a few pieces of white ginger root.
Boil till apples look clear and tender.


[Column 1]

﻿LONGFELLOW IN WESTMINSTER.

	To an American and to those who, though not
native-born, have so long been associated with
the institutions of the United States, as to view
them with patriotic feelings, it is pleasant to
note the efforts now making in the mother
country, to have a bust of Longfellow placed in
Westminster Abbey. As might naturally have
been anticipated, the movement, at its incipien-
cy, met with general though unreasonable oppo-
sition. It was urged that to place a monument

[Column 2]

of an American in a place reverently sacred to
the heroes of grand old England, would be to
sweep away a time-honored custom—a custom
which from its very age, had grown to be looked
upon as inviolate. Westminster Abbey is, if we
may be allowed the expression, viewed as a post
mortem museum of noted Britons. However,
good sense is a characteristic of the English, and
where intelligence and genius are considered,
the British are singularly free from those race
prejudices which seem inherent in people of
whatever nationality. So, after a little delibera-
tion, two or three noted Englishmen endorsed
the proposition, and added the weight of their
approval to the agitation. Nearly all the great
minds of England have favored the movement,
until at present there remains scarcely a doubt
that a bust of Longfellow will be placed along
with those of the kings in fact, and the emper-
ors in thought, in the "tight little isle."
	It is gratifying, as before stated, to think that
there should be a breadth of thought and a
democracy of sentiment among the English as
to tolerate such an invasion of ancient customs;
and it is singularly indicative of the friendly
feeling that is growing up between the mother
country and her brilliant offspring. But more
than all, it is pleasing to note the intellectual
development of an age, which recognizes genius
as belonging to no nationality, but as the pro-
duct of a grand old world, which if created for
any purpose at all, was brought into existence
for the good of all, of whatever nationality,
whatever sex, of whatever color.
	If the Supreme Father sees fit to endow one
person with a far reaching breadth of intellect,
such as will make him pre-eminent among men,
can it be supposed this intellect was intended
for the benefit and the glory of one nation only? or
is it designed for the honor of mankind every-
where? It is natural, and may be due to a wise
provision in nature, that one should point with
pride to the great men his native land has pro-
duced, but he is indeed contracted who would
refuse the meed of praise to acknowledged worth
for the base reason that he belonged to a race
which was individually or generally dispised.
In the onward march of events, the world will
outgrow all these detestable prejudices, and the
admission of a bust of Longfellow into the sainted
precincts of Westminster Abbey must prove a
veritable iconoclast of modern times in the de-
molition of race gods. It points the way to a
cosmopolitan sentiment, leading men to the
idea that they are more like citizens of a glori-
ous world than of any particular nation. Of all
Americans, it is most fitting that this honor
should be paid to Longfellow; not that it can
in anyway effect either him or his fame, but
that all who have felt the sweet influence of his
writings will delight in seeing accorded to him
that which is generally esteemed an undying
honor. Himself as free from prejudice as a
child, with a spirit of a gentleness, and a
heart of love like an angel's, it is indeed fitting
that his name and his fame should be an instru-
ment in destroying the barriers of a race preju-
dice that have so long denied to genius and to
sterling worth admission to universal apprecia-
tion and acknowledged merit. And when his
monument shall have been placed amid the
shades of greatness and grandeur and godlike
nobility of England, it will rather add to than
derive from the sanctity of its surroundings. It
will shed the same sweet influence over the
memory of the great dead with whom it is now
associated, as it did over the lives of those who
lived with the poet and as it does now over the
hearts of those who survive.


FALSEHOOD may have its hour, but it has
no future.—Pressense.


OCTOBER.

	How sadly the light wind sighs through the
tall trees, how mellow the sunlight is, and look
what dark shadows are cast upon the old stone
wall. Each little sunbeam seems like a thread
of gold, that weaves itself into a yellow pattern
on each green leaf, whilst here and there we
find one tinged with bronze and red, and some
are piteously hurled upon the ground; how sad
their rustle breaks upon the ear. Can it be that
Autumn is here ? Yes it most be so, for look up-
on the mountains, where a week since it was
green and mossy, now the light frost has com-
menced its work, and the foliage is changed to
the most brilliant red and yellow. The bushes
hang low with their clusters of red and purple
berries. The soft haze that played around the
hills a week ago is gone, and the sky is bright
and clear again. Ah! what a cool breeze that
was, and see how it shook the green boughs of
the trees, breaking the tender stems and thrust-
ing the rosy cheeked apples in heaps upon the
ground. Each one that remains looks longingly
at the bright eyed children that chatter and laugh
under the spreading boughs as if to say, “take
me, sweet cherub, for at the next gust of wind I
shall fall and bruise my tender skin;” but the
children jump and laugh and fill their little
baskets with the poor things that fell the night
before, little heeding the lonely apples that
stand out on the great bare limbs.
	The birds chirp as they flit from bough to
bough, no doubt wondering why the sun is not
so warm, and why the leaves must wither and
fall. Some of the old wise ones are seeking
homes far away, where it is always summer
Alas! how lonely we shall be without these sweet
songsters. The sparrows quarrel with each other
and the little one worry and chirp about the
warmest place in the nest. The flowers were
never prettier or more fragrant, and you can
scarcely step in the garden without treading on
a vine of morning glories; see, they have even
twined themselves around these weeds by the
walk. How beautiful are their pale delicate hues,
and how innocently they lift their sweet faces
up towards heaven. Thump! On the ground
come some more rosy apples. The children will
have busy work in the morning. The night air
is chilly and the children hurry into the house,
to the nice warm supper that is awaiting them.
Then they gather around the old hearth on
which the bright fire burns; each one listens
quietly while grandma tells about her old home,
where there were no high mountains, but woods
and forests with trees so full of large brown nuts.
The great bright eyes open wide as she tells of
how they used to go nut gathering, and little
heads look puzzled as they think of what a strange
place it must be, where there are no grand old
mountains and the little faces smile with glee
when they remember about the large barrel of
hickory nuts that grandma has so kindly pro-
mised them. The fire burns more briskly than
before, and the whole room is aglow with its
red light. All the while outside Jack Frost is
performing his night's work, nipping the fruit
and flowers, and painting each leaf with a hun-
dred different hues.
								BEATRICE.


RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.

	Resolutions of respect on the death of William
Knight, member of the 20th Ward Primary As-
sociation, who departed this life Sept. 1st, 1882,
with heart disease.
	As God in his wisdom has seen fit to call an-
other of his precious jewels, who has labored
faithfully in the performance of every duty, we,
as officers and members of the Primary Associa


tion, unanimously adopt the following resolu-
tions:
	We sincerely condole with his parents, brothers
and sisters, in their heavy bereavement, and we
tender them our heartfelt sympathy.
	May his many virtues be emulated by the
members of our Association, so that his noble ex-
ample may stimulate others to a life of virtue
and usefulness.
	Resolved, that we adopt these resolutions, as
a token of respect, that we present a copy of
these resolutions to his parents, and publish the
same in the WOMAN'S EXPONENT, and place a
copy on our record.

The angels bore him robed in spotless white,
To their peaceful home of light.
								EMMA TOONE.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


OBITUARY.

	DIED: In Brighton Ward, Aug. 12th. 1882, of
cholera infantum, after an illness of many
weeks, Minnie Presinda, the dearly beloved
child of George W. and Agnes Squires Hunting
ton; age one year and ten months, lacking three
days.
	Little Minnie was an unusually bright and
interesting child for her age, also very large;
but from her birth she was always afflicted with
sickness of some kind, so that she was only a
little sufferer during her brief sojourn in this
world of grief and affliction.
	The angels have now taken her home to the
celestial kingdom of our Lord, where there is no
more grief and pain, but all is joy and peace for-
ever.

'Twas not in anguish nor in wrath,
	The Reaper came that day,
Bright angels wished the earth,
	And bore the flower away;
A lovely bud so young and fair,
	Called hence by early doom.
"Just come to show how fair a flower,
	In Paradise would bloom."
									A. C.


R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. AND PRIMARY
REPORTS.

CASTLE DALE, EMERY CO.

	DEAR SISTER:—With great pleasure I com-
municate to your very interesting paper the
following items.
	On the 12th and 13th of August, 1882, we
were visited by two of the Twelve Apostles, E.
Snow and J. H. Smith, who held two days
meeting at this place, during which time they
recommended us to organize a Relief Society
in all the settlements of this Stake.
	The sisters of this Ward met to organize
on the 24th. August 25, elected the following
officers: Annie N. Larsen, President; Hannah
Seely and Harriet A. Willcox, Counselors;
Lucinda Jeffs, Secretary, Ellen Mitler, Treas-
urer. They were blessed and set apart under
the hands of Pres. C. G. Larsen and Counselor,
who also made some excellent remarks, and en-
couraged the sisters to be active to the duties
devolving upon them in their high and holy
calling, as Saints of the Most High. Pres.
Annie N. Larsen gave good instructions, all of
which the sisters feel to appreciate. We held
our first meeting on the seventh of September.
	Praying God to bless you in your labor of
love, I remain, with kindest regards, your sister
in the Gospel,		LUCINDA JEFFS, Sec'y.


The things that belong to men must be un-
derstood, in order that they may be loved.
The things that belong to God must be loved,
in order that they may be understood.—Pascal.


[Column 1]
SCENES AND INCIDENTS IN NAUVOO

BY HELEN MAR WHITNEY.

	The scenes in Nauvoo during the summer of
1843 were of an exciting nature, and continued
on with but little cessation till the Prophet
and Patriarch were murdered, in a boasted
land of religious liberty, and their people
forced by mob-law to seek safety among sav-
ages.
	I remember the morning that Joseph and
family left the city to visit his sister-in-law
near Dixon, in Lee County, he called in as he
was passing, to bid us good bye, and the first
news that we heard was the unlawful arrest
made by Missourians, intending to drag him off
into that state, but their brutal conduct roused
the indignation of the people at Dixon, and by
a writ of habeas corpus, served by his friends,
he was placed under the arm of the law. The
news spread so rapidly, that a company of
horsemen, numbering 175, started the same
evening under the command of Generals' Wm.
Law and Charles C. Rich. The officers from
Missouri had laid their plans to kidnap Joseph
while on the journey, but his brethren guarded
him too closely, and they arrived home in safety
June 30th, where they were met on the out-
skirts by a great multitude of the Saints, who,
with Wm. Pitts, Brass Band, and loud cheers
and firing of artillery, escorted him to the
mansion.
	A meeting had been previously appointed at
5 o'clock the same day, in the grove west of
the Temple, where he delivered a speech, which
can be duly appreciated at this present time.
	"I meet you with a heart full of gratitude
to Almighty God, and I presume you all feel
the same. I hardly know how to express my
feelings. I feel as strong as a giant. I pulled
sticks with the men coming along, and I pulled
up with one hand the strongest man that could
be found. Then two men tried, but they could
not pull me up. And I continued to pull, men-
tally, until I pulled Missouri to Nauvoo.	*	*
*	It is not so much my object to tell my
afflictions, and trials, and troubles, as to speak
of the writ of habeas corpus, so that the minds
of all may be corrected. It has been asserted
by the great and wise men, lawyers, and others,
that our municipal powers and legal tribunals
are not to be sanctioned by the authorities of
the State; and accordingly they want to make
it lawful to drag away innocent men from their
families and friends, and have them put to
death by ungodly men for their religion. Re-
lative to our city charter, courts, rights of
habeas corpus, etc., I wish you to know and
publish that we have all power; and if any man
from this time forth says anything to the con-
trary, cast it into his teeth.
	"There is a secret in this. If there is not
power in our charter and courts, then there is
not power in the State of Illinois, nor in the
Congress or Constitution of the United States;
for the United States gives unto Illinois her
constitution or charter, and Illinois gave unto
Nauvoo her charter, ceding unto us our vested
rights, which she has no right nor power to
take from us. All the power there was in Il-
linois she gave to Nauvoo. I want you to
hear and learn, O, Israel, this day, what is for
the happiness and peace of this city and people.
If our enemies are determined to oppress us
and deprive us of our constitutional rights and
privileges as they have done, and if the author-
ities that are on the earth will not sustain us
in our rights, nor give us that protection which
the laws and constitution of the United States,
and of this State, guaranteed unto us; then we
will claim them from a higher power—from
heaven—yea, from God Almighty. Before I
will bear this unhallowed persecution any
longer, before I will be dragged away again,
among my enemies for trial, I will spill the last

[Column 2]
drop of blood in my veins. To bear it any
longer would be a sin.
	"It did my soul good to see your feelings
and love manifested towards me. I thank God
that I have the honor to lead so virtuous and
honest a people; to be your leader and lawyer,
as was Moses to the children of Israel. Hosan-
nah to Almighty God, who has delivered us
thus from out of the seven troubles. I com-
mend you to his grace, and may the blessings
of heaven rest upon you, in the name of Jesus
Christ. Amen."
	He was proven to be innocent and was once
more freed from the Missourians; but they
were determined not to give up, and pretended
that the authorities of Nauvoo had rescued a
prisoner from justice, but confident that his
friends would resist every attempt on their
part to arrest him, Gov. Reynolds, of Missouri,
applied to Gov. Ford, of Illinois, requesting
him to compel our people to deliver up the
Prophet, by calling out a force of militia for
this purpose, which request Gov. Ford po-
litely refused to grant.
	Our city was occasionally visited by Laman-
ites and a deputation of Pottawatamie chiefs
were in the city waiting to see Joseph when
he returned from Dixon, and as soon as consist-
ant after the trial was over he received them.
	After being assured that all present were
friends to Joseph, their orator arose and said:
(it being interpreted;) "We as a people have
long been distressed and oppressed. We have
been driven from our lands many times. We
have been wasted away by wars, until there
are but few of us left. The white man has
hated us and shed our blood, until it has ap-
peared as though there would soon be no In-
dian left. We have talked with the Great
Spirit, and the Great Spirit has talked with
us. We have asked the Great Spirit to save
us and let us live, and the Great Spirit has
told us that he has raised up a great Prophet
chief, and friend, who would do us great good
and tell us what to do; and the Great Spirit
has told us that you are the man (pointing to
the Prophet). We have now come a great way
to see you and hear your words, and to have you
tell us what to do. Our horses have become
poor traveling, and we are hungry. We will
now wait and hear your words."
	Joseph was considerably affected, so much
so that he wept. He said in return: "I have
heard your words. They are true! The Great
Spirit has told you the truth. I am your
friend and brother, and I wish to do you good.
Your fathers were once a great people. They
worshiped the Great Spirit. The Great Spirit
did them good. He was their friend, but they
left the Great Spirit and would not hear his
words nor keep them. The Great Spirit left
them, and they began to kill one another, and
they have been poor and afflicted until now.
	"The Great Spirit has given me a book, and
told me that you will soon be blessed again.
The Great Spirit will soon begin to talk with
you and your children."
	Raising the Book of Mormon, he said, "This
is the book which your fathers made. I wrote
upon it. This tells me what you will have to
do. I now want you to begin to pray to the
Great Spirit. I want you to make peace with
one another, and do not kill white men; it is
not good, but ask the Great Spirit for what you
want. And it will not be long before the Great
Spirit will bless you, and you will cultivate
the earth, and build good houses like white
men. We will give you something to eat and
to take home with you."
	The Prophet had an ox killed for them, and
some horses were also prepared for them.
	They remembered the kindness of Joseph
and his people, and when driven from our
homes they made us welcome upon their land,
where we were obliged to make our Winter
Quarters.


﻿We have certainly seen the fulfilment of
the Prophet's words concerning the red man,
cultivating the earth and building houses, as
well as other predictions, and are satisfied that
all will be fulfilled.
	The following is from an address delivered
by the Prophet, at the grove, on Sunday, July
8th, 1844.
	"The Saints can testify whether I am willing
to lay down my life for my brethren. If it has
been demonstrated that I have been willing to
die for a 'Mormon,' I am bold to declare that
before heaven, that I am just as ready to die in
defending the rights of a Presbyterian, a Bap-
tist, or a good man of any other denomination,
for the same principle which would trample
upon the rights of the Roman Catholics, or of
any other denomination that may be unpopular
and too weak to defend itself.
	"It is the love of liberty which inspires my
soul—civil and religious liberty to the whole
of the human race. Love of liberty was dif-
fused into my soul by my grandfathers, while
they dandled me on their knees. One of the
grand fundamental principles of 'Mormonism'
is to receive truth, let it come whence it may.
If I esteem mankind to be in error, shall I
bear them down? No. I will lift them up, and
in their own way too, if I cannot persuade
them my way is better; and I will not seek to
compel any man to believe as I do, only by
the force of reasoning, for truth will cut its
own way."	
	On the following Sabbath he preached, and
I presume that hundreds are still living who
will recollect these incidents.
	He said: "Let me be resurrected with the
Saints, whether I ascend to heaven, or descend
to hell; we will turn the devils out of doors and
make a heaven of it. Where this people are,
there is good society. What do we care where
we are, if the society be good ?"
	At another time he said, "I defy all the
world to destroy the work of God; and I
prophesy they never will have power to kill me
till my work is accomplished, and I am ready
to die. * * * I proclaim in the name of
the Lord God Almighty, that I will fellowship
nothing in the Church but virtue, integrity and
uprightness."
	That same spirit which governed the Pro-
phet and his people then, controls and keeps
them together to-day; and let our enemies con-
tinue to persecute and threaten as they may,
they cannot hinder freedom of thought, nor
prevent our making for ourselves a paradise on
earth, nor from securing our passport to heav-
en; no matter where our lot may be cast, we
can all make one for ourselves, and as Joseph
said, "defy all the world to help it" or "to des-
troy the work of God," or any of his servants
until their "work is accomplished," then they,
like Joseph, will he "ready to die."
	The Missourians, with all their cruelties and
whippings, could not crush out nor subdue that
spirit, nor make a "Mormon" feel that he was
conquered, and this was what made them so
angry.
	Our circumstances, since then, have reversed;
through the goodness of our Heavenly Father
we have gained a foot hold in these mountains,
and can now sit "under our own vine and fig-
tree;" and shall "hold the fort," God helping
us.
	To be Continued.


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 71


[Column 1]
A SEPTEMBER WALK.

BY HANNAH T. KING.

	Come with me my friend, my congenial spirit,
let us take a walk this beautiful calm Septem-
ber day, with the canopy of heaven one eternal
blue bending over us, I cannot see the smallest
cloud. We will climb the hills, or as it is

[Column 2]
called "the Bench," from whence we can over-
look our beautiful city; the scenery is lovely,
in some parts grand. Fine houses and pretty
hills abound in the prospect. Let us take a
seat on Arsenal Hill, my beloved "Ensign
Peak" forming the back of our chair! The
City Hall is right before us, a large substantial
stone building, raised for time and eternity,
and for business and common sense purposes.
The Theatre then presents itself, and is a fine
and complete building. And now the grand
Temple looms up in the midst, and the heart of
every Latter-day Saint yearns for the comple-
tion of that unique mysterious House of God!
upon which rest great and mighty promises to
the true and faithful ones. On the same block
stands the small but elegant Assembly Hall,
with its ornamental turrets clustering round
the principal tower, on the summit of which is
the figure of an angel sounding the Gospel
Trumpet of Latter-day, and also denoting the
the cardinal points. This sacred block is sur-
rounded by a high wall—when the Temple is
finished a handsome iron palisading will enclose
it—with four massive gates of exquisite work-
manship, North, South, East and West. It
will be the largest and most unique Temple in
America; its foundation is laid for eternity,
and the superstructure is upon the same model.
Around, all around are those grand old moun-
tains. Well may we apply the Scriptural term
—"the everlasting Hills"—in their sublime and
eternal silence, clothed in their garments of
purple haze, or the pale green robe, and their
veil of mist, as the atmosphere, or time of day
throw around them. Here let us sit and medi-
tate for a short time. The silence of those
eternal mountains speaks to our spirit ear, and
to our spirit heart, and their voice and lan-
guage is angelic and inspirational, and also com-
forting; it whispers of the great beyond, to
which with noiseless tread, the hand of time
is leading the whole human family; it whispers
of the myriads that have lived, and revelled,
and enjoyed in the palaces of luxury, the gay
denizens of the world "clothed in robes of purple
and fine linen, and faring sumptuously every
day"—some with crowns upon their heads, and
the royal sceptre in their hands, and glitter-
ing with costly gems of the diamond, and the
pearl, the ruby and the emerald, the opal and
the turquoise, every eye watching to gratify
their wishes, even before they are expressed,
every knee ready to do them honors; their
lives so favored that it would seem a portion of
heaven had descended especially to bless these
petted children of humanity. Then reverse the
picture. Take the poor of the earth, who are
ever in the majority, see how they have toiled,
how they have indeed eaten their bread in the
sweat of their brow—from the raising of the
sun to the going down of the same; from their
days of childhood, even to old age, labor has
been their portion. And then the mourning,
the afflicted bearing life's burdens through the
three score years and ten—and perhaps ten
more. Where are all these myriads? Their
place knows them no more. These very
mountains may be the sepulchre of many
such! Yes, I know these mountians are tombs
of many of the human family, and perhaps
some called upon them to cover them and
hoped they would cover them for ever! for
they were wretched, and without hope, and
without faith; think of all the heterogenous
mass of human beings that have lived upon
this earth, and then been buried in its bosom.
Where, where are they? Speak ye grand old
mountains, that have beheld the ages come
and go, tell us the histories of those that are
now become as ye are: the dust of the earth.
The whole globe must be impregnated with
human dust; and that human dust has to be
resurrected. Think of it! fancy we hear that
voice saying, "Lazarus! come forth," and at the
sound of that Almighty voice, earth had no

[Column 3]
power to retain her prey. Is not this a type
of the whole? Yea, verily.
	You are silent, dear, do you think I am ser-
monizing? Oh! thank you. I am so glad
you feel as I do; it is these mountains that are
preaching to us, and blessed be God. Through
the teachings of his Spirit we have the faculty
to receive their impressions. "There are many
voices in the world, and none of them are with-
out signification"—some that instruct, some
that encourage, and some that fill the soul with
inexpressible poetry, for our finest feelings can-
not be written with the incompetent language
we now possess—and some fill us with silent
adoration—and we "worship in the infinite."
	Friend of my soul, you grasp my hand, and
gladly I return the pressure. We too must
pass away; surely we shall meet in the eterni-
ties. Affinity is an eternal, immutable princi-
ple, and the affinity between us is firm. Give
me your hand, let us descend the hill together,
and return to our beloved home, this day shall
be noted in the diary of our lives.
	Sept. 1882.


POETRY.

	Poetry the language of the soul, "the blossom
and fragrance of all human knowledge, human
thoughts, human passion, emotions, language."
It is something divine. It is the echo of the
the music of that heavenly choir which causes
the "strings in Being's lyre" to vibrate, and
respond in notes far from being in perfect uni-
son yet to our dull ears in sweetest cadence.
Nature overflows with spirit of poetry. The
air is full of it, and the waves cannot resist its
power but dance and sparkle in its light. The
starry heavens proclaim their unseen magnifi-
cence in innumerable voices, whose melodies
are all too perfect and delicate; for other than
the celestial beings. The stars are the poetry
of heaven. Greater desires and nobler pur-
poses are awakened, in man by its influence,
and, as Shelley says, "it redeems from decay
the visitations of the divinity in man."
	As the sun with its bright and cheerful face
dispels the darkness and fears of night, and
brings us into the fullness of glorious day, so
poetry scatters the foreboding clouds of trouble
and anger, and calms the turbulent spirit into
perfect peace. It has power to soothe our
minds to rest, after "the cares that infest the
day." We forget the toil and burden of life,
and the troubled waves of anger and resent-
ment are subdued, as if a voice had said, "peace,
be still."
	Nature's book lies open before us to read at 
will. All are not equally sensitive to its
teachings, nor does everyone understand the
language of nature; but one who cherishes a
love and reverence for the beautiful, and its
Creator, cannot fail to have those affections
continually renewed.
	Who can read Bryant without feeling that
he was an interpreter of nature? He held
"communion with her invisible forms," and "to
him she spoke a various language." The old
boughs always revealed some mystery to him,
and every tiny flower that lifted its drooping
head to be kissed by the passing breeze breathed
to him of love and tenderness. Truly he was
a poet of nature. While reading him all the
latent music of our souls is awakened, and we
are raised above the illusions of this life, and
catch glimpses of a nobler one.
	Poetry is an imitative art. Its aim is to give
pleasure by imitation. In this respect it agrees
with music, sculpture, and painting, but they
differ in the means they employ. Poetry uses
as its means, words arranged in metre, and
music employs melodious sounds, sculpture, im-
itates by reproducing beautiful forms, and
painting by reproducing delicate blendings, of
color. These sister arts have many qualities


in common, but in one poetry stands alone: it
is the only one that asserts, as words are its in-
struments Its aim is not illusion, but the ex-
citement of those emotions that would be
aroused if the scene were to be enacted.
Rhythm is a safeguard against reality, as well
as an additional pleasure. As Wordsworth
says, "There is the perception of similitude in
dissimilitude. Rhythm is the dissimiliar ele-
ment. Yet when reading the productions of
the greatest poets, our minds become so
absorbed, and our emotions so influenced and
and carried so far beyond our control, that it
seems but a short step to reality. Our imag-
inations become excited as was the poet's,
while giving to airy nothing a local habitation
and a name."
	It is doubtful whether even the greatest poets
can compose poetry at will; for it is not like
reason, a power to be exercised at pleasure,
but he must be so pervaded and engrosed by
the subjects that he writes as by inspiration
"Thoughts that breathe and words that burn."
If not thus, it will be labored and mechanical
rather than flowing and musical. Nor do we
receive the first conceptions of the poet. If he
were capable of reproducing them, it is im-
possible to imagine the result. The thoughts
that we read with greatest pleasure, are but
feeble shadows of the originals. When the
subject first presents itself to the mind, inspira-
tion flashes forth its resplendent light, impart-
ing richness and beauty, but when composition
begins, inspiration wanes, and the force and
grandeur of the beginning generally declines
towards the end.
	Poetry and music are so closely related, that
when speaking of one the other is inferred.
They are "the parents of action good and
brave."

	"Music resembles poetry: in each
	Are nameless graces, which no methods teach,
	And which a master's hand alone can reach."

	What a beautiful illustration of language is
music, but when combined with her sister art,
it becomes eloquence highly ornamented.
Music like poetry, is "a sphere descended
maid." In all nature her presence is recog-
nized, in the sighing of a reed, in the gushing
rill, in the breeze as it softly whispers in the 
trees, making the little leaves dance merrily to
its enlivening airs. Nor is monotony found
anywhere in nature; no two leaves are alike,
no two flowers, no two faces; everything has
its distinguishing feature. Fresh evidences
are ever being found of the Creator's laws of
variety.
	The power of music is irresistible. It holds
sway over the passions, allaying anger and
strife. It animates warriors to brave and
courageous deeds. In trouble and sorrow
music is a great friend, and "wisdom's aid."
	Association unites with music in her efforts
to influence the emotions.

	"It makes a glad remembrance of our youth,
	Calls back past joys, and warms us into transport."

	At close of day how pleasant to listen to the 
notes of village bells, as they burst forth loud
and clear, and then linger and die in the dis-
tance.

	"Some chord in unison with what we hear
	Is touched within us, and the heart replies."

	The character of a nation is shown by its
music. Confucius said, "Would'st thou know
if a people be well governed, if its manners be
good or bad, examine the music it practises."
The poetry of a nation expresses its sentiments.
	May America's poetry ever be of the divinest
and best, and her music the most melodious,
and so continue to live, that it can be said,
Her history is her grandest poem.
		HATTIE F. CLOUGH.
	Cohoes, N. Y.


72	WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	THE laws of Wisconsin do not allow murder-
ers, lunatics, nor women to vote. This is a
nice category in which to enumerate wives,
daughters, and mothers.—Evening Wisconsin.

	A NATIONAL society for woman suffrage has
been established in Paris. It will be indepen-
dent of any political or religious party. The
office is at 34, Rue de Chazelle (parc Monc-
eau), Paris. The minimum subscription is
three francs. There will also be honorary as-
sociates, who are not expected to subscribe.
Th[-] funds will be divided into three sections:
the expenses of the office and reports; the ex-
penses of issuing publications; and the fund for
meetings. Any other questions than that of
obtaining the suffrage for women will be ex-
cluded from the meetings and publications of
the society.—Ex.

	QUEEN ELIZABETH of Roumania, known in
literature under the pseudonym of Carmen
Sylva, has been elected a member of the Ac-
ademy of Science at Bucharest, and will be
formally installed next November. She is the
first lady to whom this compliment has been
paid. She is the daughter of Prince Herman
of Weid, and is about thirty years old. From
her early youth she has shown much taste and
talent for poetry. Her first literary work con-
sisted of poetry, which appeared in many
German reviews. Two years ago she published
a poem in German, containing four stories,
called "The Storms." It is for this work that
the Bucharest Academy has elected her a mem-
ber. She has also written a collection of phil-
osophical thoughts, which appeared in the
Nouvelle Revue; an epic called "Aschaver," and
a quantity of German translation of Rou-
manian poems, among others, those of Wassali
Alexandri, the best known of contemporary
Roumanian lyrists. The Queen is greatly be-
loved in Roumania, and it is an excellent
augury for the welfare of the young Danubian
kingdom that its sovereign assists in its literary
development—Womam's Journal.


CHILD'S ESSAY.

WEST JORDAN PRIMARY.

	My Dear Little Brothers and Sisters. As I
am appointed to write an essay I will try and
do the best I can, but I don’t think I can write
any thing that will be interesting to any of
you. I like to come to meeting and come in
time. I am willing to do any thing I am
called upon to do. We should be kind to those
placed over us, and listen to good counsel. We
are taught when we come to meeting, we should
be obedient to our parents and respectful to all
old people. We should pray night and morn-
iny, and in the day-time as well, for when any
of our little brothers or sisters are sick, we
should pray in our hearts, and have faith that
they will get well, and they will get well; inas-
much as we ask sincerely. I think if we would
attend meeting regularly we would feel a great
deal better than if we do not. My little broth-
ers and sisters, it we could only realize the
blessings that we are surrounded with. Kind
parents and teachers to instruct us. Let us
come to meeting and attend regularly and come
in time. Let us show by our actions that we
will do better in the future than we have done
in the past. Please excuse all mistakes, for
this is my first attempt; I will now close this
essay, wishing to do better the next time.
	Your Sister in the Primary Association.
		REBECCA GOFF.
July 8, 1882.
