SALT LAKE CITY,	JANUARY 1, 1883.


﻿THE YEAR 1883.

	THE WOMAN'S EXPONENT sends a hearty
greeting of a happy new year to its many readers.
Whether here at home in the valleys of the
mountains, or afar off over the seas or in what-
ever land or clime the little messenger finds its
way. May it carry with it a blessing, truth,
light, a ray of sunshine, that all who read it may
be cheered, encouraged in the path of duty,
urged to persevere in doing good and assisting
others. Stronger in the faith and more earnest
in labors for the kingdom of God upon the
earth.
	The new year is a time for good resolves, a
desire to improve in all that will help one to
gain the sure road to happiness here and here-
after. A desire to gain light and intelligence
that will be to the soul like a fountain of pure
water to refresh invigorate and strengthen it.
The year has dawned suspiciously for the Saints,
peace prosperity and every needed blessing
abounds in Utah, and future prospects seem
propitions.
	It is good to have such days as Christmas and
New Year's when one is expected to greet
friends and acquaintances with kindly or affec-
tionate salutation. One might grow cold and
hard were it not for some of the pleasant re-
minders that swoop down now and then, and
enfold one in such a loving embrace that one is
glad to submit to all the extra responsibilities,
which ever attend upon new phases and condi-
tions, for the gladness and joy it brings to the
heart. To become indifferent to the happiness
of others and be swallowed up in one's own am-
bition pride or pleasure is a very common falling
among mortals, and sometimes creates such
selfishness as to exclude even politeness, where
affability and kindness should be extended.
Kind words, cheerfulness and smiles help
one wonderfully, to be sure this is not all,
but they are often more acceptable than gifts.
This is not a homily however upon kindness
love or charity but a new year's greeting, and
whatever short comings, faults or blunders, the
EXPONENT may have been answerable for in the
past, we trust that in the future it will be able
to correct them, and improve in its appearance
ere the year that has just dawned shall have
drawn to a close. And again "A Happy New
Year" to all our friends and patrons.


[Column 1]
MRS. SAXON'S LETTERS.

	WE have received from New Orleans two
copies of the "Times Democrat," Dec. 8 and 15,
containing the letters written by Mrs. Elizabeth
Lisle Saxon while in this city dated Nov. 29
and Dec. 7. She writes without fear and tells
what she has seen, and how she has been treat-
ed by the "Mormon" people. In her first letter

[Column 2]
she speaks thus of her visit to Capt. Hooper's
soon after her arrival in this city:
	"I brought with me a letter of introduction to
him (Mr. Hooper), and as he was ill, his wife
came down and met me very cordially. I was
glad of an interview with Mrs. Hooper, for she
is an only wife, the mother of seven children.
One of her daughters is the wife of Prof.
Young, a son of Brigham's, who is one of the
corps of teachers at West Point Academy.
Mrs. Hooper is a believer in the Mormon re-
ligion, and a stout defender of its tenets. She
spoke with contempt of the Salt Lake Tribune,
declaring that if it did speak well of any Mor-
mon the rest would think that something was
wrong, so bitter and coarse had been its lan-
gauge and expressions toward them. The
Tribune editor had told me: 'It paid Hooper to
be a Mormon, and he didn't care anything about
it as a religion.'"
	The next item in her first letter is her visit to
the President of the Church. We give it com-
plete in her own language:
	"I was accompanied by Mrs. E. B. Wells to
the Gardo House to see President Taylor. This
gentleman is now President of the Church, and
has been since the death of Brigham Young.
He was one of the men in Carthage jail with
Joseph Smith and his brother Hyrum when
they were killed by the mob. Taylor was hit by
four balls. He is a stern, grave, stately and in-
telligent man, 74 or 75 years of age. He was in
the act of starting, in company with George Q.
Cannon, down to Manti, and our conversation
was very brief. 'Mrs. S. is a newspaper cor-
respondent,' said Mrs. Wells. He smiled
gravely and said, 'All we crave of Mrs. S. is
that she will tell the truth about us. Very few
do that in speaking of us.' 'Believe me, Presi-
dent Taylor,' I replied, 'I shall give your side as
fairly as I can; I look through no glasses colored
by bitter prejudice. I shall state the facts as I
see them.' A few pleasant words to Mrs. W.
and he left us."
	After this follows a good description of the
Gardo House, the Beehive and Lion Houses and
the office buildings occupied by the Church.
She speaks at some length of these places and
what she saw, and then says:
	"Nothing can be more entertaining than the
long talks with Mrs. Young, hearing her tell of
the varied experiences in her journeyings
through the Territory for near 15 years, as the
companion of her husband. She has become
familiar with the people, especially the Indian
tribes, that the Mormons call the 'Lamanites'
from the Book of Mormon."
	She speaks of our judicial affairs intelligently
and approvingly, of her visits in Mormon
homes, the people she met with, etc. A com-
plete description of the Assembly Hall and the
impressive services on the Sabbath and the ad-
ministering of the Sacrament. Here is another
extract from her letter which may be of some
interest, as it shows the true feeling "Mormon"
women have when the sacred and holy condi-
tions of celestial marriage are compared with
the vile practices which exist so extensively in
large cities:
	"I was told by a very good Mormon member
that one of the commissioners had stated 'that
he felt sorry he had seen so little of the Mormon
families during their stay here.' I am certain
that no people living could be much more
patient under all of the Federal meddling with
their affairs than the Mormons are. They hold
their religious tenets with a dogged resolve, and
they claim all as under revelation, plural mar-
riage as well. Looking at it or trying to discuss
it from a physiological standpoint is one thing
that nettles the women of the better class as
much as anything well can. I was talking

[Column 3]
with a very intelligent Scotchman to-day, and
he was citing some cases regarding our foundl-
ing hospitals and ohter Christian charities, re-
sulting from vices, and made a remark of a
nature calculated to arouse the ire of my lady
friends, and it was some little time before I
could induce them to try and consider how much
bearing this would be bound to have in dis-
cussing a subject of such nature. We held a
very earnest conversation on this subject, and
I learned from them many interesting items
in regard to the religious effect on the vices of
great cities as contrasted by the lives of Mor-
mon people, where a child is welcomed joyfully
as affording a tabernacle for a spirit to dwell in."
	In her second letter Mrs. Saxon comments
upon the political situation in reference to John
T. Caine, the Delegate of the People's Party,
and Judge Van Zile, the "Liberal" candidate,
then gives it as her opinion that the ultimate
"effect of the Edmunds bill will be hard to fore-
cast. It is impossible to convince even those
Gentiles who incline most favorably to the
Mormon people, owing to their honest, frugal,
temperate lives, that plural marriage is not still
carried on, as it is one of their revelations, and
most cherished and sacred ordinances. Com-
mon sense tells us that to legislate and enforce
law in this direction will constitute a police sys-
tem of such a nature as to place every house
under the espionage of paid individuals, and the
results growing from such a system be a more
terrible evil than the one against which we are
fighting."
	Mrs. Saxon speaks of the faith of the "Mor-
mons" in this wise:
	"Believing, as they do, in the prophecies of
the 'Latter-day Saints,' they have every reason
to anticipate great and marked changes for
themselves, under the new order and conditions
that a change of national politics will inevitably
bring about. Persecution they expect from
every quarter, and some of them glory in it, for
it more firmly unites the church and renews
the zeal of the lukewarm, binding them as a
people more closely together. This may be
fanatical and weak to the last degree, but what
can you do with a stubborn fact? Sensible,
practical, matter of fact business men will sit
down and give you the Bible, old and new, the
prophecies of Moses and Smith alike, the book
of Mormon, and Doctrines and Covenants, with
as much zeal as an old Scotch Covenanter would
the Bible; declare that he holds all true alike,
and is ready to be offered up for his loyalty to
Joseph Smith. In this day of changing faiths,
let us look upon this resolute willingness to 'die
for a cause,' with an eye of sober judgment and
put passion by. The prophecies of the past, the
signs of the times, the spirit of the people, all
seem to indicate some great and nationally im-
portant event in the near future; and to a Mor-
mon 'Zion' is paramount to all else. They be-
lieve, and so express themselves, that we are
living in the 'last days,' and this modern 'Zion'
is the promised refuge of Israel. I am glad to
say that not until to-day have I had the pleasure
of reading the letter written by Phil. Robinson
to the New York World during the summer.
He bears out my assertions made in my two let-
ters first written."
	Here is another mention of Sunday services
made in her second letter to The Times:
	"I was at church to-day and heard George Q.
Cannon speak. He is one of the twelve apostles
of the Church and had just returned from a
week's visiting with President Taylor in the
Territory. He spoke to about 3,000 people. Not
one left the church. He told of the prosperity
of the people; of the union of sentiment among
them; of the desire to be faithful to all trusts
and loyal in every way to the general govern-


ment. That this vast crowd of people are 'serfs'
is as false on the face of it as anything well can
be. The one great feature is their union and
religious zeal, and the slight line of demarcation
drawn between rich and poor. Every one is
'sister' and 'brother.' The mistress is called
'sister' by the maid who cooks her dinner, and
affection of the most brotherly character is in-
culcated in every meeting. Strife is denounced,
and arbitration is the prevailing rule. Going in-
to law courts is most severely condemned."
	There are several other items in the letters
we should be pleased to notice but have only
space for one more at this time:
	"I was invited, with my hostess, to a surprise
party given a Mormon lady on her sixty-fourth
blrthday; and truth compels me to say that for
genuine, enjoyable hospitality and good-fellow-
ship, memory holds not its equal for many years
past. The party coming brought the refresh-
ments; every relative was informed, and many
of the fifty-four grandchildren of this 'Mother
in Israel' were on hand. Mrs. M. I. Horne and
her husband sat together, when Mrs. Weils
came forward, and, in a neat address, presented
her a magnificent photographic album, with her
friends' pictures within. Her good and noble
life was referred to with genuine heartiness.
Mrs. Horne responded briefly. One of the
daughters sang, the children joining in the
chorus. Then a lovely little poem, 'To Mother,'
was read by the eldest son. Then an original
song by one of her charity co-workers was sung,
all joining in the chorus. Mayor Jennings and
his agreeable wife, with other leading Mormons,
were present. He gave a toast to the lady; a
thanksgiving prayer was offered. Then came a
variety of refreshing delicacies, all handed
around by the sons, sons-in-law, daughters and
daughters in-law of the jovial but young-look-
ing couple, who looked as if they might travel
some time on the declining hill of life, and en-
joy it too, before they slept the final sleep at its
foot, when the fever of living is over. The
evening was essentially cheerful, but its pre-
vailing feature was its religious character. The
Mormon lives his religion in his daily life. The
graces at table, the reverential deference to
Deity, the thankfulness on all occasions are
something that is too real to be anything save
sincere and heartfelt."
	This highly cultured lady who has traveled
from Maine to Texas with her eyes open, and
visited women of all classes and in all condi-
tions, has not one word to say detrimental to
the Latter-day Saints. She came here to see
and to write, to learn facts as they actually
exist, and every opportunity possible in so short
a time was afforded her, to see and hear for her-
self. If more of those who visit Utah would
take pains to gather facts of their own observa-
tions for material to write about, there would be
much less prejudice against this people outside.
Her description of the Woman's Meeting in
this city was truthful and certainly calculated
to impress all who read it with the earnestness
and zeal of the sisters old and young, and the
effectual exertions made to train up the young
in the ways of the Lord, and to understand
their mission upon the earth, instead of en-
couraging them in the vanities and frivolities of
the world. She speaks of the "Mormon" wo-
men as having ideas of their own and express-
ing them with as much readiness as other
women, and that if they were not honest and
zealous in their religion they would make things
pretty lively for the men who are engaged in it.
Mrs. Saxon knows perfectly well that the ladies
whom she met here are earnest, devout Chris-
tians, and that they are working for a better
condition for humanity, in fact for the redemp-
tion of the human family.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 117


[Column 1]
HOME AFFAIRS.

	THE Unity Club will give an entertainment
on Thursday evening, Jan. 4, for the benefit
of the Deseret Hospital in the 12th Ward School-
house; the performance will commence at 7:30
o'clock. The programme is an excellent one
consisting of music, vocal and instrumental,
recitations and tableaux, historical and myth-
ological. The committee, Mrs. Maggie Jen-
nings, Mrs. Annie W. Cannon, Mrs. Nett Snell,
Miss Prissie Jennings, and Miss Talulah Young,
have labored earnestly, ardently and continuous-
ly to make the affair pleasing and successful.

	THE Primary concert in the 11th Ward, New
Year's night, was a splendid affair. The
chorus by one hundred and twenty-five voices
was well rendered and the exercises were all in
keeping. The fairy play "The Sleeping Beauty"
was a complete success; the costumes were
pretty and the piece was well presented. Mrs.
Louie Felt deserves great credit as also her as-
sistants who aided her in giving so pleasing an
entertainment. The object is a worthy one, to
benefit an institution for the sick and injured.
The programme is to be repeated for the benefit
of the children of the Association, and we hope
the house will be again crowded to its utmost
capacity in favor of the Association.

	THE Thirteenth Ward Sunday School children
had a time of rejoicing on Thursday, Dec. 28th
Great preparations were made by the Superin-
tendent, Wm. Naylor and his assistants, Wm.
Hennefer and Joseph Morris, also the ladies of
the Sunday School of the Ward, Mrs. Elizabeth
H. Goddard, Mrs. Susan Stringam, Misses
Rumel, Reading, and others. Three Christmas
trees were loaded with presents of a very great
variety and beautiful to look upon. The after-
noon was spent in dancing and pleasant pas-
times, and at 4 o'clock the distribution of pres-
ents begun; the were all numbered and a cor-
responding number given to the child that drew
the gift, which was handed down immediately
from the tree, and the recipient made happy.
Three special prizes were awarded for punctuality,
diligence, deportment, attendance and aiding in
the work. The first was awarded to little Clara
Lawson, seven years of age; second, Anna
Stromberg, and third, Lydia Hanks. There
were some exercises in music, singing, and reci-
tation that deserve special notice. Supt. George
Goddard offered prayer, then the hymn "When
Jesus shall come in His glory," was sung; after-
wards recitations, "Elder Orson Pratt, the ven-
erable Apostle," by little Clara Lawson; "The
old Soldier," by Master Thomas Sloan; "Bingen
on the Rhine," Miss Louie Platte; singing,
Miss Annie Peterson, "They'll miss me when
I'm gone;" Miss Anna Stromberg, "Bye and
bye you will forget me." Elder C. R. Savage
came in a few moments and sang "The Merry
Mormons" in his happiest manner; he also pre-
sented a handsome Bible, Sunday School teach-
er's edition, to the Superintendent, Wm. Nay-
lor, who has filled that position nearly seven
years. Brother Goddard sang the "Hazel Dell,"
and six little girls sang together very sweetly.
It was a pleasant interchange of kindly feelings
between old and young as well as a time to be
specially remembered by the little people. There
were over 300 children present, and as gay and
merry as possible. It was certainly quite a suc-
cess.

	ON Thursday, Dec. 28, 1882, a meeting was
called by the Bishop of the 20th Ward, to con-
vene in the schoolhouse at 2 o'clock p.m. Mrs.
E. S. Taylor, President of the Y. L. M. I. A.
Central Board, her counselor, Miss Mattie
Horne, and Mrs. E. B. Wells were present by
invitation, Bishop John Sharp took charge of

[Column 2]
the meeting and effected the following organiza-
tion after some consultation with the ladies
present. Elder L. W. Richards acted as secre-
tary. The following officers were sustained by
unanimous vote: Mrs. Lula Greene Richards,
president; Mrs. Annie G. Sharp and Miss Clara
J. Romney, counselors; Miss Celia Sharp, sec-
retary; Miss Emma Phillips, assistant secre-
tary, and Miss Nettie Sharp, treasurer. The
Bishop gave some excellent advice to the young
sisters, and Mrs. Taylor, Mrs. Miller, Mrs. Wells,
Mrs. Richards and Miss Horne each made some
appropriate and suggestive remarks to the young
people on the subject of their organization.

	THE entertainment arranged for the orphan
children of the 15th Ward on Christmas day,
was appropriate and commendable. To do good
is always in keeping with the example of our
Savior, but to bless and make happy the hearts
of the fatherless is certainly in accordance with
divine precepts. The Christmas trees were
handsomely trimmed and many of the articles
were made by the fair hands of the good sisters
who assisted Mrs. S. M. Kimball, as well as by
herself; she is very ingenious in making arti-
ficial birds, elephants, rabbits, camels, etc. The
trees were lighted up splendidly with petite
wax tapers, and the hall was like a fairy picture.
Presents, useful and ornamental, were distribut-
ed to the little folks and every heart made hap-
py. There were about thirty children present.
They will undoubtedly remember Sister Kim-
ball in after years as a sort of fairy godmother.
She has indeed proved herself a mother in
Israel.


IN MEMORIAM.

ARTEMISIA BEAMAN SNOW.

	It becomes a sad and painful duty to record
the demise of our dearly beloved Sister Arte-
misia, wife of Apostle Erastus Snow, who de-
parted this life at 12:30 a.m., just past midnight,
of the 21st of December, after a lingering illness
through which she evinced a spirit of fortitude
and resignation to the will of heaven, although
she dreaded the pang of death.
	She was the eighth and youngest child of Alva
Beaman and Sarah Burits Beaman, and was
born March 3, 1810, at Livonia, Livingston Co.,
New York, and was the mother of eleven chil-
dren, eight sons and three daughters, all of
whom are living except three. She was bap-
tized into the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-
day Saints at an early date, and with her
father's family removed to Kirtland, Ohio,
where her father died in 1837, a much esteemed
and highly respected member of the Church of
Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. With her
mother and two sisters, Mary and Louisa, she
removed to Missouri, where she was married to
Elder Erastus Snow, at Far West, Dec. 18th,
1838, by the late Heber C. Kimball. Soon after
she, with her husband. moved to Nauvoo,
Illinois, at the time of the expulsion of Saints
from Missouri through the exterminating order
of the notorious and vindictive Governor, Lil-
burn W. Boggs.
	Sister Artemisia was one of those who
officiated in the Nauvoo Temple. She might
truly be termed a wanderer in early life,
being in the persecutions and expulsions of
the Saints from Ohio, Missouri and Illinois.
She was in the first company that left Nauvoo
in February, 1846, crossing the Mississippi on
the ice. She drove a team across the plains in
1818, and came to St. George in 1861. She was
a faithful member of the Relief Society from
its first organization here, and also President of
the Y. L. M. I. Association when first organized
and was much beloved by all with whom she
associated.


Her sister Louisa was the first woman mar-
ried in the Patriarchal order of marriage, being
the second wife of the Prophet Joseph Smith.
Her sister, Mary Adeline Noble, bore the first
child in this order of marriage. Sister Arte-
misia was a firm believer in this order of mar-
riage from its first institution, and freely gave
her consent for her husband to take other wives,
which law he obeyed and she lived and associat-
ed with them through life, and was ever a
mother, a comforter and companion to them;
each one of them bearing testimony to her
merit and worth, and I would refer the readers
of the EXPONENT to an address of her's pub-
lished in the issue of the 15th October last,
which more fully declares her sentiments.
	Thus has another noble spirit departed, one
whom we would gladly have retained in our
midst, and we truly feel the loss of her society,
and while we mourn her loss we testify of her
worth as a true Latter-day Saint. She was ever
genial and kind, a loving mother and generous
friend, kind to the poor and needy, a comforter
to the weary and oppressed. Sisters, be com-
forted; we shall meet her again, if we remain
faithful, in the morning of the first resurrec-
tion.
				FRANCES C. BROWN,
					Stake Secretary R. S.
	ST. GEORGE, Dec. 26, 1882.


118	WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. AND PRIMARY
REPORTS.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Having, in company with Janie Loveland,
Counselor, and Lydia S. Pierce, Secretary, re-
cently returned from a highly interesting tour
through the northern portion of Box Elder
Stake, in the interests of our Y. L. M. I. A's.
I trust that a brief review of our journey and
labors may prove of interest to some of your
numerous readers.
	Bro. Chas. Kelley, Stake President of the Y.
M. M. I. A.'s, with his Counselor, N. Madsen,
also, R. Jones, Secretary, arranged to travel
with us, visiting, at the same time, their asso-
ciations in the settlements lying in the district
above mentioned. Accordingly, appoint-
ments were sent forward some days previous
to our starting.
	The morning fixed for leaving could scarcely
have been more unpropritious; a heavy, furious
snow and wind storm had set in, also, at inter-
vals, accompanied by pelting sleet, the
weather having turned severely cold. Several
of our friends advised us to abandon this un-
dertaking, an idea we could not foster for a
moment, that of allowing our appointments to
fail, while the gentlemen's were being ful-
filled. Therefore, we bundled up, stepped into
our vehicle, placed our feet on warm bricks,
improvised for the occasion, and gathering
more closely our wraps around us, indicated to
our symyathizing coachman that we were
quite ready.
	Having made a run of twenty miles, speak-
ing in railroad parlance, through snow, mud
and driving sleet, we arrived at Bear River
Crossing, stopped at Brother Standings, and
partook of refreshments generously prepared
by his estimable wife.
	We had now another twenty miles before us
ere we reached Portage, where we had an ap-
pointment at seven in the evening, and the
storm, the severe cold, and fierce wind not
abated. Here again was suggested by some of
the gentlemen the propriety of our returning,
but quite failed of eliciting any signs of weak-
ness. When within five miles of our destina-
tion, darkness overtook us, which, together
with the disagreeable circumstances already
mentioned, failed in adding much enchantment

[Column 2]
to the scene connected with this part of our
tour. We reached Portage, however, just in
season to dispel the cold chills, warm up, and
be on time to fill our appointment. We had a
very interesting and profitable meeting, the
Spirit of the Lord being abundantly poured
out both upon speakers and hearers. At the
close of the addresses, we attended to some
matters relating to the more complete organi-
zation of the Young Ladies Association.
	Having been very hospitably entertained,
we proceeded next morning on our way, and
after a drive of about twenty miles, arrived at
Malad City, Idaho, where we held a very in-
teresting meeting with the Y. L. A., number-
ing about seventy-five members, and in a very
prosperous condition. The day following, we
held a conjoint meeting of the members of the
Y. L. and Y. M. M. I. Associations, filled
several vacancies, and effected three new or-
ganizations. Having here finished our inter-
esting labors; we took leave of the hospitable
Bishop and family, together with numerous
warm-hearted friends, and proceeded on to
Samaria, some ten miles distant, still in Idaho.
Here the meetinghouse was filled to overflow-
ing and the Good Spirit prevailed, and all re-
joiced together, visitors and visited, a time
never to be forgotten. Having attended to the
business required, we left this interesting
locality, and turned our faces homeward.
	Our next appointment was looked forward
to with unusual and extraordinary interest, at
the little city of Washikee, a Lamanite settle-
ment of about two hundred inhabitants, located
some three miles south of West Portage, in
Box Elder Co., presided over by Bishop Zun-
del, assisted by Brothers Ward and Hunsaker.
We were informed they had over two thousand
acres enclosed, six or seven hundred under
cultivation, had harvested upwards of three
thousand bushels of grain this season, had
quite a number of cows, a large herd of sheep,
many horses, and were well supplied with
wagons, farming implements, etc. We noticed
several fine brick houses, and were told they
burned their lime, made their brick, and
hauled all their building material themselves.
They have a large commodious frame building
which serves for their schools and meetings; a
very excellent school is now in progress, con-
sisting of thirty-five or forty young Lamanite
girls and boys, under the superintendency of
Brother Chandler, a very efficient and ex-
perienced teacher, who rejoices in this labor
and mission. We were informed that his dusky
pupils compared favorably with our white
children as regards their intellect, aptness and
readiness to acquire book knowledge, etc. We
were joyfully received on our arrival at this
curious and unique gathering, and warmly
greeted by hearty hand shaking, in which all
gladly participated. We gazed with wonder,
with feelings impossible to describe, upon these
Indian Saints as they were gathered and
seated in their assembly hall. The women,
neatly attired in hats, bonnets and shawls,
carrying their babes in their arms instead of
upon their backs, according to their former
custom. The men dressed the same as our
white brethren, with hair cut short, neatly
combed, etc.; and to crown all, making this
scene so impressive and solemn, was the sacred
and earnest devotion impressed upon every
feature of their countenances, while their choir
chanted praises to the Lord in their native
tongue, and one of their number opened the
meeting by prayer in the same language. When
the time was announced for commencing meet-
ing, the Indian Deacon, with becoming gravity,
and a reasonable amount of official importance
and dignity, drew from his breast pocket a
large brass key, with which he ceremoniously
locked the door; then taking the key and re-
placing it, he resumed his seat; arising there-

[Column 3]
after only to quietly admit some tardy member,
or reluctantly allow egress to an anxious appli-
cant. The order and attention observed
throughout the entire services were highly ex-
emplary.
	There was not a sufficient number of young
Lamanite sisters to form an independent or-
ganization, so we concluded to unite them, for
the time being, with the Young Men's Associa-
tion, and appointed a young lady to act as sec-
retary. The good Spirit manifested here,
warmed our hearts, giving utterance to our
thoughts as freely as at any of our previous
meetings, and its sweet and holy influences
were equally felt by the listeners.
	Leaving this singular community of Indian
Saints, formerly known only as savages, we
proceeded to fill our next appointment at Bear
River City, about thirty miles distant, where
we were very kindly received, and addressed a
large and appreciative audience. We were
pleased to find our association here in good con-
dition, spirited, and wide awake to their re-
sponsibilities.
	About three miles from this place we filled
our last engagement at the thriving little town
called Honeyville. We then drove home, ar-
riving in safety, our hearts overflowing with
gratitude to the Giver of All Good, for His
blessings, preserving care, and the aid of His
Holy Spirit accompanying and inspiring our
labors.
				MINNIE J. SNOW,
					Supt. Y. L. M. I. A.
	Brigham City, Dec. 27, 1882.

LOGAN CITY, Nov. 9, 1882.

DEAR SISTER WELLS:
	I send you these few lines to put in your
paper, the WOMAN'S EXPONENT, as I thought
you would like to know how our Primary As-
sociation of the First Ward of Logan is get-
ting along. I am only twelve years old and
am a teacher in the Association, and can tell
you with truth that I do all I can to help my
President, Sister Lundburg, with the Primary.
I sometimes sing and dismiss meeting and bear
my testimony, and with my sister teacher,
Maud Lamoreaux, gather money or calico for
the benefit of our Primary. I think God is
very good to give us health and strength, that
we may perform all the duties that are required
of us; and I know God is good to His chil-
dren, if we obey our parents, say our prayers
and do what is right. And if we read the
Bible and other good books, we will find God's
word is true, what He has said about His com-
ing on the earth to reign with His people.
	Hoping yon will find space in your good
paper, I remain your sister in the Gospel,
					SELINA P. BURTON.

PLAIN CITY. Dec. 18, 1882.

	On Friday, Nov. 24, at ten o'clock a. m.,
our little folks comprising the Plain City Pri-
mary Association assembled at the School
House to hold their Quarterly Conference and
to go through their exercises in singing, recit-
ing, etc. They number one hundred and
thirty.nine members. The meetings are inter-
esting and well attended. The children ac-
quitted themselves during the Conference to
the satisfaction of their parents, teachers and
friends. The pieces in the programme were
well chosen, and of that character that tends
to the elevation and ennobling of the minds of
those who partake of them. The exercises
were brought to a close with some short yet
excellent advice by Bishop L. W. Shurtliff and
Sisters Raymond and Shoemaker; they spoke
very encouragingly, and exhorted the children
to keep on in the good work in which they are
engaged. The little folks then partook of a
picnic, in which they enjoyed themselves ex-


[Column 1]
ceedingly well, after which they spent a few
hours in dancing, retiring to their homes at a
reasonable hour.
			JOSEPHINE CHRISTENSEN, Sec'y.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	We held our Fair on the 24th of September,
and although it was pretty good for such a
small settlement, still it did not quite come up
to our expectations. In our display might be
named, a box of charcoal, burned in the moun-
tains by a very little boy, some very nice
brackets, cornucopias, tidies, a very nice wind
mill by one of the little boys, a very nice
watch pocket done in wool, wool flowers, draw-
ings, lots of things too numerous to mention.
We had a visit from some of the sisters of
Payson and Santaquin, who gave us good in-
struction.
	We also held our annual meeting on the
21st of October, when the little folks were
treated to refreshments. They had some good
instructions from the sisters, after which they
joined in the dance for a short time, when all
went home in the best of humor.
	Wishing the EXPONENT every success, we
remain your sisters in the Gospel,
			SARAH ROBERTS, President.
			CHRISTINA HIGGINSON, Secretary.
	Goshen, Nov. 14, 1882.

WASATCH STAKE.

	Conference of the Relief Society of Wasatch
Stake was held in Heber City Hall, Nov. 16,
1882, Mrs. Emma Brown presiding. On the
platform were the second counselor, Mrs. Mary
Daybell and the presidents of the different
branches in the Stake. Mrs. Zina D. H. Young
and Mrs. E. B. Wells from Salt Lake City,
Bishop Rasband and other brethren. After
usual exercises and reading of minutes Mrs.
Young made a few encouraging remarks, felt
glad to see the sisters coming together to learn
the ways of the Lord. The Wards were then
represented, East Heber by Mrs. Rasband;
West Heber by Mrs. Foreman, Midway, Mrs.
Charlotte Gurney; Charleston, Mrs. Melissa
Murdock; Centre, Mrs. Cluff. Mrs. Wells
made some remarks in regard to the improve-
ment of the sisters who attend their meetings,
spoke of the Deseret Hospital, and made some
explanations in regard to the donations from
the Societies, said there had been a great deal
expended in fitting up the institution to make
it convenient and comfortable for the patients.
She urged the sisters to dovote a potion of time,
if it was only one hour or half hour in a day
to reading good books and cultivating their
minds, and also to read the papers and become
conversant with what was transpiring in the
world, instead of working so hard as she felt
sure many of them did. Knowledge we can
take with us when we go home but not our
worldy goods. Bishop Rasband said he felt
pleased with the meeting and asked the Lord
to bless the sisters who had come to counsel
and instruct the sisters in their duties in this
Stake of Zion. Singing, benediction by Bro.
Todd.
	Afternoon Session: Singing. Prayer by Bro.
Todd, singing. Mrs. Wells made some remarks
upon faith and the peculiar significance of the
hour, and expressed her pleasure at seeing
so many of the brethren attend the Confer-
ence. Mrs. Young spoke of the training
of the children, urged the mothers to keep
them around them and teach them prin-
ciples of the Gospel, and enjoined upon
them to know where their children were at
night. Advised the sisters to read the Book
of Mormon, the book of Doctrine and Coven-
ants, and they would receive spiritual life and
strength therefrom and increase in faith and
be better qualified to rear their children.

[Column 2]
	President Hatch made some interesting re-
marks and suggested that Sister Watson act
temporarily in the place of Sister Muir as the
latter was living at present in Salt Lake City.
President Hatch felt pleased to have the sisters
from Salt Lake come to visit us. Mother
Duke bore a testimony to the truth of the
Gospel. President Brown felt pleased with
the counsel that had been given, and thought
we had enjoyed a good time together. Con-
ference adjourned for three months. Singing
and benediction.
			MARY BRONSON, Sec.
			AVIS M. BRONSON, Ass't Sec.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 119


MARRIED.

	Mr. Samuel G. Spencer, of Pleasant Green,
son of the late Daniel and Mary Jane C. Spen-
cer, and Miss Emma Gedge, of Brighton Ward,
eldest daughter of William and Rachel Gedge,
were joined in. holy wedlock, by counselor
Daniel H. Wells, Thursday, December 21,
1882. They are a very handsome and prom-
ising young couple; and are starting out on
life’s journey very young, with every indication
of a bright and happy future before them. They
celebrated the occassion by giving a party and
supper at the bridegroom’s residence. We join
with their many friends in wishing them long
life, happiness and prosperity.		A. S. H.
	The EXPONENT congratulates the newly
wedded pair Mr. and Mrs. Spencer, and wishes
for them every blessing and experience that
will help them to secure the highest exaltation.


[Column 1]
CORRESPONDENCE.

SAN JUAN RIVER, New Mexico,
				Dec. 20, 1882.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I had fondly hoped to have seen you, with
many of my dear sisters and friends in Utah
ere this, but fate has decided otherwise. I
burried another precious baby boy the eighth
of September last, and on the nineteenth of
the same month I started on a visit to the
North, thinking that the society of friends and
relatives might help to soothe the grief that
seemed to make the future look so dark. My one
little girl was with me, and in company with
some other acquaintances we reached Moab,
Emery Co., where I concluded to remain a few
weeks with a brother. While there the sad
news came to me of the death of Mrs. Matilda
Burnham, who died the 13th of October, six
hours after giving birth to a son, her eleventh
child. She was in the 33d year of her age,
was the wife of Luther C. Burnham and
daughter of John and Eliza Barnett. For
the last five years she, with the rest of the
family, has been laboring as missionaries to
the Navajoes, and by them she was much be-
loved. Previous to her moving to this coun-
try, she resided at Hyde Park, Cache Co., and
was an active and devoted member of the
Relief Society. Her sudden death cast a
gloom over all her friends and especially to
her own family was it sad, to whom she was
a loving wife, a kind mother and sister, and
a pleasant companion and is deeply mourned
for by all who had the pleasure of her ac-
quaintance. After receiving this sad message,
I returned home at once and with the help of
the Lord will do all I am capable of for the
bereaved family.
	There are only eight sisters in this place,
making it very lonely at times. Sister Aba-
gail Stevens is a faithful nurse and a great
help in times of sickness and trouble. Oh!
how often we think of the great and good
times you have at your meetings in Salt Lake,
and so wish for the time to come when we may

[Column 2]
enjoy the blessed influence that ever attends
such gatherings. The sweet spirit there en-
joyed would help to lift us above the cares of
this life. We trust we are not forgotten by
these same dear sisters whom we regard as the
greatest and best women that live upon the
earth. How thankful we should all feel for
the light of the Gospel that all may enjoy if
humble and prayerful, and how can they live
who are not prayerful? I fear we are too care-
less in regard to this. There is no comfort
like the Spirit of God, that will ever abide
with us if we seek faithfully for it. How
much we have to be grateful for that the world
knows nothing of. We have pure and upright
men for husbands and fathers, and we have
perfect confidence in them knowing that the
secret acts of their lives would bring no dis-
grace on us, or our innocent children. Have
the women of the world this confidence in their
"Leige Lords," would to heaven they had;
there would be many an aching heart lighter,
many desolate homes happier, many innocent
girls saved from the terrible fate that awaits
them through the evils of the present genera-
tion. Do we need the pity of the world? For
my part I think it would be better if they
would pity the condition of their own souls,
and try to save them and not worry so much
about the women of the Latter-day Saints,
who are many times better off than they them-
selves are. In conversation not long since
with a Gentile gentleman, he told me that he
had understood that all the wives who entered
a family after the first wife, did so as servants
to the first family. I assured him that the
people called "Mormons," were too intelligent
and independent to be slaves that all wives
were equal, the marriage ceremony being ex-
actly the same for each wife.
	I have been much interested in Mrs. Scott's
letters and pleased that so many of our sisters
reply to them, have often wished I Mas capable
of so doing, but I trust that she with every
honest hearted person may become convinced
of the Truth, for that will bring more peace
and satisfaction than any amount of wealth
and grandeur could do. We are having love-
ly weather now almost like summer. The
merry holidays will soon be here. God grant
that the New Year may be a happy one for
the Saints; and for all who love to do right.
Success to the EXPONENT always. It comes
like a dear old friend and cheers many a lone-
ly home.	MARY STEWART BURNIIAN.

FILLMORE CITY,
	Dec. 12th, 1882.

DEAR MRS. SCOTT:
	As I am a reader of the EXPONENT, have
become very much interested in you also in
your questions and the answers by the Sisters,
on our religious principles I bear my testimony
to you, that they are every word true and em-
anate from the holy laws of God the Eternal
Father.
	I obeyed the Gospel in the Far South at the
early age of 13 years, have never doubted for
one moment since of the truth of this work,
it being 31 years last August since I became a
member of the Chnrch of Jesus Christ of
Latter-day Saints; my husband entered into
the order of Celestial Marriage with my full
and free consent, and I have lived 21 years
in that order of Marriage and I bear my tes-
timony to you that it is true and emanates
from a Superior Power than that of man.
	I do not deny the petty trials of life, those
who are most tried are most triumphant, do
we wish to live here without a trial? then
we would wish to die but half prepared for an
exaltation. We must taste the bitter to appre-
ciate the sweet. Our blessed Lord and Sav-
ior passed through the severest of trials with-
out a murmur, and we must not not expect a


salvation without striving for it, stars shine
brightest in the darkest night, difficulties are
things that show what we are, God has pitted
us against rough antagonists, for what end?
that we may prove Olympic conquerors; and
this cannot be without toil. You say you can-
not accept any religion that will not bear sift-
ing. Our religion will bear sifting, shaking,
turning inside out, there is not one principle
but that can stand the test. Investigate with
pure and honest motives and your harvest will
be rich.
	I believe you to be a woman of true integ-
rity, and will say, Dear Lady, please listen to
and obey the still small voice that whispers to
you from day to day, that the religion so called
"Mormonism" is true; and have a testimony
for yourself and gather to Zion where you can
hear the good word of God proclaimed from
time to time, that will comfort and strengthen
you, and enable you to bear all things patient-
ly. Those who are filled with the love of God
and his laws take no note of petty trials, but
pass them by and say all tend to purify and
sanctify. I bear this my testimony to you with
a loud voice although my body is frail; I have
been an invalid for five years, these are the
sentiments of my heart in, reading your corres-
pondence a great desire came over me to bear
my testimony to you, and hope it will be of
worth. I remain an ernest watcher of your
conversion to the truth.	THE UNKNOWN.


120	WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
MISCELLANEOUS.

	The sword is but a hideous flash in the
darkness—right is an eternal ray.—Victor
Hugo.

	A good conscience is the palace of Christ;
the temple of the Holy Ghost; the paradise
delight; the standing Sabbath of the saints.—
Augustine.

	In Washington, D. C., Dec. 8, 1882, Mr.
Bingham, of Pennsylvania, chairman of the
committee on postoffices and postcards, report-
ed a bill in the House reducing letter postage
to two cents, and it was referred to the com-
mittee of the whole.

	Dr. Marie Zakrzewska, at the recent Wo-
man's Congess in Portland, spoke first of con-
vent life and its effect upon the health. Nuns
live the quietest and most retired life, all their
time engaged in occupations entirely feminine.
But they fade and grow sick and old sooner
than if they were more actively engaged. Di-
ametrically opposed to these are the fashiona-
ble women whose existence is one round of
gaiety and excitement. They fade and wear
away almost as soon as the religious devotees.
The women who are engaged in industrial oc-
cupations do not have a long lease of life and
health. The settled and unvarying hours of
labor, the close confinement, the noise and con-
fusion of the machinery to which they are con-
stantly subjected, the continued absorption of
interest in one direction—all combine to sap
their bloom and strength. In professional life,
Dr. Zakrzewska placed all women engaged in
any kind of active work which calls for the
exercise of all the faculties. She would have
it include not only physicians, ministers, law-
yers, and all who are usually classed as profes-
sionals, but also nurses, housekeepers, book-
keepers, all who have a definite occupation re-
quiring knowledge and skill and calling into
play more than one set of faculties. These, she
said,are the healthiest women. They stay young
and fresh longer than any other class, and as
they grow old are better preserved, both men-
tally and physically. The health of women
generally is much better now than in former
days, but there is still much that needs to be
remedied,—Ex.

[Column 2]
	O flattery, how potent is thy sway; how wide
the bounds of thy pleasing jurisdiction !—Cer-
vantes.

	Books, are the true levellers. They give to
all who faithfully use them the society, the
spiritual presence of the greatest and best of
our race.—Channing.

	A Boston paper is in "favor of women
voting if they want to." A Western paper
"would like to see the man who would make
them vote if they did'nt want to."

	Helen M. Gougar, editor of Our Herald, has
sued Harry Mandler, chief of police, and Chas.
Pook, for libel, and claims damages in $10,000.
This is a straightforward and eminently proper
course to be taken by an injured party.

	It is only a step from the sublime to the ri-
diculous. We read that "Madam Adelina
Patti was invested with the Order of Kapiola-
ni, recently, by the Chamberlain of King
Kalakaua. The order is a star on a red and
white striped ribbon, and was accompanied by
a letter on parchment from His Majesty."
What an honor.

	The rights of woman are obtaining recogni-
tion in Norway. The parliament has just
passed a law, which the King has signed, giv-
ing women the privilege of attending the uni-
versities and applying for degrees in all the
arts and sciences; but the right to hold office
in the service of the state and of participating
in the competitive examinations for such posi-
tions is not yet accorded them.

	A meeting of the executive committee of the
fair for aged and friendless women was held
recently at the house of the president, Mrs. Dr.
Carelton, who presided. Accounts were ren-
dered in from the various tables and other
connections of the fair, sufficient to make it
evident that the net receipts will exceed $2,-
000. A vote of thanks to the president was
enthusiastically passed.

	MADAME SARAH BERNHARDT'S engagement
for South America, the preliminary details of
which the Figaro was the first to announce two
months ago, is, I understand, not yet quite a
fait accompli. Certain details have yet to be
settled. Still the contract, onerous as it is, is
more workable than the first proposition by
which Madame Bernhardt wished to be paid
£300 a night even while she was traveling
from place to place. The new contract is, it is
understood, for £33,000 for fifty performances,
the services of M. Damala being included. This
is at rate of £660 a night, certainly a larger
sum than has ever before been paid to any
dramatic artist. Should Madame Bernhardt
go to South America, she will be absent from
April to September, and will compensate Mr.
Mayer for the loss of his London season at the
Gaiety.—Ex.

	An old Quaker lady of Nantucket, in a
private letter to Dr. Sarah J. McNutt, of New
York, gives a number of curious items from
the genealogy of the island. One of these we
are permitted to quote: "Rachel Bunker, wi-
dow of Dan (?) Bunker, and previously widow
of Barnabas Coleman, and daughter of Sylva-
nus and Abiel Hussey, died 11th mo., 9th,
1796, after an illness of 48 hours; aged 80
years, 6 mo., 23 days. During her time she
discovered much Christian fortitude and resig-
nation, and closed a life of usefulness with
calmness and serenity. She has been for 33
years eminent and successful in her profession,
having in that time assisted at the birth of
2,992 children (30 pairs of twins). She had 12
children, 113 grandchildren and 94 great-
grandchildren." We commend this case to the
attention of those who think professional life
incompatible with maternity.—Ex.

[Column 3]
	The Utah Commission, in a report to the
Secretary of the Interior, recommends that the
women of Utah be deprived of the ballot as a
means of suppressing polygamy. The most in-
telligent women of the civilized world will re-
commend that the present commission to Utah
be supplanted by men of common sense and
ordinary honesty.—Denver Queen Bee.

Men's Socks, Ladies' and Childrens' Stockings, Infants

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.

VOL. 11	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JANUARY 15, 1883.	No. 16


HELEN AND VIRGINIA. 

My friend, when once you asked of me
My reason for the truth I bear,
I answered briefly, for my gift
Is not in language as with men
Accustomed through their lives to share
Conflicts in pulpit or by pen. 

And so unsatisfied, I turn
To answer further one who seems
Inclined impartially to hear
The weaker side, and to receive
Such truth therein she may discern
As by our Church taught and believed.

It has been argued, theories,
However plausible to view,
Fail when brought into daily life,
And our's requirements and restraints
Are, while its precepts we pursue,
Beyond the strength of even Saints.

And that 'tis better far to keep
The "golden rule" on every hand,
Leaving the rest to our dear Lord,
Avoiding thus all cause of strife,
Attaining so through all our land
An onward march of peaceful life.

For us who know we have been called
To rise and work, not sit and wait,
Such well meant counsel does not fit
Our cause and time on this world's stage;
The field is wide, the hour is late,
And none must loiter who engage.

For in the kingdom there's a place,
A name and work for every one,
According to the talent lent
By the great Master over all,
Who notes the humblest service done
In answer to His holy call.

So if I answer you by way
Of narrative culled from the lives
Of those whom I have known for years,
Accept the picture, you will see
Of "Mormon" maidens, mothers, wives,
In proof of what we claim to be.

Not seed of promise, bud or bloom,
Taken with hopes and anxious fears,
Lest it prove thorned and barren tree,
And life's long labor spent in vain;
This is the fruit, after long years,
The fruit we've wrought in faith to gain.

Not that we lacked the "corner stone,"
Whereby to build, or rule to guide;
Each spirit knew by inward sight
The system's truth, its course and goal;
And gathered close or scattered wide.
Labored as one harmonious whole.

If in this garden you should find
Fruit all unworthy of its name,
Remember, e'en in Eden grew
A thing of evil, bringing sin,
Where all was pure; nor choose to blame
All, for the few tares found therein.

This is our work: to root from earth
Evils that have for ages spread;
And not by words alone, by works
That shall be felt the whole world through,
Till vanquished sin with bending head
Yields unto Truth the honor due.

And who will do this mighty work?
Not men of war in war's array,
But gentler ones who armor bear
Against all selfishness of heart;
Of these I weave my rhyme to-day—
Women who bear an honored part.

				AUGUSTA JOYCE C[-]OC[-]ERON.


[Column 1]
﻿DESULTORY MEMORIES.

	Every reflective person has times of medita-
tion. These may occur from some event that
transpires, or peculiar frame of mind in part
unaccountable, or from certain surroundings.
Floods of remembrances rush in as it were un-
bidden, and burst asunder the barriers time
has thrown up between us and the scenes of
earlier life; all at once rivers of thought flow
over the soul and almost bury the present for
the time being, so absorbing, is the emotion,
carrying us back to other days. Vividly the
galleries in memory's hall stand out in bold re-
lief, and we recognise here and there the
familiar faces and forms of those we loved,
and recall the associations connected with them
which had been almost forgotten, in the whirl-
pool of busy everyday life, that swallows up
so completely our thoughts and energies, while
actively engaged in the occpations that bring
us in close contact with the people among whom
we live. It is right to a certain extent to be
thus engaged in the present, and in looking
forward to the future; and yet it is well to
pause now and then in retrospective reverie
for the mind needs rest and recreation.
	The present demands attention, the future
is calling for action. These are facts with
which we are all acquainted, but to revel occa-
sionally in the realms of the past, is oft-times
like sweet music heard in dreams, that thrills
the senses when we awaken, and imparts pleas-
urable sensations, that word scan never express.
There is a pathos in memory hallowed and
sanctified, that is always elevating; and to be
able to call up from within, pictures
taken from incidents that actually transpired,
is better than to create air castles for the fu-
ture, that are not possible of fulfillment. If
you ask me why? I answer because it cultivates
the heart. Only those who are capable of in-
tense affection, the highest enjoyment, or the
keenest suffering, can fully appreciate what it
is to traverse memory's halls and search out
the beauty of that which has departed.
	To cultivate the graces of human life and
bring into exercise the finest faculties we pos-
sess, is to breathe a higher atmosphere, which
will develop the broadest phase of character,
that will search out the truth and the light,
and find a mantle of charity to cover the weak-
nesses of those less favored with conditions to
promote advancement. It is from women more
especially that this refinement of culture is ex-
pected. Her gentleness, her amiability, her
intuition are all aids and helps in softening,
subduing and polishing the manners, the cus-
toms and the hearts of the masses of the peo-
ple in the world we inhabit.
	No festival or entertainment is ever perfect
without woman's help; it is her prerogative to
embellish, adorn and beautify, and in all the
pictures memory portrays, she is ever a promi-
nent feature. The mother, the sister, the
friend, the one who could enter into all the
griefs of our childhood, was a woman. Yet
she is not perfect in and of herself, but associa-
ted with man, the two forming one perfect
whole, how complete do wo find the arrange-
ment of the divine Creator.
	The departure of the old year and the usher-
ing in of the new, is apt to awaken some of
those tender phases of feeling that are spoken
of as sentimentality, often in a careless manner;
but there is always a lesson in these recur-
rences, and there are those who mark such

[Column 2]
days as epochs in their lives. It matters not
what calls forth tenderness, it is always a bless-
ing. To be indifferent and callous to all things,
is to mar instead of make character; not that
we should always be carried away by our
excess of sysmpathy, or deceive ourselves with
superabundance of fine feeling without judg-
ment, or principle; but to be kind, gentle, ten-
der and considerate, and educate the heart and
affections epually with the head and hands.
	It is well to reason and to be practical, it is
commendable to dig deep for scientific know-
ledge, but there must be a balance of spiritual
force and human affection or we should have a
race of sages and savantes,without that love and
reverence that purifies and sanctifies joys and
sorrows, and draws mankind closer together in
bonds of universal brotherhood. Then let us
keep up the old-time customs that encourage
coming together and exchanging courtesies and
kindnesses, that our children may look back to
the holidays and annual festivities with as
much pleasure and satisfaction, as we do now
to the good old times when we were young and
all things seemed gilded with brightest hopes
in rainbow hues. How fondly we recall child-
hood's memories; the old homestead, the mea-
dows and groves, the singing birds, the swal-
low's twitter, the lark's high note, the mourn-
ful whippoorwill; the speckled trout in the
little brook near the old home, that sailed
along the babbling water. Was anything ever
so tasty as these little fish? the berries with
which our fingers were often stained, and the
autumn nuts, gathered in the dear old woods
and stowed away in the attics and garrets, to
be brought out in the winter evenings when
the work was all put aside and we ate apples,
cracked nuts, and, sometimes, though perhaps
we ought not to tell it, the old folks drank sweet
cider. Can we ever forget these creature com-
forts? No! Neither should we, they help
make up the sum of human happiness, and we
are better, if not wiser, for such pastimes and
enjoyments around the fireside with those, who
love us and can appreciate our feelings. To
eat together cements friendship—and the cus-
tom of inviting our friends to partake our
hospitality in the good old-fashioned way,
without too much style and ceremony, should
not be cast aside for new-fangled notions of
formal calls and fashionable dinner parties.
								AUNT EM.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

	I have taken much interest in questions
asked by Mrs. Scott, and she seems to me a
dear friend, although I have never seen her
face or heard her voice. I see from her letters
that her mind has been exercised in many
instances the same as my own was once.
I have been waiting to learn what the sisters
who answer would say to her, for they some-
times express my feelings better than I can do
myself. She asks what the Commissioners did?
Well, I consider they did well. They did what
the Lord permitted, and as his hand is over all
his works, we have nothing to fear, but trust
implicitly in Him. Those men and women that
were not allowed to vote because they had ac-
cepted the doctrine of plural marriage, know,
that they are excused because of having ac-
cepted a higher law. Yet we had four thous-
and votes more than the previous year. They
were more honorable than the Governor, be-


cause they gave the certificate to the person
who had the largest number of votes. Was
not this honorable? Should we not praise the
bridge which carries us safe over? They would
have done more had they known that this was
the work of God our Father. They shall have
my prayers that they may at some future day
know this to be a fact. I fancy I see Mrs.
Scott and her family, sitting around the table
seeking intelligence; one of them reading the
problem of life. How important, because this
is a duty we owe to ourselves and our Maker.
I wish Mrs. Scott could read Mr. Charles W.
Penrose's remarks upon this subject. I thought
of her last evening while listening to the re-
marks of Prof. T. B. Lewis, the able Superin-
tendent of all the schools in this county. He
was speaking of the importance of informing
our minds upon subjects pertaining to this
earth, because the Creator of heaven and earth
was the Father of all our spirits. He thought
a son was not dutiful if he neglected to make
himself acquainted with the works of his
father. Yet said, our education would not pro-
cure salvation, but our characters would. He
related a conversation with the brother of a
young man who had been on a mission, who said
his brother when in England, wrote very en-
thusiastic letters, full of light and intelligence,
but since he returned home, became darkened
in his mind and "now has no religion in him."
To illustrate this, Prof. Lewis likened it to a
man who went up stream to visit a person who
lived five miles at the head of the stream.
Said the man rowed the boat until he came
just near the house, when he became tired and
threw down his oars, saying, "I am so near I
will rest, I shall soon be there." But the cur-
rent took him back even beyond the starting
point. He then showed that application was
necessary to success. He also compared it to
the study of music. What great attainments
are made by application to that study? To
prepare for a concert, how often it is necessary
to practice in order to become proficient.
Argueing that those who had recieved the gift
of the good Spirit, had obtained it by seeking
diligently and applying their whole soul to the
rules that our Savior has given us to live by.
When we do this, great are the results. Yes,
the Scripture says, they who keep those laws
as he the Savior kept them, shall be heirs and
joint heirs with Jesus Christ. Not only so,
but possess a joy and peace the world can
neither give or take from them. Those who
adhere to the principles he taught, have the light
which St. John speaks of as being the true
light, which lighteth every man who cometh
into the world. With this light, how clear, how
plainly we can see and comprehend all that we
touch and handle. We will not fail to be suc-
cessful, for we are constantly improving. The
Savior said it would be like a well of water
springing up into everlasting life. This is our
experience. If Mrs. Scott will try it in the
sincerity of her heart, her experience will be
the same. The Savior was called faithful and
true. If we attain to perfection, we must be
like Him.			M. E. KIMBALL.


122	﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
OUR TRIALS.

	The Lord says He will have a tried people,
and if it is his desire, and he permits us to be
tried, it proves it is for our good. We are to
be tried to see if we are able to stand the temp-
tations of the evil one; our Savior was temp-
ted, but he said, "get thee behind me, Satan,"
and if we are in the line of our duty, we also
will have power given us to rebuke the evil
one in the name of Jesus, that name which,
when uttered, will cause, as the poet exclaims,
"the devils to fear and flee away." But as we
must he tried in order to be God's children we
must pass through perhaps bodily affliction,
and how many of us under such circumstances

[Column 2]
have felt to exclaim, "It has been good for me
to be afflicted;" then is the time we more es-
pecially are free from the cares of life, and
although our bodies perhaps are suffering great
pain and weakness, our minds are strong, our
spirits buoyant, and we hold sweet communion
with our Father, his spirit whispers comfort
and peace, and fills our hearts with joy unutter-
able, and full of glory, so that we have cause
for gratitude, and praise God that we have
been afflicted; we realize that some of the dross
has been consumed and we are enabled to go
on our way rejoicing. But some persons will
say, I am so tried, I cannot, and will sometimes
go so far as to say they will not bear it. Stop,
my dear sisters, before you utter these words,
and reflect for one moment where these thoughts
come from, and from what source they spring.
	We are told the heart is deceitful, and des-
perately wicked, and so long as we feel in that
state of mind, we dare venture to say there is
something of that kind burning within. What
is it you cannot bear? May be it is the very
thing the Lord intends to purify and bless you
with, perhaps it may be the principle of plural
marriage, which, when practised in purity and
righteousness, will ennoble woman in this life,
and eventually exalt her in the Celestial King-
dom of our Father and God.
	Shall we, as women of Zion, we who have
embraced the everlasting Gospel, and have
tested of the good things of the kingdom,
perhaps enjoyed some of the gifts therein, shall
we lift our puny arm against the Almighty,
or any of the principles which have been re-
vealed! nay verily! Let us not be guilty of
such an act, but let us rather rejoice to have
husbands worthy of more than one wife, and if
we will do so, our Father in heaven will be well
pleased with us, and we will have more joy
than our hearts had ever conceived of.
	Again, some who have come to Zion have
left good homes and every worldly comfort.
What induced them to do so? was it for the
Gospel's sake, or had they any other motive in
view? If the former, they will be satisfied
when they arrive here. They may be tried in
many, various ways, but they are blest, and
their way is opened. On the contrary, if we
come with other motives than to serve God,
keep his commandments, and help build up
Zion, we shall get discouraged and dissatisfied,
and say things are not what they were repre-
sented to be, then commence to be dissatisfied
with everything, and everybody, and especially
with those who preside over the Church, which,
by the bye, is the first step to apostacy; then
our minds become dark, and eventually such
persons will leave the Church. Such are they
who cannot stand the trials, and are neces-
sarily not of those whom the Lord will have;
and inasmuch as they are not willing to be sub-
ject to his laws, will have to be subject to him
whom they list to obey, who will verily prove
to be a hard master and give poor pay. Then
in consideration of these things, let us endeavor
each day, to "prove all things, and hold fast
that which is good," and patiently endure all
the Lord sees fit we should pass through. Let
us pray for strength to enable us to do God's
righteous will, and all his laws obey; Seek
him with all our might, we then will know,
and prove what is true. We must our trials all
pass through to gain celestial glory, and we
will then realize 'twas good we had "Our
Trials."
	LEHI, 1883.				E. R, C.


﻿PRIMARY FAIR.

	A Primary Fair was held in Snowflake, Dec.
20th, 1882, under the auspices of the President,
Sister Emma S. Smith and Counselors, and
the President of the Relief Society, Sister M.
J. West.
	The fair was opened at 10 a. m. by


singing and prayer. There was a general
attendance of the people from Snowflake
and Taylor. The children showed con-
siderable energy and skill in preparing
articles for the occasion. One little boy
eight years old, made and painted a garden
rake, a little boy, nine, made a harrow, a little
girl, four, made a quilt block, a little girl, five,
a sweetcake, a little boy, seven, an ox yoke, a
little girl, nine, a basket covered with beads,
a little boy, eleven, made and painted an
armed rocking chair, he also made the paint
brush that he used; a little girl, five, covered a
cross with shells, and made a wreath of flowers,
a little boy, eleven, made a spring wagon with
cover and spring seat, a girl, eleven, braided
straw for a hat, a girl, fourteen, cut and made
her father a coat; nine pairs of stockings were
made by girls from eight to twelve years old.
	The Primary girls also pieced blocks for
both sides of a quilt, set it together, carded the
wool, and quilted most of it. They had bread
and a variety of cakes and pies; ornaments of
cardboard, wool flowers, cushions, crochet work
and embroidery, besides many other articles
that were very creditable. The boys also had
many articles very creditable,
	The afternoon was spent in recitations from
cards of our faith, speaking pieces and singing
songs. Closed by complimentary speeches
from President Jesse N. Smith and Bishop
Hunt.
			ELLEN J. LARSON, Secretary.


﻿THE GRANDEUR OF WOMAN.

	When you want to get the grandest idea of
a queen, you do not think of Catherine of
Russia, or of Anne of England, or of Marie
Theresa of Germany; but when you want to
get your grandest idea of a queen, you think
of the plain woman who sat opposite your
father at the table, or walked with him arm-in-
arm down life's pathway; sometimes to the
thanksgiving banquet, sometimes to the grave,
but always together, soothing your petty griefs,
correcting your childish waywardness, joining
in your infantile sports, listening to your even-
ing prayers, toiling for you at needle or at the
spinning wheel, and on cold nights, wrapping
you up snug and warm. And then, at last, on
that day when she lay in the back room dying,
and you saw her take those thin hands, with
which she had toiled for you so long, and put
them together in a dying prayer that commend-
ed you to the God whom she had taught you to
trust—oh she was the queen! The chariots of
God went down to fetch her; and as she went
in, all heaven rose up. You cannot think of
her now without a rush of tenderness that stirs
the deep foundation of your soul, and you feel
as much a child again as when you cried on
her lap; and if you could bring her back again
to speak just once more your name as tenderly
as she used to speak it, you would be willing to
throw yourself on the ground and kiss the sod
that covers her, crying: "Mother! mother!"
Ah, she was the queen! To make one such
woman as I have described, how many thous-
ands would you want of those people who go
in the round of fashion and dissipation, dis-
tressing their body until in their monstrosities
they seem to outdo the dromedary and hippo-
potamus; going as far toward disgraceful ap-
parel as they dare go, so as not to be arrested
by the police, their behavior a sorrow to the
good and a caricature to the vicious, and an
insult to that God who made them women and
not gorgons; and tramping on down through a
frivolous, dissipated life to temporal and
eternal damnation.—Frank Leslie's Sunday
Magazine.


"One soweth and another reapeth," is a ver-
ity that applies to evil as well as good.—
George Eliot.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 123


A SERMON IN RHYME.

SELECTED.

If you have a friend worth loving,
	Love him. Yes, and let him know
That you love him, ere life’s evening
	Tinge his brow with sunset glow.
Why should good words ne'er be said 
Of a friend—till he is dead.

If you hear a song that thrills you,
	Sung by any child of song,
Praise it. Do not let the singer
	Wait deserved praises long.
Why should one who thrills your heart
Lack the joy you may impart?

If you hear a prayer that moves you
	By its humble, pleading tone,
[---] Do not let the seeker
	Bow before his God alone.
Why should not your brother share
The strength of "two or three" in prayer?

If you see the hot tears falling
From a sorrowing brother's eyes,
Share them. And, by sharing,
Own your kindship with the skies.
Why should any one be glad
When a brother's heart is sad?

If a silvery laugh is rippling
Through the sunshine on his face,
Share it. 'Tis te wise man's saying--
For both grief and joy a place.
There's health and goodness in the mirth
In which an honest laugh has birth.

If your work is made more easy
By a friendly helping hand,
Say so. Speak out brave and truly,
Ere the darkness veil the land. 
Should a brother workman dear
Falter for a word of cheer.

Scatter thus your seeds of kindness 
All enriching as you go;
Leave them. Trust the Harvest Giver,
He will make each seed to grow;
So until its happy end,
Your life shall never lack a friend.


[Column 1]
A VENERABLE WOMAN.
PRESENDIA LATHROP KIMBALL.

	It is a singular fact, and one that any
thoughtful person cannot avoid noticing, that
many of the men and women who first em-
braced the Gospel in this dispensation, were the
direct descendants of those who fled from per-
secution in the old world, to find an asylum in
the new, where they could worship God accord-
ing to the dictates of their own consciences.
And how similar has been the history of this
people to that of the Pilgrim fathers and
mothers. Among this number is the lady who
forms the subject of this sketch. Her gene-
ological record on the father's side (Huntington
was her family name), has been traced to
Simon Huntington, the Puritan immigrant
who sailed from England in 1833, but died at
sea, leaving a widow,Margaret Huntington and
cthree sons. Of Mrs. Kimball's illustrious and
estors was Samuel Huntington, one of the
signers of the Declaration of Independence,
Governor of Connecticut, and President of
the Continental Congress. Wm. Hunting-
ton, grandfather of Mrs. Kimball, and
nephew of Samuel Huntington, was born
in Tolland, Connecticut, and served in the
army through the Revolutionary war. He
moved to New Hampshire in 1784, and
there Mrs. Kimball's father, Wm. Hunt-
ington, was born; he also served in the war
with Great Britain in 1812. In 1804, the
Huntington's removed to Watertown, New
York, and there Presendia Lathrop was born,
also her sister Zina D., whose biography we
published last year in the EXPONENT. These

[Column 2]
two women are descended from a race of patri-
ots, and it is not strange that a generous share
of heroism has descended to them, and that it
very strongly marks the character of the fami-
ly. The Huntington families are intermarried
with the Lathrops, from whom Presendia was
named, and this family was also among
the early New England settlers, one of
whom was the Reverend Samuel Lathrop,
who, for non-conformity, being a preacher
in the First Congregational Church in
London, was imprisoned for two years, and
on being released in 1634, came to America
and was the first minister of Scituate,
Massachusetts.
	Mrs. Kimball's mother was Zina Baker
whose father was one of the first physicians in
New Hampshire, and whose mother descended
from the Dymock's of Sir Edward Dymock,
Queen Elizabeth's champion. Presendia was
the fourth child of her parents, and was born
in Watertown, Jefferson Co., N. Y., September
10, 1810. Mrs. Kimball is said to be the exact
counterpart of the Eliza Huntington whose
likeness is in the book, the record of the Hunt-
ington's, as a type of the race. Sister Presendia
is a woman to see once is to remember always.
She reminds one of the dames of the olden
times, large, tall, grand and majestic in figure,
dignified in manner, yet withal, so womanly
and sympathetic that she seems the embodi-
ment of the motherly element to a degree that
would embrace all who come under her influ-
ence. Truly she may, in every sense of the
word, be termed a mother in Israel in very
deed. Her life has been exceptionally event-
ful. It is impossible to give in a brief sketch
like this even a tithe of her wonderful experi-
ences, but a few items will convey to the reader
something of the life of this good woman, so
rich in faith, so full of integrity to the Gospel,
and will perhaps be beneficial to others, and show
of what material have been the women who
have been foremost in helping to establish the
principles that have called forth so much com-
ment throughout the civilized world. It takes
women of more than ordinary stamina to en-
dure all the stigma, reproach and calumny
that is sure to follow the introduction of any
new ideas, even though they may be for the
progression and advancement of the human
race here and hereafter. The father and
mother of Sister Presendia both became mem-
bers of the Church at a very early date, they
had been Presbyterians of the strictest kind.
	The Huntington's with their family, contrary
to the wishes of their relatives and friends,
shortly after embracing the Gospel as taught
by the Prophet Jeseph, removed to Kirtland,
Ohio, where the saints were gathering together
and assisted in building the Temple in that
place, which is still standing as a testimony to
all the faithful labors of the people of God,
in erecting Temples to the Most High in the
face of the bitterest persecutions. At the time
of the gathering to Missouri, Father and
Mother Hnntington went up to Jackson
County, and shared in the hardships and perils
of that eventful epoch in the history of the
Church. The hardships endured there by
these veterans are too dreadful to contemplate,
and eventually both of them laid down their
lives in consequence of the privations and per-
secutions to which they had been so cruelly
subjected. Faithful and true to the very last
hour of their lives, were these noble saints of
the Most High; descended from those who had
battled for freedom from oppression and nobly
earned the title of freemen for themselves and
their posterity; yet for the self-same reason,
their religious faith compelled them to pass
through the same series of trying ordeals, un-
til death came to their relief. Who shall an-
swer for the noble lives thus sacrificed? Is
there not an avenger of the innocent and suf-
fering of the Saints? Yet these things trans-

[Column 3]
pired under the broad spread folds of the flag
of liberty, freedom to all, religious liberty to
every creed and color. Consider too this was
not on account of the practice of plural mar-
riage, there could be no such excuse then, for
the principle had not yet been revealed.

To be Continued.


N. W. S. A., WASHINGTON, D. C.

	The National Woman Suffrage Association
true to the principles they profess, have re-
solved to use their influence to defeat Cassidy's
Bill introduced into the Congress of the United
States, with the object of annulling the act of
the Utah Legislature of 1870, in passing the
bill granting suffrage to the women of this
Territory. At a preliminary meeting held in
the Riggs House Parlors, Washington, D. C.,
Saturday evening, Dec. 30, the following reso-
lution was adopted:
	"Resolved, That the bill introduced into the
House of Representatives by the Hon. G. W.
Cassidy, of Nevada, to deprive the women of
Utah of the ballot is a retrograde movement,
and aimed at the perpetual disfranchisement
of all women, notwithstanding his assertions
to the contrary, as women who are polygamists
cannot vote there now; and that we will
use whatever power we possess to defeat that
measure."
	Another resolution was adopted at the same
meeting in reference to the cruel and wicked
persecution of Mrs. Helen M, Gougar, editor
of "Our Herald," a woman's paper published
and very ably conducted by that estimable
lady, who on account of her bold and fearless
speaking and writing against wickedness and
crime has been most cruelly slandered. Mrs.
Gougar has brought suit for slander against
the men who so basely traduced her character.
The following is the resolution:
	"Resolved, That the Washington branch of the
National Woman's Suffrage Association extend
to Mrs. Helen M. Gougar their moral support
in her suit, now pending against calumny and
detraction, the weapons only of cowards; and
that we consider her cause the cause of all
women."
	We heartily endorse the sentiment expressed
in the resolution, and hope Mrs. Gougar may
come off triumphant in the suit now pending.
Mrs. G. says in her paper of Dec. 9:
	"The slanderer and his attorney have raked
around among our former servant girls and
sewing women; have searched hotel registers to
find our "character" but they have studiously
avoided calling in the hundreds of Christian
homes where we have been hospitably enter-
tained from time to time."
	With all the wicked and vile things that
have been circulated about the suffrage work-
ers, this is the first instance we have heard of
when suit has been brought, and the ladies who
believe in "equality of women before the law,"
will watch the proceedings of the court in this
matter with anxiety and intense interest. Mrs.
G. is a handsome woman, a fine figure, most
pleasing address, brilliant writer and a great
favorite on the platform. A woman to be
loved and respected.


On the whole, it is good, it is absolutely
needful, for one to be humbled and prostrated,
and thrown among the pots from time to time.
Life is a school; we are perverse scholars to
the last, and require the rod.—Thomas Carlyle.

	At the last meeting of the "Woman's Liter-
ary Club" of Indianapolis, an exceedingly
well-prepared paper upon the life and charac-
ter of Margaret Fuller was read by Mrs. May
Wright Sewall.
