WOMAN's EXPONENT.
	The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.
Vol. 11.			SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, MARCH 15, 1883.			No. 20.


ADDRESSED
To Mrs. E.R. Snow Smith:
	In a quiet spot ’neath a cedar tree,
	Is a little grave very dear to me;
	A darling, my baby, lies buried there,
	Safe from temptation, and free from care.
	To-day is his birthday and yours, dear friend,
	And tenderest greeting to you I send;
	A sweet, sacred tie it seemeth to be,—
	That grave, this birthday, 'tween you and me.
	
	In his life’s early dawn he passed away,—
	Your life has been one long busy day;
	Why, wherefore, no mortal tongue can tell;
	God willed it so, He doth all things well.
	Time hath chastened my grief for the absent one
	In submission I say, Thy will be done.
	Dear friend upon this day,
	This milestone on thy way
	I've stopped awhile, ’midst work and care;
	Your history to read. Its pages fair
	Abound in noble deeds,
	From morn, through noon, still busy sowing seeds
	Of good, which will, and do bring forth 
	A harvest rich of love and faith.
	Methinks I hear recording angels say:
	"Another year of noble work to-day;”
	
	Could I but near you stand,
	See your face, clasp your hand,
	And say: “God bless her,—bless this, her birthday,
	And may it bring her happiness alway:”
	My heart would thrill with joy
	God grant we meet where bliss hath no alloy.
	A crown awaits you there, angels will sing
	Their sweetest song for your glad welcoming;
	For many souls can now more hopeful be
	Of heaven, for your faithful ministry.
	
	And I, in daily life
	As woman, mother, wife,
	Will set my standard high, as you have done,
	And nobly, truly work, till victory's won
	You have not lived in vain,
	Your life hath given joy, and lessened sin and pain.
	Your light doth shine brightly afar,
	Leading Jesus, as did the star
	Of Bethlehem. Faithful work, and noble aim!
	All womankind may love and bless your name.

	Accept this tribute of respect and love;
	We may not meet on earth, we'll meet above,
	That little grave shall as an altar be
	For prayer, and incense to your memory.
	When flowers bloom thereon, their fragrant breath
	Will tell of joy and heaven–after death;
	That winter's cold, like worldly strife,
	Is but the preface of a brighter life.
	And when the year goes round and brings this day,
	Beside that grave I'll think of you alway
						EMILY SCOTT.
Ohio, Jan. 21, 1883.


ANSWERED.

DEAR MRS. EMILY SCOTT:
	The grave—where'er that grave may be,
	Which forms a link 'twixt you and me,
	Will burst asunder and resign
	Its trust.
	Whose arms will then entwine
	The precious darling baby who
	Has been, and is, so dear to you?

		All things above and all below,
	Are bound by law; it being so,
	A law of God requires of you
	An act performed—a work to do,
	By which, for true affections aim,
	You may secure a legal claim,
	That you may clasp that baby dear,
	And into perfect stature rear


Its little form, in purity,
And glorious immortality,
Stern death will sever every tie
That is not based above the sky.

	Dear lady, I now testify
God has revealed a law whereby
The dearest of all earthly ties,
We may securely legalize;
To have—to hold—to enjoy forever,
Beyond the power of death to sever.

	Sec to it lady—'tis of more
Intrinsic wealth than golden ore—
Comprising more true, real worth
Than all the precious things of earth
Which perish with their use—'tis more
Of value than all worldly lore.

	Thanks for the kindly feelings you express:
My heart reciprocates with friendliness.
Methinks I see you sitting by that grave,
Absorbed in thought as deep as ocean's wave.
While there alone enrapt'd in reverie,
How strange that you, a stranger, thought of me!
Yet, if the curtains of the past were drawn,
And we our pre-existence gazed upon,
Perhaps life's contest fully might explain
We once were friends, and now are friends again.

	Important movements have head quarters where
The greater gifts and privileges are.
Here are the Temples of the living God—
From here His mighty work goes forth abroad.
Then leave that grave: angels will guard its sleep
While you secure the right that ba[--] to keep:
Prepare 'neath holy Temple roofs to tread,
And labor for the living, and the dead.
	Changes rush on—unique events appear—
We may clasp hands, and bless each other here.
						E. R. SNOW SMITH.
Salt Lake City, Feb. 1883.


THE CULTIVATION OF MIND.

	The thought that our actions here will either
enhance or retard our happiness in the next
world should be ever present with us. As
we progress in any branch of learning, our
preceptions are quickened or cultivated in re-
gard to it, as for instance in music; any triv-
ial mistake that would pass unnoticed by
others would excite our notice and offend the
ear, so with our moral preceptions. Quarrel-
ing and bloodshed, which seemingly delight
some gross natures, would make exceedingly
unhappy more feeling and sensitive beings.
	Virtuous actions are not always rewarded in
this life, as may be seen by reading the lives of
Phocion and Socrates; such lives serve to
remind base, despicable persons of their own
shortcomings, and appeal too strongly to their
better feelings, but instead of standing as
guides to a better life, often rekindle envy
and jealousy and the desire to pull down every
thing to their own mean standard, and these
are only two noble lives among the many
which have been taken or made miserable
through constantly walking in the narrow
path.
	Because the higher qualities often prove dis-
advantageous in this world, probably most
affecting the amassing of riches, it is no reason
why they will not, with knowledge, lead to
eternal bliss in the next, and as we are there
sure to meet sooner or later, those we have
benefited or wronged here, it is easy to perceive
that our heaven there will be in accordance
with our deeds done here.
	What happiness would we have in this life
without friends, and how is the tie of friend-


ship made but by kindred feelings based on
confidence, and as that could not exist between
conniving unscrupulous parties there could be
no friendship.
	From all the instructions given us relating
to our preparing for the next world, the cul-
tivation of the mind certainly takes the first
place, as through its enlightenment we are
prepared to receive or reject baptism and
other doctrines, and it is also certainly that
which prompts us to pure or vicious actions.
It can be cultivated in any direction.
	Prejudice is a "noxious weed" which should
not be allowed to grow, as it often overrules
our reason in the face of facts; this is often
the case in relation to the Book of Mormon,
for though no fault can be found with its
contents, many are prejudiced too much
against it to even read it.
	The following from the Millennial Star I
have often read with pleasure.
	"The Mind.—The Almighty gives mind,
man can only give example and education.
The natural minds in men are like the natural
fields in agriculture. There is every degree of
fertility, but they will all grow noxious weeds
unless cultivated and cared for."
							ANN FELLOWS.


SCENES IN NAUVOO AFTER, THE
MARTYRDOM OF THE PROPHET
AND PATRIARCH.

BY HELEN MAR WHITNEY.

	When Horace returned to Nauvoo he found
that a year or more had wrought great changes,
not only by the death of Joseph and Hyrum,
but other dear friends had passed away—num-
bers were married and others had grown up
till he could hardly recognize them, and
among the most noticeable was myself, which
fact his looks and words expressed the first
morning after his return, as he called to give
us, among other old neighbors, a friendly shake
of the hand. I was not yet sixteen, and
though the feeling between us then could not
rationally be called love, it was something
akin to it, admiration—which only needed
time and acquaintance to mature. I was con-
siderably tinctured with romance and enter-
tained the bright hope that I had already ex-
perienced my most bitter portion of the reali-
ties of life. His mother and sister were
pleased with the idea of our union, and the
latter had early expressed herself to that ef-
fect. Not very long after this Sarah and I
accompanied him and my brother William
across the prairie to Carthage, to see the jail
where Joseph and Hyrum had been murdered.
William and I had often passed through that
little town on the way to Ramus, to visit at
the brothers Perkins; but never before under
like circumstances or with such peculiar sen-
sations. The wife of the jailor very kindly
showed us up stairs. The walls of the room
had been whitewashed and the floor had been
scoured many times, but the stains of blood
were still quite visible, and we saw a number
of bullet holes in the door. We looked into
the cell, into which Willard Richards had
dragged Elder John Taylor after he was
wounded and covered him up, hoping that he
would be overlooked and his life spared to tell
the tale; not expecting that he himself would
ever escape alive.


[Column 1]
	﻿As we stood by the well-curb where Joseph
fell, Horace picked up a small chip covered
with blood, and which he still has in his pos-
session, though the blood is hardly discerni-
ble—nearly thirty-nine years having elapsed
since that awful tragedy was enacted, and
seemingly sanctioned by our nation, under
this grand Constitution which we had learned
from our earliest childhood to love and revere.
And no heed was paid to the wailing cries of
the bereaved, nor the solemn prayers of an
oppressed, weak and defenceless people, but
they were driven out with the supposition and
the hope that they would perish from destitu-
tion, cold and hunger.
	I copy the following incidents from my
father's journal. "May 28th, 1845. This
day the timbers were raising in the attic story
of the Temple, on the dome or steeple.
	"May 29th. Spent a short time at Bishop
Whitney's—had a good time talking of the
order of salvation.	*	*	In the evening met
with my brethren at Willard Richard's office.
*	*	We called on the Lord for rain, and for
the brethren upon the islands of the sea, for
those who had gone west, for the sick, and for
union. Great union prevailed.
	"May 30th. Sister Sarah M. Kimball came
and took myself and wife in her buggy.	*	*
I got out at Warsaw street. Went to General
Charles C. Rich's.	*	*	Then to Elder
John Taylor's, where I found the Twelve.
*	*	William Smith was dissatisfied, other-
wise the Twelve were one. Took dinner with
Elder Taylor. Mother Smith came into our
council at 2 o'clock to express her feelings be-
fore the Twelve—called us her children. The
feelings of the Twelve were expressed by our
President towards the families of the Smiths—
that we would do all that we could for them.
	"Sunday, June 1st. I went to the stand
*	*	Meeting was opened. I was called
upon to speak.	*	*	Spoke one half hour.
John Taylor followed, then President Brigham
Young spoke for some time. There was much
joy among the Saints, as we had not been on
the stand before for three weeks—had been
obliged to hide up. In the evening met at
Elder Richard's for prayer—prayed for rain,
had a good time and broke up at half past
one in the morning."
	That day was my mother's 39th birthday,
which my father mentions in his journal. The
2nd day of June he speaks of five of the
Seventies coming to dig his cellar, and says:
"We had a fine shower in answer to prayer.
We praised the Lord for His great goodness.
2nd day. Began to lay stone in my cellar.
*	*	The day pleasant—all peace and har-
mony in our city."
	About two years previous to this, a brick
addition was made to our log house. The log
part was now torn down and a two story one
of brick built in its place. Father had pur-
chased a good house and lot adjoining ours of
Brother John Tibbits, and presented it to my
brother William. The chamber of it we oc-
cupied until the upper part of ours was fin-
ished and painted, containing a parlor, two
bedrooms and a clothes-press to each, with
hall. The largest room, with exception of the
parlor, was mine, with two large windows, one
opened to the south and the other east,
towards the Temple. The rooms on the base-
ment were very similar. The hall passed east
and west, front, door opening towards the river,
and over it was a large stone on which my
father had his name engraven. This was the
first nice house that he had been able to build
us, his time having been previously engaged in
the Father's vineyard with his brethren, the
Apostles, and then, much of the time they
were obliged to keep themselves hidden or
disguised to escape writs being served on them.
They had to adopt different disguises,
which were sometimes very droll and ludicrous,

[Column 2]
and which afforded us no little amount of fun
and amusement. The last year spent in Nau-
voo, though it was not all gladness and delight,
was the liveliest, and I took more real pleasure,
and can look back to it and my associations
there as the brightest and happiest that I ever
experienced in that city. "Pleasures are bright-
est as they take their flight."
	I was a passionate lover of music, and had
so longed for the privilege of learning to play
the piano, and this I enjoyed during the sum-
mer of 1845. I had an accomplished lady
teacher who had received the Gospel in Lon-
don. My first lessons were given me on a
small piano standing in a milliner shop, which
was owned by the Miss Grays, then living on
Main Street. One of them is now Mrs. Rumel,
who has a large millinery establishment on
Main Street in this city. My teacher would
often praise my aptness in learning, and say
to me, "You will go ahead of me, Miss Helen,
for you have a voice to sing, which I never
had." President Brigham Young had a small
piano and invited me to come to his house
and practice with his daughter Vilate, who,
though younger than myself, had had previous
advantages, but was rather indifferent, and he
thought if I practiced with her she would take
a greater interest. Their piano stood in Sis-
ter Young's room, and her health being very
poor he proposed to have it brought to our
house when the upper part was done. This
pleased us both immensely. I never became
weary of practicing until after I heard it was
decided that we were to be broken up and
move to the Rocky Mountains. Though the
piano remained there through the winter I
felt no encouragement to continue taking les-
sons, though father tried to stimnlate me to go
on, and said, to encourage me, that they should
have the necessary material taken to manufac-
ture pianos and I should have one, but I knew
that I would forget it all; and we little
thought of its being so long before we got to
our destination.

To be Continued.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.﻿


[Column 1]
COTTAGE HOSPITALS.
FROM “WOMEN AT WORK."
	England is far in advance of this country
in her provision for her care of the sick in
small cities and towns, and the system of Cot-
tage Hospitals there in operation, is one which
we may study with advantage and adopt with
great benefit to the sick.
	Our large cities are, in general, well sup-
plied with hospital accommodations, but the
smaller cities and larger towns are lamentably
deficient in this respect.  Concerning the de-
sirability of providing these places with small
hospitals, there can be no question.  Especial-
ly are they needed in the larger manufactur-
ing towns, in which many of the employees,
are without a home, living in boarding places
and in case of accident or disease are but poor-
ly provided for, and many lives are lost in
consequence of improper care.  Any physi-
cian practicing in these towns well knows how
unsuitable are these boarding places for the
care of the sick, and often finds his labors
will be of no avail in face of the improper
conditions which surround his patient. How
diffierent would be the result, if the sick or in-
jured could be removed at once to a quiet
home-like cottage hospital, where, with a
trained nurse in charge, and attended by the
physician of his choice, he would also have all
the resources of the hospital at his command!
	The value of these small hospitals has
been well tested in England, during the past
twenty years, and their simplicity, the home-
like surroundings, and ready adaptability to
the needs of country life, have won for them a

[Column 2]
warm place in the regard of the English peo-
ple.  Two hundred and fifty cottage hospitals
are now in successful operation in that country,
relieving about 50,000 patients annually, who
contribute at least one-sixth the cost of their
maintenance to the hospital.  If this system
could be generally introduced into this coun-
try, its value would be  provided with hospital ac-
comodations, who are now deprived of their
advantages, many of whom would be willing
to pay a small sum weekly for the medical
care and nursing thus provided.
	A large outlay is not required, for the neces-
sary building need not be expensive.  In Eng-
land, the average cost of cottage hospitals,
having a capacity of about eight beds, is from
$4,000 to $6,000 each.  In my own experience,
with a capacity of twenty-five beds for child-
ren, $5,000 will about cover the expense of
land and building, of course, the cost of con-
struction will vary with the location, but there
is surely no town of 10,000 inhabitants, and
upwards, too poor to establish and maintain
one of these small institutions.
	A small hospital managed by ladies has
been in successful operation at Pittsfield,
Mass., for several years, another is contem-
plated at Newton, while a third, intended es-
pecially for children, has just been established
at Baldwinville, in the same State.  This is a
work for which ladies are well adapted, and
much of the energy now lying idle, or exerted
in wrong directions, might be profitably ex-
pended in the establishment and support of
cottage hospitals in every town of 10,000 in-
habitants in this country.
	The plan of operation may be very simple,
a small building, in a good location, can be
rented until desirable to build, and a nurse
placed in charge, with a woman in the kitchen,
and such other help as may be needed.  The
physicians of the vicinity will, I have no
doubt, be ready and willing to co-operate, and
can arrange their attendance so as not to inter-
fere with other engagements.  Each physician
is expected to take charge of the patients sent
by him to the hospital, and in England it is
customary to appoint one or more of the medi-
cal staff, in rotation, whose duty it is to look
after all the cases in the hospital requiring
especial attention in the absence of their
physician.  The medical director, as he is
called, is not to prescribe, except in an emer-
gency, for any but his own patients, but is to
carry out the wishes of his colleague, who is to
visit the patients under his care daily, and
order any alterations he may deem desirable.
L. W. Baker, M. D.


ELIZABETH MANNING HAWTHORNE.—Miss
Elizabeth Manning Hawthorne, the eldest
sister of Nathaniel Hawthorne, died recently,
at the residence of Mr. Appleton, at Mount
Serrat, Beverly, Mass;, at the age of eighty
years. She has lived a retired life for the
past thirty years in the old homestead where
she died. She was the eldest child of Nathan-
iel and Elizabeth Manning Hawthorne, whose
children were Elizabeth Manning, Nathaniel
the poet, and Maria Louise Hawthorne. The
youngest sister was lost on the steamer Henry
Clay, several years ago. Elizabeth was a lady
of pronounced literary tastes and liberal
culture. She was a writer, and was given
to much study, but so far as is known has
never given any of the fruits of her genius
to the world. She was two years older
than her gifted brother, who often said of
her that she could attain greater fame than
he had done, if she wonld apply herself to
literary pursuits. An ardent admirer of
nature, she was content to retire among the
woods and flowers which she loved.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 155


SILENT VOICES.

The voice of the heart, and the voice of the brain,
Their language is silent, but thrilling the strain!
What volumes they write! and what volumes they tell,
And the soul stores them all in its innermost cell.

At midnight, when sleep has forsaken the eye,
These voices come thronging, and sleep they defy;
And memory rising, obeying their call
Draws aside her sweet veil, or throws back her sad pall!

Alone on the mountains, these voices we hear,
Breaking sweet on the heart, on the brain, on the ear;
Transporting the soul to a land of its own,
And binding it round by a mystical zone!

In a voice without sound, they converse with the soul,
Defying restraint, and defying control;
'Tis a carnival sweet, that the spirit then holds,
And wraps the corporeal in magical folds!

'Tis a foretaste of that which in future will be,
A mirror upheld in which visions we see!
A picture presented—a realm of the soul,
Releasing the spirit from mortal control!

How we hunger, and listen to voices like these,
That come in the flowers, that come in the trees,
That come in the cataract, roaring and wild,
That has often our soul of its sorrow beguiled!

Let us often be silent, and watch for the time,
When such voices ring out in their mystical chime!
Such are often in mercy sent down from above,
Infused by the spirit of God!—who is love!

Oh life! earthly life! thy ENIGMA is grand!
Thy mystical units of elysian land !—
Thy whisp’rings bring balm to a sorrowing soul,
And it seeks not the magical charm to control.

'Tis like manna descending each day, as of old, 
And the same loving shepherd is watching His fold;
And His children are fed in a mystical way,
For the voice of the shepbord they love and obey.
								HANNAH T. KING.
	Salt Lake City, Feb. 21st, 1883.


﻿PATCHWORK.

'Tis not genteel, they tell me now,
	To make a patchwork quilt,
'Tis sign of poverty they vow
	And poverty is guilt

In worldly eyes—well let it be,
But you and I should clearer see,
I think it is a branch of art
That forms a good, important part
In home economy and joy,
And pleasant hours without alloy,
May thus be spent in social chat,
While fingers fly this way and that,
Forming designs of light and shade
Until a perfect piece is made;
And then the quilting, Oh how nice!
To meet in friendship and rejoice,
When work is done the circle spreads.
Mingling the white and golden heads
Of father, mother, sister, brother—
[If not your own, why then, some other,]
In pleasant play or may-be dance.
While jest and song the hours enhance;
From many a patchwork quilt I trace
Some well known name, some dear, dear face,
And from one star will spring anew,
Some time of love and friendship true.
Our parents loved those simple joys,
Shall we forget—their girls and boys,
And in the new-scenes scorn the old?
Ah no! there lies the purest gold,
And is not life all patchwork too,
Our thoughts and actions, false or true,
Pieces that go to form the whole
Great plan divine the Gods control;
Then be our station high or low
Let’s cut and fit the pieces so
That perfect shape and colors blend,
Praying that One above to lend
His wisdom where our own would fall,
Courage when our weak hearts would quail.
Then may our life work be replete
In all things perfect and complete.
									HOPE.


[Column 1]
A VENERABLE WOMAN.

PRESENDIA LATHROP KIMBALL.

[Continued.]

	Many incidents worthy of record and evinc-
ing the dauntless courage of Sister Presendia
occurred while the Buells were living on Fish-
ing River, Clay Co., Missouri. Mr. Buell
was employed as carder, at a mill some dis-
tance from home, and his wife and family were
consequently very lonely, as the log house
they occupied was some distance from neigh-
bors, situated in the woods. Here in this se-
questered spot Sister Presendia lived with only
a young girl for her companion. And even
the girl who had come there on purpose to
teach in that district, of course left Mrs. Buell
entirely alone during the day, as her little son
George was old enough to attend school and
went with his teacher. She writes, "I used to
stay alone all day in the woods, my company,
the wild birds, and my music the cooing of the
turtle doves in the forest." In this very hum-
ble home, on the 24th of April, 1838, her first
daughter was born, but lived only four hours,
she called her Adeline Elizabeth. It is won-
derful how much hardship Mormon women
have borne; and under what peculiarly trying
circumstances many of the children of the
Latter-day Saints have been born.
	As an example of heroic courage, we are
proud to give the following. One day while
Mrs. B. was living in this secluded place, she
had occasion to go to the mill four miles dis-
tant; she drove her own team and took her
little son George with her. As she came out
upon the prairie she had to pass through a large
company of armed men going up to Far
West. She well knew their purpose. Their
horses were loaded down with bedding, spades,
shovels and cooking utensils, and there was a
terrible clatter of tin dishes. There were two
men who knew Mrs. Buell and spoke to her.
She says, as the horsemen rose to go up the
hill, she, with her team and little child, was
only a little to the left of them. The horse-
men called a halt—and dismounted, facing
the company towards Mrs. B. and the child,
holding their horses by the bits. Sister
Presendia says, "I thought of Mr. Carey, who
had a short time before been killed on the
prairie as he was returning home from the
mill. Then, for the first time in my life, I
expected to be shot. I asked the Lord in
silent prayer to let my only child go with me
if I should be killed, as my husband had en-
tirely lost his faith in the Gospel. I did not
hurry or urge my team forward, but went
steadily on over the hill, and the horsemen
stood looking at me, but the shock nearly
overcame me." Think of the trial of that
moment; and also of the brutality of men who
style themselves "protectors" of women and
children, to thus intimidate a defenceless
woman and her little child on an open prairie,
where there was no possibility of obtaining help!
It is too inhuman to bear thinking of. Those
who read this account will doubtless look with
still greater reverence on the white hairs of
this aged veteran in the faith. The same Fall
Mrs. B., while living on the river bank, had
another dreadful fright from these Missouri
mobocrats, who were anxious to drive and des-
troy the Mormons. One day six men rode up
to her door on horseback, all well armed.
They inquired if she had seen two men pass
by there on foot. She said, No, she had not;
then they crossed the river in search of them.
They had but just gone when two brethren,
Bro. Rust and Bro. Cleveland, came in; they
were in search of work. Mrs. B. imformed
them of the danger that awaited them if they
proceeded. She gave them something to eat
and to carry with them in their pockets, and
told them they had better go up the river and

[Column2]
back to Far West and travel all night as they
had certainly been watched for evil. The
brethren were often whipped and sometimes
killed if they could be caught outside of the
"Mormon" settlements.
	Later on in the season when the carding
was over where Mr. Buell had been employed,
he rented a grist mill and two carding ma-
chines, built a comfortable house for his fami-
ly, out of the woods, and commenced business
again on his own account, and run the mill
through the following winter. The persecutions
against the Saints grew stronger and stronger.
Mr. Buell having apostatized from the Church,
was possessed of a very opposite spirit to that
of his wife, who was strong in faith and fervent
in spirit. Sister Presendia prayed earnestly to
the Lord for her companion, that he might re-
turn to the faith, but his mind had grown very
dark and it was useless to reason with, or en-
treat him to see the error and doubt which
had misled and confused him. The Hunting-
tons were obliged to leave Far West at the
time of the driving of the Saints from Mis-
souri in the Spring of 1839, and Sister Pres-
endia felt entirely alone and forsaken. She
expresses it thus, "I never saw my mother
again. I felt alone on the earth, with no one
to comfort me, excepting my little son George,
for my husband had become a bitter apostate,
and I could not speak in favor of the Church
in his presence. There was by this time not
one true Saint in the State of Missouri to
knowledge."
	Sister Huntington (the mother of Sister
Presendia) died on the 8th of July following,
from hardship and fatigue brought on by the
merciless men, "who persecuted the Saints
unto death;" many were killed and many died
of hardships, others deprived of health, which
they were never able to regain.
	From that time on Sister Presendia suffered
greatly in her feelings, and was overburdened
with sorrow, and her mind saddened with the
griefs of the people she so loved, and she
obliged to remain away from them all, with no
one to whom she could open her heart. Mr.
Buell prospered in business and in a temporal
point of view, the family had an abundance of
everything necessary to make them in every
way comfortable.
	About this time another son was born to the
house of Buell, Norman Oliver, on the 31st
of January, 1840, which one can readily per-
ceive was directly after the dreadful outrage
perpetrated against the Saints in Mo. These
two boys, George and Oliver, both to grew man-
hood, married and had families of their own.
The next few years after the Saints left the
State of Missouri, some of the greatest events
transpired in the history of the Church.

To be Continued.


TO PUBLISHERS AND EDITORS.—Many
newspapers and magazines have been estab-
lished in the United States and Canada within
the last two years, the names of which do not
appear in any newspaper directory or cata-
logue. The publishers and editors of such are
invited to send copies and a full description of
their respective publications to the editor of
Hubbard's Newspaper and Bank Directory of
the World, New Haven, Conn., U. S. A.,
that they may be properly catalogued and des-
cribed in the forthcoming edition of that work
for 1883. Editors who kindly give this notice
an insertion in their columns will confer a
favor upon the Press of America.


It is hard to personate and act a part long
for where truth is not at the bottom, nature
will always be endeavoring to return, and will
peep out and betray herself one time or
other.—Tillotson.

SALT LAKE CITY, MARCH 15, 1883.


[Column 1]
THE DESERET HOSPITAL.

DESULTORY NOTES.

	We are often asked why there is not more in
the EXPONENT. concerning the Deseret Hos-
pital. To give definite particulars that the
public might like to know would occupy consid-
erable space, and would scarcely be of sufficient
importance to give so large a share of a very
small paper, which is published in the interest
of women generally, both young and old, and
not one particular organization or department
of either charity, industry, or politics. We
may say, however, with all safety at the pres-
ent time, that thus far the Deseret Hospital has
prospered. With the many difficulties to sur-
mount that ever attend the establishing of an
institution of this character, and, taking the
whole matter into consideration, it cannot but
be said the ladies so far have done well. To
be sure they were most of them inexperienced
in hospital work, but time, patience and per-
severance accomplish wonders when the heart
is interested, and certainly the ladies of this
city and adjacent cities and counties have mani-
fested a true spirit of benevolence and liberality
in bestowing of their means, and a desire to
help carry on this enterprise, which is most
praise-worthy and admirable. There has been
obstacles to encounter and emergencies to meet
which were not anticipated in the beginning,
but such things are incidental in all depart-
ments of life and work, and therefore, scarcely
need commenting upon. The people were ap-
pealed to for free donations to assist in furnish-
ing and fitting up the building, and the res-
ponse was general from both men and women,
as well as prompt and generous.
	A great many have feit very anxious to help
forward the institution by giving public enter-
tainments; perhaps, because they knew more
of the needs of such an institution, and it is
customary to aid in this way, and certainly
it has been a great blessing and help to the
Hospital. The proceeds of the concert given in
the large Tabernacle last July, under the direc-
tion of a very able Committee, Elder George
Goddard, Chairman and general manager of the
affair, did considerable towards helping this in-
stitution in its incipiency. Since then a Pri-
mary Concert has been given in the Elevnth
Ward in this city, and the proceeds handed over
to the Hospital, $41, from the children, exceed-
ingly good indeed for the little folks; of course,
a great deal was due to the excellent manage-
ment of Mr. Louie Felt and her associates in
getting up the entertainment.
	The Unity Club, which waa organized in
November, 1882, have made a donation of $500
(five hundred dollars) to the Deseret Hospital
for the benefit of that institution. It is certain-
ly most commendable in these ladies to work
and aid with material help, a place for the sick

[Column 2]
and injured to be cared for and made comforta-
ble as they could not be in their own homes.
Tne money given was the proceeds of entertain-
ments and parties, for which the members of
the club worked with all diligence and enthu-
siasm, realizing they were laboring in a charita-
ble cause for an institution just in its infancy,
struggling for permanent establishment.
	For years the sisters actively interested in
the Relief Society in this Church have seen and
felt deeply the need of a place of this kind.
But where to obtain the means to found a hos-
pital seemed to be the difficulty in the way of
its accomplishment. There did not appear
beforehand, at least in the minds of many, so
much apprehension about maintaining it were
it once practically started. However, having
undertaken this benevolent enterprise, the
ladies of the Executive Board find that it will
require continuous application to meet the
monthly expenses, as there are necessarily more
patients that can only pay a small sum towards
their care and treatment, than those who can
pay in full the expenses for board and medical
attention.
	Every person must know what it is to go into
an empty house and furnish it with everything
for comfort and convenience, and besides the
furnishings such as are necessary for family use
in a home, a hospital must have a dispensary
supplied with medicines, and also instruments
for operations of all kinds. In this respect the
Deseret Hospital is well fitted up. The instru-
ments were purchased in New York City, and
are of the very best material and manufacture.
	All the apartments and wards are neatly and
suitably furnished, and the accomodations are
such as the patient might enjoy in a home,
more than like an ordinary hospital. All the
people employed there strive to make the
house home-like and its inmates happy. Ser-
vices of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-
day Saints are held in the reception room every
Sunday afternoon, and the sacrament is admin-
istered under the direction of the Bishop of
the Ward and his Counselors. The sick have
the privilege of the administration of the Elders
of the Church, which is certainly an advantage
and blessing to the suffering, which the Latter-
day Saints can appreciate.
	The medical board is ample for all emergen-
cies. Dr. W. F. Anderson, the regular visiting
surgeon, has an established reputation as a
physician, of more than a quarter of a century’s
standing in this community. Several lady
physicians are on the visiting and consulting
board who are well and favorably known as suc-
cessful practitioners and a house surgeon, who,
though a young lady, has already won laurels
in the profession, and made hosts of friends in
the calling she has chosen.


NO ADVERSE LEGISLATION.

	Congress has adjourned and comparatively
little has been accomplished, at lsast according
to the statements and dispatches received. This
people can congratulate themselves upon the
fact that the adverse legislation proposed by
anti-Mormons has all failed. None of the bills
proposing harsh or arbitrary measures having
passed this session, notwithstanding all the elo-
quence of famous senators wasted upon the
matter. One point has certainly been gained,
the seat has been given to the delegate of the
people's party from this Territory. The bill pre-
sented by the Utah delegate, which we publish
in this issue, was certainly more favorably re-
ceived than the opponents of "Mormons" would
like. The memorial of the committee was also
candidly considered and both the bill and the
memorial are such as can be recommended by


the people, in whose interest they were present
ed.
	The work of the Constitutional Committee
having been finished for the present session
they have returned home, and we shall proba-
bly hear more definitely what has been done
when the Committee meets in this City on the
10th of April next. However as it is we feel
satisfied all has been done that could be during
so short a time and with the bitter prejudices
that have been aroused and the antagonism that
has grown up in consequence of the falsehoods
and misrepresentations of those who are de-
sirous of making political capital out of the
Utah question.


MR. CAINE'S BILL.

	Hon. John T. Caine, on the 26th of last
month, introduced the following bill into the
House of Representatives:
	"A bill providing for the election of certain
officers in the Territory of Utah.
	"Be it enacted, by the Senate and House of
Representatives of the United States of Amer-
ica in Congress assembled, that at the election
to be held in the Territory of Utah on the first
Monday in August, 1883, the qualified electors
of said Territory may vote, not only for mem-
bers of the legislative assembly and other offi-
cers whose election falls upon that day, but
for all territorial, county, and percinct officers
in the same way and with the same effect as
they might have done on the first Monday in
August last, if the commissioners appointed
under the act of March 22nd, 1882, had not
failed to appoint registration and election offi-
cers: Provided, that their terms of office shall
expire at the same time they would have done
had they been elected on said first Monday in
August, 1882; and in the meantime the duties
of the said offices shall continue to be performed
by persons now holding said offices agreeably
to existing laws."
	The bill was read twice and referred to the
committee on the judiciary and ordered printed.


THE VOICE OF ONE OF THE POOR FE-
MALES OF UTAH.

	No man has the right to prescribe to another
what shall be his religious belief, and the
Latter-day Saints, who have added more to the
wealth of this nation than any other class or
colonists—by reclaiming and populating these
barren wastes, being the majority in this Terri-
tory claim the right of self-government, and as
Judge Black said, at the close of his argument
for Utah, (whose name we shall honor in con-
nection with others who have nobly plead the
cause of the oppressed,) "You have no author-
ity to legislate about marriage in a Territory; the
people of Utah have as good a right to their
religion as you have to yours." But if you, who
profess to have gained so much greater light
and wisdom, could show us a better way, or any
better fruits of true civilization, from your own
domestic relations, we would be willing to yield
the point. But when a people assume claims
and power which are unconstitutional and try
to force them upon us "by means flatly pro-
hibited" and "The right to say that the women
of Utah, or the men of Utah, shall not exercise
their religion as they may think it right and
proper, and that they shall be deprived of all
political power in this country because of their
entertainment of opinions upon this subject dif-
ferent from their own," and "that women who
are not found to be bigamists or polygamists," or
to have contracted this tie are to be disfran-
chised, and though we "Mormons" have never
infringed or violated the rights or claims of any


[Column 1]
﻿man or set of men, yet we are not to be allotted
even the claims of the robber or murderer or
criminals in general, but are to be convicted
and punished without judge or jury, we have
neither inclination nor intention to submit to
such an outrage quietly, without even a struggle.
And were we allowed to act upon our constitu-
tional political rights we would first expel from
our midst the characters who "openly and
notoriously" practice their immoralities and
also those who publish the lowest of slanders
against an innocent people, and it would in my
mind not "require the judgment of a court" to
remove such institutions. Let them cut out
their own cancer, which is the foulest blot that
could ever come upon civilization, and if the
feeders and supporters of these wicked and low
lived establishments were treated as they
should be they would not be permitted to vote
or to hold office. These criminal practices were
never countenanced in any "Mormon" com-
munity, while they were allowed to hold the
reins of government, but "where the carrion is
there will the eagles be gathered." This nation
is indebted not only to the "Mormon" men, but
to the "Mormon" women, and though we have
been wronged and borne our wrongs in silence
for many years and were willing to forgive and
"bury the hatchet," when we see that the very
worst enemies" to our Constitution are being
allowed to harass and afflict us, whom they
know to be the most peacably inclined, indus-
trious and enterprising class of people, and who
first colonized and made these once dreary and
barren deserts inhabitable, leaving no stone
unturned to rob and strip us again of our rights
as American citizens, and of every lawful
means of defence against the meanness and
violence the most corrupt set of lawless aggress-
ors that ever disgraced a nation, we do not
intend to yield quietly again, to be driven from
our happy homes, which are far dearer to us
now than were those which they robbed us of
in an early day and drove us into the wilder-
ness. We will publish our history and also
theirs, and expose to the world the abominable
schemos and hypocrisy of some of the wise and
learned of their "Scribes and Pharisees;" who are
trying to disfranchise the "Mormon" women
because, forsooth, we vote for the men of our
choice, being to our own interest to do so,
instead of for those who have openly acknowl-
edged themselves to be our most bitter enemies.
What an enormous "sacrifice" for the few Gen-
tile ladies who vote in Utah to deny themselves
this privilege long enough "to destroy the power
of the Mormon Church," expecting, as many do,
to share in the booty.
	We would call their attention to a time when
some were so very humaue as to propose the
right of suffrage to be conferred upon the "Mor-
mon" women that they might free themselves
from the awful bondage of the "Mormon"
Priesthood or hierarchy. This right having been
given us by the "Mormon" Legislature, over
twelve years ago, with the full consent of the
United States. We were not slow to avail
ourselves of it, and if we were in bondage and
felt our chains to be very galling we could most
assuredly have refused to live in, or sustain
plural marriage, and we are at perfect liberty, at
all times, to free ourselves if so disposed, and if
there was the least danger or necessity we
could call on this army—stationed here upon
the bench to protect us. This fact was proven
in an early day, when a few weak minded sisters
sought protection under the wing of Colonel
Steptoe's army, but which, I think, proved a
warning to others to beware of "wolves in
sheep's clothing." "Mormon" women are not
so ignorant as might be supposed, of the power
that they hold to declare polygamy illegal.

[Column 2]
But before Senator Edmunds and others allow
themselves to be convicted by overstepping their
authority in taking away our rights without
due process of law, they would do well to come
and visit some of "these poor females," for
whom they profess so much sympathy and'
wish to emancipate "from the slavery of voting.
(I wonder these words did not choke him)
Instead of trusting to the tales of those who are
using them as "cat's paws," and we warrant that
they "could get at the real opinion of the
"Mormon women," who are at liberty to speak
for themselves and are more than willing to
"give them the free exercise of those opinions,"
and they might possibly blush at their own
ignorance and bigotry, and return wi er if not
better judges of the human heart, and of the
superior blessings and privileges enjoyed by the
"Mormon" wives who have honored this Celes-
tial order. I believe that they would find more
advocates and those who would sustain this
order among the women than among the men.
Women are generally inspired by the strongest
religious motives; and they have the greatest
faith and courage to endure pain and the various
crosses in life. This has been demonstrated in
all ages. There are of course, exceptions to
every rule, but in the life of Christ, see the devo-
tion that was shown by woman. She was last
to linger at the Cross, and was first at the Sep-
ulchre. We read "when it was yet dark" and
"when the disciples went away again to their
own home" Mary still "stood without the Sep-
ulchre weeping," and she was the first to hear
the Savior's voice, who gave to her the message
to take to His brethren.
	I know of no plural wife who has felt fright-
ened enough to propose being divorced from her
husband, but I did hear of one man who, at the
first sound of the news that these commisioners
were to be sent here, gave to his second wife a
bill of divorcement. And I remember (not
many years ago) of a few dissatisfied men who
murmured against the authority of the Priest-
hood and when in darkness they sought revela-
tion from peepers and other spurious sources
and withdrew from the Church—and to gain
favor and popularity in the world they had to
put away their plural wives, though some had
become mothers and were devotedly attached to
their husbands and the parting between some
of them was enough to melt the hardest heart.
They had never given it a thought, when leav-
ing this Church that they would ever repudiate
any of their wives. But some of the first wives
gave their lords to understand that they had
no right to live with a plurality of wives out-
side of the Church, which they had withdrawn
from. So the virtuous portion sorrowfully sub-
mited to their fate with martyr-like fortitude
and the majority, losing what little light they
had fell into the worst darkness, spiritualism
and infidelity, and how much higher are they
now in the estimation of the world than are the
ones who held on to "Mormonism" and stood
out in defence of this Celestial principle of mar-
riage? I fail to see that they have gained any
thing; but they have lost every thing that was
essential to true happiness in this world and
all hope in the world to come. And as for the
demon jealousy, it flourishes more outside of
plural marriage than it does among the ones who
practice it whatever may be said to the contrary.
We all know and expect that our husbands will
acknowledge their wives before the world, there-
fore there is no deception nor necessity for prac-
ticing the system of hypocrisy and the awful,
vices of intrigue and lying which our most
zealous and over righteous accusers are addicted
to—encouraging secret transgressions; they
steal away from the only woman, whom they
honor with the name of wife, and indulge pro-

[Column 3]
miscuously without any regard to honor or mo-
rality, and many of them without the least
restraint, indulge in open profligacy, and then
adding insult to injury such characters set them-
selves up to judge us—denouncing the men and
the women who are "in the marriage relation"
as criminals, doomed to suffer any penalty which
they see fit to place upon us. But "people living
in glass houses should not throw stones." If it
is crime they wish to repress, let them begin at
home and first honor "that most sacred tie
which lies at the foundation of the home and
of society," (which we not only preach, but
paactice,) instead of saying, "I am more holy
than thou," because a new faith happens to
come in contact with the old rules and prejudices
and long established views of society, and their
opinions of "social order." "He that is without
sin among you let him cast a stone at us." But
we truly rejoice that we stand in our own posi-
tion to-day instead of theirs; for "he that is of
God heareth God's words," and the Latter-day
Saints have long been prepared for the scourges
and judgments which were to "commence at
the house of the Lord," and we see the calami-
ties already coming which were pronounced,
(through the Prophet Joseph Smith) upon
this favored nation, if they would not repent
and cease their persecuting God's people. The
things once viewed in the far future are now at
our doors, and do we rejoice at the sufferings of
these our fellow beings—even though some may
have sanctioned and had a hand in making these
wicked and inhuman laws to destroy and break
us up—rending assunder the dearest ties of con-
sanguinity, to rob and drive us again? No! there
is not one in possession of a Christian spirit, but
would be glad to save them if it were possible,
from the suffering which their own deeds are
bringing upon them. But when they not only
refuse to accept salvation when it is offered
them by God's chosen servants, and scorn the
warning voice, but continue to persecute them
to the death; they cannot eacape, for He has
long since decreed these things. We read in our
papers from day to day of the awful suffering
from disasters causing untold misery and an-
guish, such as no language has power to de-
cribe—sweeping thousands of God's creatures in-
to eternity without the least preparation or
warning, and to think that we have no power
to prevent it, and the only thing we can do is
to plead with that God of mercy, whose hand
has ever been over us for good, that he will show
mercy unto those who are innocent. But the
wicked of this nation have got a heavy debt to
pay. There are a few of God's predictions
which we see are being fulfilled. "Behold,
saith the Lord, I will bring plagues upon the
world, the sword, famine, death and destruction
I will hold my tongue no more. The innocent
and righteous blood crieth unto me and the
souls of the just complain continually. There-
fore, saith the Lord, I will surely avenge them,
and receive unto me all the innocent blood from
among them. Behold my people are led as a
flock to the slaughter. I will bring them with
a mighty hand and a stretched out arm.
	Woe to them that sin and keep not my com-
mandments! saith the Lord: Behold clouds from
the east and from the north unto the south,
and they are very horrible to look upon, full of
wrath and storm, and there shall be great fear-
fulness and trembling upon earth: and they
that see the wrath shall be afraid and trembling
shall come upon them. Fire and hail, and
many waters, that all fields may be full, and all
rivers, with the abundance of great waters, and
they shall break down the cities and walls—and
grass of the meadows, and their corn. Like as
thou hast done unto my chosen, saith the Lord,
even so shall God do unto thee, and shall deliver


thee into mischief—Thy children shall die of
hunger, and thou shalt fall through the sword,
thy cities shall be broken down, and all thine
shall perish with the sword in the field.” Our
God seeth all things, and He is surely avenging
the wrongs of an innocent oppressed people,
and our enemies will have no power to escape
the fierce judgments of the Almighty.
						H. M. Whitney.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 157


158	WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
EDITORIAL NOTES.

	Classes for instruction in midwifery and
obstetrics are being formed in the city in order
to meet the requirements of competent help in
this department of science.

	DR. R. B. Pratt, who has been teaching
for some years past, advertises her class will
commence in April. Terms thirty dollars for
the course, and twelve dollars for the books
used by the students. See advertisment.

	For sale at this office, Two Poems, by Bishop
O. F. Whitney, now on a mission in England.
The book is in pamphlet form, 10cts. per copy.
The proceeds of the sales are to be given as
a donation to the Deseret Hospital.

	IN the last issue of the EXPONENT we re-
published "A Sermon on Canvas" by O. F.
Whitney, but forgot to give him or the Millen-
nial Star credit for the article. We beg
pardon for the neglect and recommend the
article to our readers as a sublime and touch-
ing pen picture; those who read the descrip-
tion will feel as if they had actually seen the
artist's masterpiece.

	THE Conference of the women's organiza-
tions of Weber Stake was held in the Taberna-
cle, Ogden City, March 8, 9 and 10. Sisters
E. R. S. Smith and Zina D. H. Young went
up to the Conference on Thursday, but re-
turned the same evening, as the woman's
Conference in this Stake convened on Friday
and Saturday. Reports of these Conferences
will be published in the next number of
this paper.

	ON Wednesday, March 9, in company with
Mrs. M. I. Horne, we went to Coalville in
the interest of the Deseret Hospital. A meet-
ing had been previously appointed for half
past seven in the evening and a crowded house
was the result. The subject of the benefits of
hospital treatment for the sick and injured
was dwelt upon, and some committees were
appointed to make further arrangements for
the benefit of the institution.

	ON Saturday, March 3, an annual meeting
of the Primary Association of Farmington
was held at that place. Sisters E. R. S.
Smith, M. I. Horne and E. C. Clawson of
this city, went up on the morning train to
attend the meeting. Arriving at the depot,
they were serenaded by the Primary band,
who escorted them to the meeting house, play-
ing some enlivening music. The meeting was
well attended and the exercises ably conducted
by Mrs. Aurelia S. Rogers, who has been
untiring in her efforts to help the children
forward in their organizations. The pro-
gramme was interesting, the selections appro-
priate and very creditably rendered.

	ELIZA R. SNOW'S POEMS. The first volume
of Sister Eliza's Poems, which were published
several years ago in England, has been re-
bound and will soon be ready for sale. This
book contains many sublime effusions
which strike a harmonious chord in the heart
of every Latter-day Saint. "The Invocation
or the Eternal Father and Mother," a poem
entitled "Two Chapters of the Life of Presi-
dent Joseph Smith," "The Assassination of
the Prophet and Patriarch" and many other

[Column 2]
grand and touching pieces are contained in
this work. The Young People's Libraries
should be supplied with this work.

	Dr. E. S. Barney who has spent the last
five years in medical studies, most of that
time in some of the best medical colleges in
the East, makes quite an advantageous offer
to those wishing some obtain a knowledge of the
science of medicine and anatomy. Dr.
Barney offers for the specified sum of $2 per
week to take twenty-five students and provide
furnished rooms, bed, fuel, light, cook and
washwoman, making it something of a club ar-
rangement, as they will bring with them or
furnish their own provisions. The doctor will
give them free medical instruction, lectures on
anatomy, physiology, materia medica, surgery
and practice. This is certainly an economical
plan to work upon; particulars can be obtained
on making application by letter to Dr. E. S.
Barney, Box 555, Salt Lake City, Utah. Mrs.
Barney was a very successful school teacher
for many years in this city, and has, by patient
application and her own efforts, risen to the
ranks of the medical profession. The offer
she has made should meet with a ready res-
ponse from the several parts of the country,
for it has certainly been made in good faith
and with an earnest desire to benefit human-
ity. A more extended knowledge of nursing
and obstetrics, and the laws of life and health
cannot fail to be beneficial to all.


RESOLUTIONS.

	Resolutions of respect to our President, Margaret A.
Lambert, who departed this life January 6th, 1883,
aged twenty three years, ten months and eighteen days.
	Through the providence of God we are again called
to mourn, the loss of one of our members by death, two
others having died within the year, and this time our
beloved and respected President, whose loss we deeply
mourn for we loved her for her kind and gentle man-
ners, her faithfulness and integrity in the cause of
Christ, her prompt and punctual attendance to the
duties of her office, and the exemplary life she led
worthy of imitation in both her public and private
career.
	Therefore, be it resolved, that we condole with her hus-
band in his irreparable bereavement of a faithful and
loving wife and companion, and with her parents and
relatives and friends.
	May we long remember her saintly life, and emulate
her virtues, and her moral, religious and domestic life,
and may her infant daughter never know the want of a
mother's care, but live to fill up a life of usefulness.
	Resolved, that we present a copy of the foregoing reso-
lutions to the parents and husband of the deceased.
	Resolved, that we send a copy to the EXPONENT for
publication.
							ANNA LAMBERT,
							ELVA WOOLSTENHULME,
							EDITH ATWOOD,
											Councelors.
					ELIZA ATWOOD, Assistant Secretary.
Kamas, January 22nd, 1883.
	P. S. Since writing the above her infant daughter
has passed away.


﻿RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.

	Resolutions of respect to the memory of Sister Ellen
Condie, who departed this life Febuary 21st, 1883, aged
seventy years.
	The following preamble and resolutions were unani-
mously adopted by the members of the Fourth Ward
Relief Society.
	Whereas, an Allwise Providence has called from us,
by the hand of death, our beloved and respected sister;
	Resolved, that we recognize in the death of our Sister
Ellen Condie, the loss of an able, efficient and worthy
teacher and co-laborer in the Fourth Ward Relief Society.
	Resolved, that we esteem her past faithful labors and
devotion to duty as worthy the fellowship and highest
respect of us all.
	Resolved, that we condole and deeply sympathize


with the bereaved family and friends In the loss of &
hind wife, affectionate mother, worthy teacher and a
good and respected citizen.
	Resolved, that these resolutions be spread upon our
record, and that a copy be furnished the bereaved
family, also the WOMAN'S EXPONENT, for publication.
							MRS. SUSAN SPERRY.
							M. E. TAYLOR, Secretary.
	Salt Lake City, March 4th, 1883.


PRIMARY REPORT.

CHESTER PRIMARY.

DEAR SISTER WELLS:
	I am happy to say that our little meetings
are well attended, considering our scattered
condition. We have had one Primary fair.
A large table was covered with children's arti-
cles, such as wool flowers and fancy card work,
hoods, stockings, and many other things that
I will not mention. We have had a visit from
Sister Helena Madson and her Counselors.
She is our Stake President. She is a very
kind lady. Our much beloved President is
not at home at present; when she is away we
miss her kind voice and wise counsel. I am
but thirteen years old and this is my first at-
tempt to write to you. I send a New Year’s
greeting to all my Primary friends. Praying
that our Heavenly Father will bless us all,
that His Spirit will rest upon us is my prayer.
	I remain, your sister in the Gospel,
						MINNIE ALLRED.
	P. S. Sister Wells, I am one of the little
girls that helped make the hair wreath that
was sent to you from Chester three years ago.
I thank you for the little Primary Hymn
book you sent me.			M. A.


LETTER FROM ARIZONA.

ERASTUS, APACHE CO. ARIZONA,
				January, 30th, 1883.

DEAR EXPONENT:
	I thought perhaps a few lines from this out
of the way place might find a corner in your
delightful little paper. Last Friday, the 26th
of the month, was the birthday of one of our
good mothers in Israel, Sister M. P. Killian.
She is President of the Reief Society of this
place. Having recently lost her husband she
was feeling quite low spirited and cast down.
The brethren and sisters turned out en masse
and made things lively in the shape of a sur-
prise dinner, Sister Killian not being aware
of their intentions until they came into her
house well laden with baskets, etcetera. Soon
the tables were fairly groaning under their
weight of good things and all sat down to par-
take of the delicious repast. After dinner the
Choir sung several hymns, Bishop S. E. John-
son, made a few remarks and spoke in praise
of Sister Killian, who was completely over-
come by this unexpected demonstration. Bro-
ther Johnson presented her a beautiful card
bearing a quotation from Psalms, 27th chapter,
17th verse. "Wait on the Lord; be of good
courage, and He shall strenghen thine heart:
wait, I say, on the Lord."
	the evening passed away, each one enjoying
themselves to the utmost.
				BY ONE OF THE MEMBERS.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

DEAR EDITOR:
	As it is the contributions that in part com-
prise your valuable paper, I thought I would
throw in my little mite, hoping it will be ac-
ceptable. The EXPONENT has proved a great
blessing to the ladies in this vicinity, fo[-] as
yet we are in an out-of-the-way place, and can-
not get information of what is going on except
by what we glean from reading. What a great


blessing it is to be able to read! It proves in
this, as in all other things, that God in his in-
finite goodness' has provided for his children!
When I stop to meditate on the many good
books—some of them written by inspiration—
for the benefit of the human family, I cannot
bat have a greater reverence for my Heavenly
Father for the gifts he has blessed us with.
And do we appreciate it as we should? I fear
there are a great many of us who do not!
What are we placed on this earth for? Is it
to work; toil and eke out a miserable exis-
tence? I think not, and I think that the Lord
will hold us accountable for letting our talent
lie dormant; our minds need food as well as
our bodies. I believe the Lord intends that
his children shall enjoy the blessings and gifts
that he has blessed them with. We should avail
ourselves of every opportunity that comes in our
way, to improve our minds. What class of wo-
men is there on the face of the earth that
should be more noble, great and good than
those of the Latter-day Saints—with the spirit
of their religion to help them? Then let us,
dear sisters, seek for improvement, and prove
to our Heavenly Father that we are not uncon-
scious of the many blessings He has given on
this earth for us to enjoy. And let us place
such books before our children as will instil
in their little minds principles true and exalt-
ing, that when the Lord comes to make up his
jewels, we will not be so low and ignorant that
He will pass us by.		JOSIE E. CHILDS.
	Castle Dale, Utah.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 159


[Column 1]
CORRESPONDENCE.

	WAYNESVILLE, OHIO, Feb. 19th, 1883.
Mrs. A. Agatha Pratt—

		DEAR MOTHER:
	I have been to see the floods of Cincinnati
and will give you a few items. The papers
give a pretty fair description of things as they
are in the flooded district.
	I will not attempt to tell how many houses
are under water, how many floating down the
Ohio, how many families homeless and desti-
tute, how many people thrown out of employ-
ment, nor can I tell how many deaths will oc-
cur from the effects of damp and filthy holes
that people call home. If the destruction is at
the same ratio all down the Ohio valley as at
and near Cincinnati I will simply say it will
do much towards the fulfilment of prophecy.
Elder Marks and I took train Friday, a.m. for
Cincinnati, on the only Railroad that runs into
the city, all others being stopped by water.
	We arrived in the city about noon, and put
in good time traveling the flooded districts.
The streets were full of boats of all kinds and
sizes, full of people of all kinds and colors, on,
all kinds of business, some on pleasure bent
some taking food, water and fuel to those im-
prisoned in the upper stories by the floods,
some moving goods to higher ground; some
taking the sufferers to places of safety. This
will describe life in all the flooded towns. We
took boat for the bridge between Cincinnati
and Covington, Ky. The boats that carried the
passengers to the bridge were run by the city,
fare, three cents. We were taken around
several corners pretty well up in the world, at
least we could look in at second and third
story windows. We were landed safely on the
Mammoth Suspension Bridge, high and dry
over all. It is a grand sight to see and a
master piece of art. The water was sixty-six
feet four inches above its regular level, ye
some of the steamers could go under the bridge
by lowering the upper extension of the smoke
stack. We got a splendid view from the bridge
of these large cities, Cincinnatti on the north
side of the Ohio, Covington on the south side.
This place is on high ground and but little of

[Column 2]
it was under water. Newport is on a low point
of land between Licking River and the Ohio.
We stood some time on the bridge taking a
view of the world afloat. We could see the
heights of Cincinnati and the flooded portion.
Old out-houses, dwelling houses of various
dimensions, stores, warehouses,. R. R. depots,
distilleries, coal barges, R. R. cars, engines,
steamboats, tug boats and all kinds of wrecks
seemed to mingle freely together. The only
apparent difference was, the houses, factories,
etc, seemed like a demolished fort and the
tugs and steamers seemed like the triumphant
army that defied the elements and stood guard
over the vanquished forts, riding proudly over
all, sometimes pushing coal flats defiantly
against the stream, or retiring gracefuly with
the current to their place of mooring. Piles
of lumber, frame buildings, hay stacks, boxes,
barrels, etc, were kept in constant motion
by the raging waters, while the whole scene
was dotted by boats of every kind paddling
or pushing with poles, some on the lookout for
plunder, some for fun, some trying to save,
some laden with baskets of food, etc.
Took a look around Covington, crossed to
Newport, the windows all along each side of
the streets had much the appearance of the
show window of a Photo gallery, with this
difference the pictures were all life size and in
their every day dress or undress. We walked
around the high part of the city a little, then
went back to Cincinnati via Newport Bridge.
It was a very warm day, the waters were
receding and the air seemed filled with stench
from the unhoused filth and rubbish.
	We arrived in Cincinnati about dark. The
city was in darkness, the gasworks being
under water. In walking round the city we
saw some of the houseless urchins minus coat,
shoes and hat, curled up in the recesses of the
doors and the streetsteps; that was the most
heart-rending scene of all. To see the little
fellows curled up on the cold stones to shiver
till morning, then go in search of breakfast.
	We walked the streets for one hour,
saw all kinds of people in all kinds of styles,
sharpers, drummers, news boys, boot-blacks,
business men, shop men, women and children
of every kind and color, factory girls, waiter
girls, and bad girls, Irish, Dutch and Yankee.
We took a room facing Fountain Square, went
to the opera saw Haverly's Minstrels pre-
form very good. Next morning took another
look around, boot-blacks thick as fleas, news
boys with the early morning papers yelling
out "Morning Journal, penny post, all about
the floods for one cent." All kinds of ped-
dlers vending their different wares. Went
through the markets saw meats, fishes, fruits
and vegetables of every concievable kind and
flavor. We followed the lower winding alley
as close to the water as possible; sometimes we
would see a crowd of Irish trying to keep
warm by a fire fed by the ruins of boxes and
other peices of timber raked out of the water.
We saw nearly everything afloat that would
float, pig pens, out-houses and dwelling houses,
some just coming in sight by the falling waters,
some turned cross wise, some over and some on
end. We found our way to the place where
the southern depot used to be, but is now a sea
of muddy water. The main buildings were
gone, some were in sight badly wrecked,
some places the tops of the box cars were in
sight, sometimes a road engine with the smoke
stack out of water. We visited the "Early
Breakfast" stove works. It had shut down,
but Mr. Burton showed us through his sample
room and workshop. They make nine hun-
dred and fifty stoves per week. We did not
visit any other factories as they were all
stopped. Many houses are falling down as the
water leaves.
						M. W. PRATT.


THE CROWN JEWELS OF AUSTRIA.

					VIENNA, Oct. 3rd, 1882.
	No one leaves Vienna without a visit to the
treasury, which is full of rich and remarkable
curiosities. On the long walls of the entrance
room hangs many a page of Austrian history.
The standard of the nation catches your eye
as you enter the hall. Just beyond this comes
a delightful assortment of herald's robes—robes
embroidered by fingers which forgot their cun-
ning in the centuries that drifted into the
great past long before the more modern heroes
were born. The shield of the Grand Marshal
calls up a thought of gorgeous pageants and
imperial shows. The ebony casket containing
the keys of the coffins of the ancestors of the
imperial family is equally suggestive. A large
crystal goblet set with rich jewels takes us
back to the times of Charles the Bold. We
sit beside him at some grand feast, watching
the old courtiers as they pledge themselves
and their arms to the fair ladies whose knights
they bound themselves to be. The bright eyes
and rosy lips that graced the court of the
brave old monarch lives only in song and
story, but the old goblet flashes back the light
of the present century as clearly as though
just fresh from the lapidary. The appoint-
ments for the tables of royalty were neither
simple nor inexpensive in the times before
our own. Francis I., of France, counted
among his valued articles a salt-cellar, the de-
sign wrought from the fertile brain of Ben-
venuto Cellini.
	We go on through these wonderful rooms
till we come to the jewels of the imperial
family. We find that our republican educa-
tion is no bar to our enjoyment of crown
jewels. We confess to a positive satisfaction
in the contemplation of all these regal appoint-
ments. This Austrian regalia dates back to
the time of Rudolph II, and includes the
crown sceptre and imperial globe. They tell
their own story of individual power and force, 
differing widely from any our own continent
has ever known. The crown of the Empress
looks fresh and modern, having been re-set
within a few years. Diamonds of fabulous
value flash out their glittering rays as we go
on from case to case of these rich jewels. A
beautiful hat-clasp contains the wonderful
Florentine diamond, valued at about three
hundred thousand dollars. This belonged to
Charles the Bold, of Burgundy; it was found
by a peasant after the battle of Granson and
sold by him for less than a dollar. It is
probably that the merchant of Berne who
bought this royal gem was as well satisfied
with his share of the spoil as any of the actors
in the famous battle. A scarf contains the
grand cross of the Maria Theresa order, com-
posed of hundreds of diamonds clustering
around a large pink one. Rubies and eme-
ralds of the choicest kind are used in all sorts
of devices in these magnificent ornaments be-
longing to the imperial families. Many articles
are the decorations of different orders. One of
the Golden Fleece is made up with at least a
hundred and fifty brillians, the Frankfort dia-
mond with coronation robes which have playes
a grand part in the historical days of the past.
	The little silver cradle of the King of Rome.
still bright with its golden coating, is the most
pathetic thing we find in all this grand dis-
play. It stands empty and neglected in the
same oom where we saw the insignia of Napo-
leaon as King of Italy.
	Historical curiosities greet us at each step.
We sigh for longer days and fewer limitations
to our time. As we come upon the old horo-
scope of Wallenstein, we wonder if the talis-
man may not be able yet to work its spell. If
we could lay hold of one of those astrologers of


old, how persistently we would besiege him
till he should give us length of days and the
ability to comprehend the things about us.
	The storehouses of our memory are all in-
adequate to hold the wares offered by these
old empires for our consideration. The sabre
of Haroun al Raschid savors of romance and
daring deeds. In another small room, without
even a guard to keep watch over its treasures,
we find another case of relics that might well
make us stop and look down the ages. Charle-
magne rises out of the events of the past
which come crowding down upon our bewilder-
ed senses- This great, ambitious king strides
grandly before us. Here is his crown—iron-
faced and firm as when it rested upon his im-
perial brow after the siege at Pavia in 773.
Here is his stole, his girdle, his sword. Here
are his coronation robes and his sceptre; the
imperial globe which was never held by a
firmer grasp than when the mighty king put
his strong hand upon it. The present century
seems the one that is dim and far away as we
stand in a dazed condition. These famous
relics of the "Emperor of the West" were
found in his tomb at Aix La Chapelle. When
the vault was opened he was found sitting in
most regal state on a throne. A mightier des-
pot sat side by side with the imperial body.
Death alone had curbed the spirit of this
mighty warrior king. France had cause to
take great pride in this wonderful sovereign,
Lombardy to hate him, Italy, Spain, Germany
to fear him. Learning, art and science owe
much to his patronage. He was as extra-
ordinary as despotic. We seem to come almost
into his visible presence as we stand gazing at
the old crown.
	We are quite content, however, with our
own day as we shake off the illusions which
gather around us. There is another great
country beyond the sea where trade and
industry find a home, where art and science
are encouraged, where there is free thought
and free speech, liberty of conscience and room
for all who crave it. There are no old coro-
nation robes hanging on the walls of its
treasury; no crowns whose wearers have put
their iron heels on the hearts of its children.
Charlemagne's hour is over. A grander na-
tion has risen—a West which he never
dreamed of.
	The Prater is the grand park of the city,
where all visitors delight to drive. Grand
carriages of the nobility are daily the admira-
tion of the walking public. We hunt up the
old buildings frescoed on the outside, admire
the fountains and statues, roam through the
quaint sections of the place, and wish we had
weeks to spend within its walls. We could be
busy all the time; hunting up new sights and
old curiosities. When we turn from here it
will be with regret; but everywhere we leave
much unseen which we shall hunger and thirst
for in the days to come. We generally coax
ourselves off with the promise that "next time"
we shall do all these wonderful things which
we leave undone now. If the promise is never
to be quite fulfilled, we go away rather more
cheerful for our little delusion. If we had all
our lives to live over, we half suspect we might
after all leave out some things we would be
loth to do without. In the morning Vienna
will be a long way behind us. We have had
a royal feast within her gates. The banquet
has been both rich and rare. We are more
fortunate than the Romans of old; we turn
away before our appetite is wholly appeased.
	Ex.						RUTH RANDOM.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HAVE you ever thought of it? The memory
of an eye is the most deathless of memories,
because there, if anywhere, you catch a 
glimpse of the visible soul as it sits by the
window.—D. G. Mitchell.


SECRET prayer must be the root for nourish-
ing all public prayer and service. "Apostasy
begins at the closet door," says Philip Henry.

	A conscience so lazy as to be prone to fears
of extra duty is certainly in itself very differ-
ent from the real, old, saintly kind which used
to be always inclined to overburden its owner
with duties.

	THERE is no danger that man will not ulti-
mately do man's work and woman woman's
work. But it will yet take the experience of many
emancipated generations to determine the
maximum limits of both; and after all, the
best part of all the work will be that which
both sexes do in common.—T. W. Higginson.
