WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
The Eights of the Women of Zion, and the Eights of the Women of all Nations.
Vol. 11.
SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, APRIL 15, 1883.	No.	22.


A GIFT.
		______
Sweet souled and faithful one,
To-day I have no other
Dear gift, thee to bestow upon,
Than this—God bless my mother!

And with it, these fair flowers,
Emblems of peace and beauty,
Hoping they’ll cheer some lonely hours,
Of waiting and of duty.

A year ago—O how
Are earthly pleasures riven !
Father was with us then, but now, 
He sings God’s praise in heaven.

Never will be complete
Our peace and joys—O never !
Till in that holy, safe retreat,	
No kindred tics will sever.

But there, O. blissful hours !
When perfoct family chains,
Unite through Jesus’ love and powers,
And joy eternal reigns !
April 3rd, 1883. 					Lula.


LIFE’S LESSON.
Wouldst thou learn from Life a lesson,
	Learn’d but slowly and by few?
Wouldst thou know from Death’s dominion,
	How to win the Ever-new?
Then thy soul prepare for trial,
	Bare thy shoulder to the rod,
School thy mind for self-denial,
	Learn to love the Lord thy God.

Build no shrine to earthly idol,
	Lest there  come a shattering day,
Leveling to the dust thine altars,
	Driving all thy hopes away.
Pleasure's tree may tempt thee sorely,
	Golden apples grace the sod,
Touch them not!--they turn to ashes--
	Thou shalt love the Lord thy God.

I have gazed on beauteous woman,
	With a fond, adoring eye;
I have stood where Mammon's altars
	Rolled their incense toward the sky;
I have quaff'd the wine of Pleasure,
	Heard the winsome notes of Fame,
Armed with nature's gift and heaven's,
	Fought and toiled for honored name.

I have seen proud Mammon's towers
	Dashed to atoms by a breath;
I have lived to see the setting
	Of the sun of Love in death;
I have drank the dregs of sorrow,
	I have kissed the chastening rod,
I have learned, if Name be lasting,
	I must love the Lord my God.

What is wealth, that man should worship
	Dust from when his vileness came?
More than help-meet, lovely woman--
	Source and destiny the same?
What is earth, with all its glory?
	Earth shall answer: "Ichabod!"
Seek that kingdom all-including,
	Worship One--the Lord thy God.
London, March, 1883				O.F. Whitney.


[For the Woman’s Exponent.]
	WILD FLOWERS OF DESERET,

Tired of Winter’s chilling breath,
Wishing for Summer, thinking of death;
For I was weary, the sick-room dreary,
	O, so lonely and sad,
I longed for Spring flowers and sun-lit hours.
	And song-birds to make me glad.
I turned from the window’s busy scene,
The earth cold sheeted in icy sheen,


And the falling snow, to the warmth and glow
	Of the blazing firelight,
And at random took from the shelf a book-;
	My gloom soon changed to delight.

The words seemed traced by a magic wand,
The pen been held by a fairy hand;
Else how could they banish my gloom, how vanish
	So quickly the pain and regret?
The air in the room seemed filled with perfume
	Of “Wild Flowers of Deseret.”	

“Leonardo Da Vinci” first I read,
Sighing to think of the lovers—dead,
Then laughed right out at “A Lost Thought,”—
	And the laughing did me good;
For by the time I had finished the rhyme
	I felt in a happy mood.

“Grandfather,” “Grandmother,” and then
“My Heart and I,” and “Thoughts Within,”
Like sacred music rang, till “Polito Slang”
	Again woke the merry mood,
I cannot attest which pleased me the best,
	Each and all were so very good.

Ah, nothing is better in lonely hours,
Than this sweet cluster of Deseret Flowers;
Culled from the mountains, gathered near fountains.
	Fragrant with faith and love;
Giving cheer to this life of wearisome strife.
And pointing to heaven above.
Ohio, March 20th, 1833.              EMILY SCOTT.


THE YOUTH OF ZION.
				____
	It is evening, and while quietly thinking of 
the past, present, and the future, my mind es-
pecially dwells on the youth of Zion, male and
female, and wonder what they are doing at the
present time. Some may say, what buisness is it
of yours? Well, I know how mine are engaged,
and I think it would be well if all mothers
knew how their sons and daughters were em-
ployed; for verily this is a time for us to ear-
nestly watch our young people, and preserve
them from being enticed and overcome by the
evil one, and the temptations that exist, not
only in this city, but so far as I can learn, in
the various settlements. O! my young brethren
and sisters, flee from the evil as you would
from the poison of a snake; yea avoid the very
appearance of evil; take the advice and coun-
sel of those who have had more experience in
the world than you; think of the many privi-
leges you enjoy by being in this Church and
Kingdom, and the opportunities and induce-
ments held out to you, for gaining knowledge
which will fit and prepare you to fill honorable
positions in this life, and enjoy endless lives in
the worlds to come. O! ye daughters of Zion,
why will ye spend the precious time given to
you in frivolity, which I am sorry to say so
many do? Remember you are wasting the
moments that you should be spending in gain-
ing knowledge as to how you can best prepare
yourself for that high and noble purpose for
which you were created even to bring forth sons
and daughters, whose spirits were created by
our Father in Heaven, and have been reserved
to come forth in this dispensation, and you will
be held accountable for the manner in which
you train them.
	Can an impure spring bring forth pure water?
Nay, verily, and it is just as impossible for a
mother to teach her children the principles of
truth, unless she practices the same herself.
But some may say, I don’t know that Mormon-
ism is true, I have not had a testimony that
the work called the Gospel of Jesus Christ has
been restored upon the earth; if these be your
feelings, then rest no longer in this uncertain
state of mind; ask the Lord in humility of
heart, by earnest prayer, to open the eyes of your
mind to see and understand, and know for
yourself; and if you will do so, you will not be
long without a testimony of its truth, and that
Joseph Smith was a Prophet of God. Again,
there are many who possess a knowledge of its
truth, and are in the enjoyment of the blessings
of the Gospel. "They are those who take every
opportunity which they have to attend meet-
ings, read good books, and in every possible
way gain knowledge for the position of wife
and mother. These will be among the wise
virgins, who when the Bridegroom cometh, will
have their lamps trimmed and burning, whilst
on the contrary, those who have neglected to
improve their present opportunities, will find
themselves unprepared, it will be to late to get
oil, and they cannot borrow, therefore they will
not be permitted to enter into the Marriage
Supper of the Lamb.” These things, my
young sisters, are not to be trifled with. Then
seek while young for that wisdom which is of
far more worth than all the splendor or fleeting
vanities of this life; be virtuous and retiring
in your demeanor, let your daily walk and
conduct be circumspect, that you may not give
cause to any one to think lightly of you, for
verily a wise man will certainly look for and
admire a woman who is discreet and modest in
all her movements, and who is endeavoring to
improve in all that is good and praiseworthy.
That this may be the aim of all the daughters
of Zion, is the prayer of your humble servant.
	February 19th, 1883.			E.R.C.


SCENES IN NAUVOO AFTER THE
	MARTYRDOM OF THE PROPHET
		AND PATRIARCH.
			---------------
	BY HELEN MAR WHITNEY.
			_____
	In justice to the wives who were sealed to
William Smith I will here say, that they were
pure and noble women, and they had supposed
him to be a righteous man. The Lord took
the first one, and the others, finding out his
true character, soon left him. After the young
wife left he married again. I understood her
to be a sister of his first wife’s. But she, like
him, was void of the spirit of this Gospel; and
she must also have been blind to his faults, or
to put the most charitable construction, it
might have been for the sake of her sister’s
children. None of us are capable of passing
judgment upon one another. That, we should
leave to Him who will soon come to judge
both the living and the dead.
	One of the most interesting incidents within
my recollection was the laying of the capstone
on the south-east corner of the Temple. This
was on Saturday morning, May 24th, 1845.
The description of it is accurately given by
one of the Apostles, who, with the rest, came
out from their secret retreat long enough to
perform this ceremony, when they again re-
turned to their hiding places.
	The Apostle wrote:  "The singers sang their
sweetest notes, and their voices thrilled the
hearts of the assemblage; the music of the
band, which played on the occasion, never
sounded so charming; and when President
Young placed the stone in position, and said:
The last stone is now laid upon the Temple,
and I pray the Almighty, in the name of
Jesus, to defend us in this place, and sustain
us until the Temple is finished and we have all


[Column 1]
﻿got our endowments.’ And all the congrega-
tion shouted, Hosanna! Hosanna! Hosanna,
to God and the Lamb, amen! amen! and amen,
and repeated these words the second and third
time, the spirit of God descended upon the peo-
ple, gladness filled every heart, and tears
of joy coursed down many cheeks. The words
of praise were uttered in earnestness and fervor;
it was a relief to many to be able to give ex-
pression to the feelings with which their hearts
were overcharged. Altogether the scene was a
very impressive one, and we doubt not that
the angels looked upon it and rejoiced.”
	“So let it be,” said President Young, con-
cluding the ceremonies, “this is the seventh
day of the week, or the Jewish Sabbath. It is
the day on which the Almighty finished his
work and rested from his labors. We have
finished the walls of the Temple, and may rest
to-day from our labors.”
	A great amount of sickness prevailed dur-
ing the summer months. The Apostles and
their brethren were in the habit of meeting
together every evening, and sometimes oftener,
at Brother  Willard Richard's house, to unite
their faith in the holy order of the Priesthood
in behalf of Israel.
	They also had to labor with their hands.
My father worked at his house all the time
that he could possibly get. July 7th he wrote,
“Twelve men came to work on my house, six of
them were masons. Much work was done.
*	*	I went to W. Richard’s to read history
in company with Brigham Young and George
A. Smith. An Italian brother Joseph Toronto
(now of this city), came there and gave up
all he had, which was $2,600.
	“Tuesday, 8th. Fourteen men came to work
on my house. 	*	*	Went with Brigham
Young and Willard Richards to the Temple.
Had council with the trustees. Gave the $2,600
to the Bishop. Visited some sick in the evening.”
	My mother’s babe was very sick at this time,
which fact father mentions in his journal as
being a source of anxiety and sorrow to wit-
ness his suffering. Two successive days some
of his brethren came in to join him in prayer
for him in the holy order. “Sister Jane
(Uncle Joseph Young’s wife) and Sister Whit-
ney were present and spent much’ time with us
to minister to our babe.”
	“On the morning of the 9th of July,” he
says, “I was sent for by Brother Willard Rich-
ards to administer to his wife who appeared to
be dying. About ten o’clock I went again.
Brother George A. Smith and his father,
(John Smith) Levi Richards and John Taylor
were present. We anointed and prayed for
her alter the holy order and she died in about
half an hour. She (as is well known) was one
of my father’s earliest converts in England.
I will here mention one or two incidents,
though this and much more has been published
in his history, concerning her. She was
the daughter of a Mr. Richards, a Presby-
terian minister; and when father first met her
she was visiting a family in Preston with
whom he was acquainted. As soon as they
were introduced she entered into a conversa-
tion with him on the subject of the Gospel.
He found her a very intelligent lady and very
anxious to hear and understand the doctrines
of the true Gospel. She went to hear him
preach that evening and the following, and
then she was fully convinced of the truth and
sent for him and expressed her desire to be
baptized, which request he cheerfully complied
with, and confirmed her at the water’s edge.
And as an illustration of his prophetic charac-
ter, the first time he met Brother Willard
Richards he exclaimed, “Willard, I have bap-
tized your wife to-day.” He writes in his jour-
nal, that Sister Jeannette Richards was buried
at six in the afternoon of the 11th.
	“On the evening of the 9th,” he says, “the
Bishops made a feast at the mansion." Broth-

[Column 2]
er John Pack had charge of the house.
Joseph’s family having moved back into the
White House, nearer the river. Father says,
“I went to John Pack’s to wait upon the
Smith family as the Bishops made a feast.
About one hundred persons present. All
things passed off well.
	“Thursday, July 10th. The Saints met at
the stand at ten o’clock in the morning and
gave alms to the poor. I spoke, also Brigham
Young, George A. Smith, John E. Page and
Orson Pratt. Adjourned at two in the after-
noon, as the heat was so intense. A great
many are sick.”
	After the meeting of fasting and prayer,
father says, “I met with my brethren in the
evening and prayed for rain.” And on the
13th he wrote, “On Sunday morning it began
to rain very hard—have had a beautiful
shower. This was in answer to our prayers.
The Lord be praised for His goodness.” He
wrote the day previous that his wife, Sarah
Peeke, was very sick at Brother Stephen Win-
chester’s, and he sat up with her most of the
night. The evening of the 16th, after witness-
ing the death of Brother William Gheen, who
died at 7 o’clock in the evening, father took
Sarah and Sister Winchester to the river and
baptized them for their health. He was pay-
ing them for the board of his wife and two
daughters, whom he had adopted. Brother
and Sister Winchester and their family, if they
had been our nearest kin, could not have
thought more of one another than we had done
from the time that we were neighbors in Kirt-
land, Ohio and in Missouri.
	“Brother William Gheen,” father says, “was
buried on the evening of the 17th, at 6 o’clock.
Most of the Twelve were present.” Brother
Gheen and family were among the Saints who
came from Pennsylvania. He had given two
of his daughters (Anna and Amanda) to my
father as wives, and did not consider it a
dishonor to be thus connected with him, but
quite the reverse. They have each borne him
a numerous family. And though Anna has
left us to join him and thousands of loved ones
beyond “this vale of tears,” her name will
ever live in the hearts of countless numbers
besides the many who are connected with her
by family ties.
	For five successive days father wrote in his
journal that his time was spent working on his
house, visiting the sick, in council, and a va-
riety of other duties. “Saturday, 26th,” he
says, “I visited many sick and attended coun-
cil. We nominated several officers for our
August election. All goes well, and the Lord
is on our side.” Sunday he mentions going to
meeting with his wife and daughter Helen.
“Brothers George Miller and Amasa Lyman
spoke a few words upon the Nauvoo House,
then Brigham Young spoke.” Most of his
time on the following Monday, Tuesday and
Wednesday was spent visiting the sick; and he
speaks of a funeral that he attended with Brig-
ham Young, and speaks of them reading his-
tory and going on the Temple. The house
of the Lord was well on towards its completion,
And they could truly say that this was one of
the results of faith with works. These things may
look strangely to those who are unacquainted
with the principles of our faith and the great
work which we have been engaged in for over
fifty years, and especially that our people should
continue working at the Nauvoo House and
Temple, and at the same time preparing to
leave them to the mercy of a set of sacrile-
gious mobocrats to demolish. I think that this
should be a convincing proof (at least) of the
sincerity and honesty of their motives.
Nothing could daunt their spirits; but if they
had not enjoyed something superior to any
man-made religion they could never have
been supported under all those sorrowful and
trying scenes, It was through the united faith

(Column 3)
and prayers of the faithful few that we were
permitted to remain there long enough to finish
that Temple, that they might be endowed with
the blessings which the Lord had promised
them, and for which they cheerfully gave their
mite and labored faithfully to finish the house
which the Lord had commanded to be built.
Words cannot express the gratitude that I feel.
for being counted worthy to have place among
the ones of whom the Lord has made a “pecu-
liar people,” which is the only Church ever es-
tablished upon the earth since the one we read
of in the days of Christ, who believe and ac-
cept the whole of the Gospel as taught in the
ancient Scriptures, instead of choosing that
portion only which agrees with our peculiar
ideas and notions. The ones who do this are
blind indeed.
	If Christ is truly our pattern, and He had to
submit to bear all manner of crosses and sink
below all things, that He might rise above all
things, how are we to become joint heirs with
Him unless we have a similar experience in
this life? This people have proven their wil-
lingness to submit to be persecuted and hated
of all men for righteousness’ sake; and where
is there another people who have manifested
such true Christian patience, and faith enough
to trust in an unseen hand under all circum-
stances and still believe in and rely upon those
promises made by our Savior, who also com-
manded that we should become one in all
things, and said, “Unless ye are one ye are not
mine.” And because we are striving to obey
this command and every other Christian and
God-like principle, that we may be saved and
have part with Him in that Celestial glory
prepared for the faithful and pure in heart of
all nations, and that they may hear the same
glad tidings of great joy and be gathered with
us into the fold of Christ, making sacrifice of
our idols and rejoicing even in the midst of
our sorrows, when we part with our beloved
brethren, husbands, fathers and sons, who are
going forth in the service of our Master to
save the souls of men, that they may also be
partakers of this joy which is unspeakable and
that life-giving power which has ever brought
comfort and cheer to the humble heart and is
free to all who will seek for it; and because we
have sought to become of one heart and one
mind, we have ever been looked upon with a
jealous eye, and hated by the world, who refuse
to hear and understand the truth, but will mis-
judge us and all our motives. Union is power;
and this is the great bug-bear, and because they
cannot break up and destroy it, they consider
us a dangerous rival, and in their madness cry
polygamy as the only plausible excuse for break-
ing up and destroying this power;
	They do not understand the character and
the unwavering integrity of an honest Latter-
day Saint; but we can well afford to be chari-
table, for we know what is in store for them,
and many of us have drank deep enough of
the bitter cup of adversity to know how to feel
for our fellow creatures, and I fervently pray
for those who are deceived in consequence of
the great prejudice which is in the world, be-
cause of the awful lies which have been manu-
factured and set afloat, for effect, by the wicked
in our midst; and our foes are more to be pitied
than we are. They certainly have met with
poor success so far, and feel rather chagrined
at the present time. And Longfellow says, “If
we could read the secret history of our enemies
we should find in each man’s life sorrow and
suffering enough to disarm all hostility.”
	Supposing they should oblige us to leave
here, it would be doing for themselves a more
sorry job than when they drove us from the
United States, and if they were wise they
would leave us alone; for it has long since
been proven that we are like a mustard stalk,
which, if disturbed, will only multiply the
more.		To be Continued.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


WO﻿MAN'S EXPONENT.


HONOR TO PARENTS.

"Honor thy father and thy mother that thy days may be
long in the land which the Lord thy God giveth thee."

If thou with angry words would strike,
Or striking wound another;
O never let such venomed dart
Descend upon thy mother.

Remember all the days gone by,
When thou wert in her keeping,
When gazing on her love-lit eye,
Or on her bosom sleeping.

She gave thee of her life and heart,
And blessed the day that bore thee;
In love she ever bore her part,
And softly guarded o’er thee.

When helpless to her hand you clung,
Her warm heart fondly beating,
Upon your gentlest accents hung,
Its tenderness repeating.

But now to stalwart manhood grown,
Such scenes no more reverting,
Beware of words like arrows flown,
For who can tell their hurting.

When feeble stops yet bear her on,	
Toward life’s other border,
Still let the love she bears her son,
some balm in life afford her.

Let not thy tongue speak idle words,
Or thoughts to grieve or pain her;
But cheer her heart if lone or sad,
While you may yet retain her.

For soon her weary steps will pass
Beyond life’s busy portal,
And earth will hold one spirit less,
And heaven hold one immortal.

And then in all the coming years
That	hold	thy manhood’s glory,
You’ll see no more her smiles or tears,
Or listen to her story.
						MARY.


CLIMBING UP.
I am trying to climb up higher;
	The ladder of truth and light
Is pointing to God and heaven,
	And reaching beyond my sight.

Trying to climb up the ladder;
	Only one step at a time,
With Self climbing closer than ever;
	The ladder steeper to climb.

I tried to leave Self at the bottom.
	And take only Truth for a guide;
But then when I make my best efforts,
	I often find Self by my side.

If I could see over the ladder,	
	Or how many steps to the top;
If I knew that I never should falter,
	Or lose my foothold and drop,

I should be more sure of my climbing,
	But this I never can know;
I must go on trying and striving,
	Be my progress ever so slow.

I think I can see bending over,
	The face of the Father above;
He beckons, and whispers “Come hither,
	I’ll save thee by infinite love."

How gracious, how precious His promise,
	I trust Him, he never will fail;
If I only have strength to continue,
	I think I will surely prevail.

So many are climbing up with me,
	We’ll lend each other a hand
When we come to the steepest places,
	And do the best that we can.

We will lead the young and the tender,
	Put out a hand to the old,
As we climb up the shining ladder,
	The ladder brighter than gold.
					ESTHER H. BENNION
February 17th, 1883.


A TRIBUTE
		TO HANNAH T. KING.
Once more, dear muse, I pray thee, come to me,
And tune thy lyre, and let thy music flow,
In praise of her, whose soothing melody
Hath lightened many a heavy load of woe.

Her themes are not the deeds of mighty men—
The heroes of the crimson battle-fields— 
She sings of those whose lowly lives have been
Crowned with the glory that all goodness yields.

She sings of those whom poverty and care,
And all the changing evils of their lot,
Have sought in vain their weary feet to tear
From that sweet rest they have so dearly bought.

She sings of those whose gentle lives are given
To deeds of mercy and to words of peace;
O, may she sing, when life’s frail bands are riven,
With those whose songs of joy shall never cease.
							SILAS SELLER.


“A BEAUTIFUL REFLECTION.”

   It cannot be that earth is man’s only abiding
place. It cannot be that our life is a bubble,
cast up by the ocean of eternity, to float a few
moments on its waves, and then sink into
nothingness. Else why is it that the high and
glorious aspirations, which leap like angels
from the temple of our heart, are forever wan-
dering about unsatisfied? Why is it that the
rainbow and the cloud come over us with a
beauty that is not of earth, and then pass off
and leave us to muse upon their faded loveli-
ness? Why is it that the stars hold “their festi-
val around the midnight throne,” and set
above the grasp of our limited faculties, forever
mocking us with their unapproachable glory?
And finally, why is it that the bright forms of
human beauty are presented to our view, and
then taken from us—leaving the thousand
streams of our affections to flow back in Alpine
torrents upon our hearts? We are born for a
higher destiny than that of earth. There is a
realm where the rainbow never fades, where
the stars will spread out before us like islands
that slumber on the ocean, and where the
beautiful beings, which here pass before us like
shadows, will stay in our presence forever.
							TRUTH.


A WOMAN’S LETTER.

Laurel, Franklin Co., Indiana,
March 28th, 1883.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	Having read your paper, sent me by an
Elder of your Church, who happened to be in
this section of country preaching, whom my
husband invited home with him to stay over
night, from a meeting held in this place in
October last, viz., S. R. Marks, I admit my
mind has been much enlightened and I must
say that I admire the tone of your paper, and
confess that it has done me much good. I
have always heard so much evil of your peo-
ple, that I was like many others, rather preju-
auced against your religion, but I am happy to
say through the preaching of Elder Marks and
reading of your paper, that prejudice is entirely
removed from my mind.
	I was always taught to believe that our sex
were very much abused, and kept in abject
slavery among the “Mormons,” but I am
pleased to learn from Elder Marks, and by
reading your paper, that it is the reverse; for I
find that your people are now fighting with the
arm of the law, and the word of truth, for the
rights of women; while those who have been
crying slavery, are the ones who are trying to
enslave you.
	I am not of your faith, but I must confess
it is Scriptural, and it must triumph over the
false systems of to-day, and I hope that all


those who fight against the liberty of your peo-
ple will come to shame and dismay; for liberty
is guaranteed to all by our glorious Constitu-
tion, and belongs to Utah’s people as much as
any in this, or any other section of our common
country, and should be enjoyed by its citizens
in common with ours.
	Wishing the greatest success for your valu-
able paper, and your triumph over the enemies
of liberty,
		I remain	respectfully yours,	
MRS. JOHN LEWIS JOHNSON.


NOTES AND NEWS.
The graduating class of the Chicago
Woman’s Medical College numbered eighteen
this year.

	Miss Susan B. Anthony reached England
safely on March 3rd. Her address is “Ameri-
can Exchange, 449 Strand, London, England."

	One hundred and seventy colleges in the
United States practically believe in co-educa-
tion.

	After grave deliberation Columbia College
decides to adopt the next thing to, opening her
doors to women students—an annex.

	Mrs. Lorreta Brownlow, of East Paw Paw,
Ills., has patented, a simple and convenient
machine for crushing and straining fruit for
making jellies.

	Anna K. Hawley, of Delhi, Louisiana, has
patented a button that can be readily attached
to garments without sewing and readily re-
moved without injury.

	The invention of the cotton gin, which
revolutionized the industries of the world was
due to a woman, though the patent was taken
out by a man.

	The woman's department of the coming in-
stitute fair at Boston has organized, with Mrs.
Julia Ward Howe, president, Mrs. S. E. B. Chan-
ning, secretary. and Abby W. May, treasurer.

	A co-operative store and woman’s labor
exchange has been organized for the benefit of
the working women of Washington City.
They keep on hand all kinds of fancy goods
and bric-a-brac; all kinds of sewing and fancy
work is made to order at short notice; and help
supplied for all kinds of labor, such as sewing,
sales-ladies, clerks, copying, and good domestic
help for housework. It is in charge of Mrs.
M. A. Butler, 516 Eleventh street, N. W.
Washington, D. C.


SISTER POLLY STEIN GAM.

   Sister Stringam is well known to the Latter-
day Saints as one of its oldest members and
her many friends will be glad to know of her
health and prosperity. Her birthday was cele-
brated at her home in Holden, Millard Co., on
Tuesday, April 3rd, when she completed her
80th year. A number of her family connec-
tions, of whom there were four of her children,
twenty-one grand children and eight great
grand children, called to see her. Besides
these a large number of personal friends were
in the company. The venerable lady was the
recipient of many loving congratulations, ac-
companied by tokens of affection in the shape
of presents. In the morning she had been
feeling somewhat feeble, but tno troupe of lov-
ing ones with their pleasant greetings quite
revived her aged heart and made her feel
much stronger. Sister Stringam was associa-
ted with the Church in the days of Kirtland,
and has remained steadfast and true.

SALT LAKE CITY, APRIL 15, 1883.


FIFTY-THIRD ANNUAL CONFERENCE.

	THE General Conference of the Church, held
in this city, commencing with preliminary
meetings on Thursday, April 5th, and the regu-
lar Conference commencing April 6th, and con-
tinuing over Sunday, was in our opinion the
largest and grandest assembly of the Latter-day
Saints that ever convened at any time in the
history of the Church. The weather, part of
the time, was not warm enough to admit of
opening the large Tabernacle, and the meetings
were necessarily held in the Assembly Hall, and
thousands of people could not gain admittance,
but most of the time the immense Tabernacle
was open and well filled. On Sunday it was
crowded to its utmost capacity, and many hun-
dreds could not get in at all.
	There has been a general feeling of satisfaction
with the people, an appreciation of the great
blessings enjoyed through the providences of
God, and a desire manifested by the Saints to
persevere in the path of duty, to grow rich in
faith and excel in good works, keeping the com-
mandments of God and rendering obedience to
his laws, and trusting in Him for deliverance
from those who oppose his work upon the 
earth.
	The brethren who addressed the people
seemed filled with the spirit of their calling in
the priesthood and the power of God rested upon
them, and the immense congregations heard-and
felt the influence. The preaching was by the
Twelve Apostles and First Presidency of the
Church, and certainly was impressive and power-
ful. On the last afternoon of the Conference
President Taylor spoke of business matters and
gave his reasons why financial statements were
not submitted to the Conference, which was
satisfactory, and a vote was taken on the matter,
that proved unanimous. President Taylor oc-
cupied the remainder of the afternoon, speaking
with great power and grandeur. He referred
the people to the fact of their almost marvelous
deliverance, and called on the congregation for
an audible acknowledgement of the hand of
God, in delivering his people from the hands of
their enemies. When all that grand congrega-
tion responded to the "Amen" feelingly uttered by
the President; the impression was thrilling and
touched every heart, for every Latter-day Saint
could feel that this weak expression of gratitude
was only a feeble recognition of the manifes-
tation of the mighty power that can turn and
overturn nations and kingdoms at his will.
   To those who have eyes to see and hearts to
understand, it must be apparent that there is a
power with this people that the world cannot
understand or comprehend. Who would have
thought, except a Latter-day Saint, that so little
harm would have been effected, by the passage
of the Edmunds bill? And yet the facts are be-
fore the people, Zion continues to grow and in-


crease, the Gospel is being preached in every
land, and the honest-hearted gathered home to
the mountains. Temples are being erected to
the Most High, where the Saints can perform a
work for the living and the dead, that Israel may
be redeemed and all things prepared for the time
when the lamb and the lion shall lie down to-
gether, when Satan shall no longer have power
to afflict those who serve the living and true
God, and peace shall reign in the hearts of all
men and over all the earth.


﻿CONSTITUTIONAL CONVENTION.

	The Constitutional Convention met pursuant
to adjournment at 2 p.m. April 10th in the City
Hall, Hon. Joseph F. Smith in the chair. The
roll was called and it was found there was a
quorum present. The chaplain offered prayer.
Minutes of last meeting read and approved.
	Hon. D. H. Wells, chairman of the committee
on statistics and other essential matter, reported
that a document had been prepared, which he
submitted for the approval of the convention.
It was read by the secretary, received and adopt-
ed by the convention, and 1,000 copies ordered to
be printed for distribution.
	Hon. John T. Caine then presented a report
of the delegates, accompanied by the memorial
which had been presented in both houses of
Congress, and published in the Congressional
Record; these documents were also read by the
secretary, and a motion made and carried to
have them printed and published with the re-
port of the committee on statistics.
	A vote of thanks was given to the Washing-
ton delegation, and they were highly compli-
mented on the able paper preparod as a memo-
rial or a petition to Congress.
	Hon. F. S. Richards, in behalf of the delega-
tion, made a very happy and appropriate res-
ponse to the compliment tendered the delegates,
and recounted a few of the difficulties under
which they labored to bring the matter before
Congress at its last session.
	In the report of the progress of the committee
by the Hon. John T. Caine, the fact of the
death of Hon. Wm. H. Hooper, chairman of
the delegation, was alluded to. This reminded
the president of the vacancy and he made a
suggestion to the convention, that they take
the matter into consideration. One or two
members offered motions and others amend-
ments to the motions; but it was finally decided
that as the committee on nominations previous-
ly appointed in convention assembled, had not
been discharged, this committee should be pre-
pared with a nomination to fill the vacancy re-
ferred to at the next meeting of the conven-
tion. Carried.
	A motion made by Hon. Hosea Stout, that
when the convention adjourn it be until the
9th of October next at 10 o’clock in the morn-
ing, prevailed.
	Hon. D. H. Wells made some very sensible
remarks upon the political situation of Utah,
the constitutional rights of the people, their
heritage of religious liberty guaranteed by the
constitution, and the attempts of some of the
members of Congress to pass unconstitutional
and aggressive laws to oppress a free people
and bring them into bondage; he spoke of the
attempt to annul the woman’s suffrage act;
to disfranchise every class of citizens they pos-
sibly could in order to further the schemes of
some men who desire to obtain possession of
the offices of the Territory.
	Hon. John T. Caine made a few explanations
of matters of public interest that had not been
quite understood in regard to men and measures,
and which it was necessary should be made
clear in connection with what had been spoken.


﻿Hon F, S. Richards added a few words and
made some positive statements on the subjects
before the convention. Minutes were called for,
read, and ordered to be printed.
   The convention then adjourned until October
9th, at 10 a.m.


ARTISTIC ENTERTAINMENT.

   The Unity Club entertainment given in the
Theatre Monday evening, April 9th, was an
unqualified success. The programme had been
arranged with great care, and the members of
the club and other performers were diligent in
their preparations beforehand and they were well
repaid for their labor in the appreciation of their
efforts by the general public. The ladies of the
Unity Club are possessed of artistic, dramatic and
musical talent, taste, tact and executive ability
which enables them to carry out an entertain-
ment with but very little outside help. Bishop
H. B. Clawson has been specially interested in
helping the club, partly from a genuine love and
taste for the drama and stage, and also from the
goodness of his heart and a desire to aid the
sick and unfortunate. On the last occasion of
the benefit of the Deseret Hospital he was ma-
terially aided by Henry McEwan, Esq., who has
in days gone by been much accustomed to the
theatre and familiar with all details of the
mechanical work. The programme of the Unity
Club was one which could scarcely fail to draw
and certainly evinced taste of a high order.
The music was the best that the city affords,
and the singing was from some of the sweetest
songsters in these mountain vales, Miss Agnes
Olsen, Miss Nellie Druce, Mrs. S. E. Langford,
Mrs. Maggie Hull and Mrs. Edith Clawson
Knowlton. The piano solo by Master Joseph
McIntyre deserves special mention on his own
account and that of his teacher. Messrs. Care-
less, Carrington and Clive rendered their music
in the most approved and pleasing style. The
tableaux were all attractive and beautiful, fore-
most among them being four seasons, represent-
ed by Lutie Thatcher, Spring; Louie Wells,
Summer; Dalla Cummings, Autumn; and Clara
Felt, Winter. Their costumes were exquisite.
   The costumes for representation of character
in the tableaux were exquisitely arranged and
everything denoted cultivation of a high order.
Among the tableaux of a historical character
were Amy Robsart, groups of Statuary, Boadicea,
Evangeline and Joan of Arc, Cornelia, mother
of the Gracchi, Children, Early at the Cross,
Niobe and children, The Tomb Revisited, Othel-
lo and Desdemona, Faust’s vision of Margue-
rite, Execution of Lady Jane Grey. Besides
these were the Good old days of Yore, Love and
Duty, Tableau from Patience.
   In the comic, Lord Ullin's Daughter, The
Old Woman who lived in a Shoe, and Oft in the
Stilly Night. These were so exceedingly funny
as to provoke the entire audience to laughter
and loud and continuous applause. The reading
by Miss Julia Thomas was much better than at
her first appearance. Her selections were such
as showed to more advantage, her culture in the
beautiful art of elocution. She has a clear and
expansive voice, and is perfectly at ease on the
platform.
   Miss Ada Dwyer, one of our home artists, is
a great favorite on the Salt Lake platform.
Her voice is fresh and sweet and her style is
very pleasing and attractive. She impresses her
audience powerfully. She recited Jamie so
effectively that it seemed real. There is no
doubt that she will attain to marked distinction
in the profession of elocution.
   The Unity Club has certainly made a success
in this last entertainment, and the members
have every encouragement for future efforts in


the direction of culture and benevolent enler-
prise.
   Bishop H. B. Clawson and Henry McEwan
rendered the most valuable assistance, otherwise
the ladles would have never been able to man-
age all the stage arrangements so admirably.
   Success to the Unity Club, and may it con-
tinue to prosper and accomplish good results.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿DESERET HOSPITAL.

 REPORT TO APRIL CONFERENCE, 1883.

   Financial report of the Deseret Hospital from
October 6th, 1882, to April 6th, 1883, inclusive.
October 6th, 1882, to balance: cash, $1,858.00; or
ders, $103.20. Receipts during the six months,
cash,1,815.25; orders, $37.25; groceries, $47.50.
Received in fees per patients, cash, $483.10; or-
ders, $270.35; produce, $33.62 1/2; on a cow, $33.00.
Total, $825.07. Total cash to date, $4,101.85.
Total cash expense, $3,143.63; cash on hand, $1,
017.72. Total expense in orders, produce and
groceries, $129.68 1/2; orders on hand, $62.34. Total
expense, cash, orders, etc., $3,573.31 1/2; average
expense per month, $593.55 1/2. Fees to physi-
cians, $490.00; matron and helps,$734.00. Rent
for building, $360.00.
   In addition to the amounts donated in cash,
orders, etc., there have been donations of coal,
five tons, three stoves, a liberal quantity of wines
and brandy, towels, blankets, quilts, pillow-
slips and bedding of all kinds. One cow and
calf, one pig and quite a number of chickens.
One quarter of beef and one of venison and
many other useful articles. The names of the
donors would fill a pamphlet. Of the money
given, the Unity Club gave the largest amount
during the past six months. The Primary As-
sociation of the Eleventh Ward gave $41.00.
From party in the Fourteenth Ward by the
Relief Society and Young Ladies Association,
$20.00. Donation by Joab Lawrence, $100, at
New Year’s.
   The people have been liberal and the Execu-
tive Board feel encouraged in their labors. It is
expected that an annual meeting of the officers
and members of the Deseret Hospital Associa-
tion will be held on the 12th of May next in the
Council House in this city at 2 p.m, to transact
business pertaining to the Association. Re-
spectfully submitted.
						E.	B.	Wells,
								Secretary.
Apil 6th, 1883.

our next number.

count of tbe real name of the writer not being
given.

and other matters which are not published will
be printed in process of time.

	THE Annual meeting of the Primary Aasscio-
tion of the Thirteenth Ward will be held in the
Thirteenth Ward Assembly Rooms on Friday,
April 19th, at 3 o’clock p.m, Brothren and Sis-
ters interested in these meetings are invited to
attend.
 AMONG other ways in which the Auerbach
Bros, celebrated the wedding of their niece, Miss
Meyer, to Mr. Barnett, on Monday, was by
presenting to the Deseret Hospital a check for
$50, and wishing the long continued usefulness
of that benevolent institution.


Evan Stephens’ Concert in the Salt Lake
Theatre, March 17th, which was repeated in
the Salt Lake Assembly Hall, April 5th, was a
grand success. The most beautiful sight one
could imagine was two hundred little choristers
on the platform, their sweet, fresh young voices
making harmonious melody in the most appro-
priate pieces. Mr. Stephens richly deserves the
congratulations that are showered upon him
from all sides. He has done a wonderful work,
in training the little ones in the beautiful science
of music, and he never wearies of his continuous
labors in that direction. Mr. Stephens is, with-
out doubt, a genius, and he composes music
with the greatest ease. He has ability and per-
severance, and with these he ought to excel in
the rank of composers.


﻿A WOMAN’S TESTIMONY.

	How thankful we should be, as Latter-day
Saints, that we are permitted to live on the
earth in this our day, when the words of the
prophets of old are being fulfilled every day be_
fore our eyes, and that the power of Jehovah is
felt on the earth; for are we not the favored of
the Lord? Is He not stretching out his mighty
arm for our deliverance? Should we not be
proud of our name and station? What should
be our bearing before the world? We should
not give way to pride and folly, but seek to
serve the Lord our God with full purpose of
heart; a prayer should be ever on our lips.
Righteousness and truth should be our motto;
seek ever to draw unto Him who has said, “I
am the Lord thy God.” The earth is His and
the fulness thereof, the cattle on a thousand
hills; and wo are His Saints; and has He not
promised, in his own due time He would give
all to His people, but we must live faithful
before Him, Saints in very deed; shall we deny
any principle He has revealed for our exalta-
tion? No, verily, no! for have we not a testi-
mony within our hearts, that every principle is
heaven born; for many years have I lived in
plural marriage; as well might you tell me that
the sun would stand still at my bidding as tell
me Celestial marriage is not true, it is the grand
high school to exaltation in our Father’s King-
dom, where we shall meet with our Elder
Brother, even Jeses Christ, who sitteth on the
right hand of God, who has said, “Fear not,
little flock, for I have overcome the world.”
There is no happier lot than that of a true Lat-
ter-day Saint. Let us do the part assigned unto
us well, that it may be said unto us, “Well
done, good and faithful servant, enter thou into
the joy of thy Lord.” God bless you, dear Edi-
tor, and all your co-workers who write so good
for our dear little paper.			B. M. B.
	Richmond, April 4th, 1883.

was inserted December 1st, please correct. For Rosina

town of Denmark, Lewis County, State of New York;
and oblige your sister in the Gospel,	 B. BROWNER.


[Column 1]
﻿OBITUARIES.
Died, at Fillmore, Febuary 7th, 1883, Sister Elizabeth
Richmond, daughter of William and Mary Green. Born
October 2nd, 1806, at Lugwardine, Herefordshire, Eng-
land. Became a member of this Church in 1810. Emigrated
to America with Apostle Orson Hyde’s company, on his
return from Jerusalem, ariving at St. Louis late in the
fall; remained there until Spring, then proceeded to
Nauvoo; there she was married to Thomas Richmond
and resided until the expulsion of the Saints, in which

[Column 2]
she suffered severely. After burying her husband on
Musquitoe Creek, emigrated to Utah in 1851, making her
home in Provo until 1854, when she moved to Fillmore,
where she has since resided until her demise. Her quiet,
peaceful countenance bespoke the joy aud satisfaction
she experienced in the everlasting Gospel. she was a
faithful member in the Relief Society, and with the
“widow’s mite” was ever ready with untiring zeal to as-
sist others. Her death leaves her aged sister very lonely,
as their lives have been so closely connected, sharing
each other’s joys and sorrows for so many years, that a
separation was painful, and the entire community
extend their heartfelt sympathies to the bereaved and
pray the consoling influence of the Holy Spirit, may be
with her in this hour of affliction. The funeral services
took place in the meeting house, where a very large as-
sembly met to pay their last respects to the departed.
D. K. O. 

Died, at Lake View, Tooele. County, March 6th, 1883,
of child-bed fever, Jane Adamson, beloved wife of Robert
C. Shields, and daughter of David and Jane Adamson.
Born February 11th, 1846, in Rumford, Stirlingshire,
Scotland. Emigrated to Utah with her parents in
1862.
She has been a faithful Latter-day Saint, and a true
member of the Relief Society ot the Lake View Ward.
She was a loving wife and mother, and leaves a husband
and eight children and a host of friends to mourn her
loss.	Relief Society Teachers.

 Died, at Malad City, Oneida Co., Idaho Territory,
Emily Jane Evans, daughter of Leonidas Clinton and
Margrett Emma Mecham, born April 19th, 1863, at
Richville, Morgan Co., baptized into the Church of Jesus
Christ of Latter-day Saints, when eight years old; re-
baptized by her own desire in 1874. Died, March 25th,
1883, of convulsions, being nineteen years, eleven
months and six days old. She leaves a loving husband,
a little babe of a few hours, and a large circle of rela-
tives and friends to mourn her loss.
All who became aquainted with Sister Emily loved
her, for her amiable, cheerful, and happy disposition,
dispersing clouds of gloom with the merry sunshine in
her loving heart. She was Assistant Secretary to the
Y. L. M. I. A., which she filled with credit; she also took
an active part in our ward choir, and filled our hearts
with joy in listening to the songs of Zion as they flowed
from her lips; and though we sympathize with the
bereaved relatives, and acknowledge the hand of God
our Eternal Father in all the trials and vicissitudes of
life, yet we can say with the poet,
“Dearest sister thou hast left us,
And thy loss we deeply feel,
Yet ’tis God that has bereft us,
He can all our sorrows heal.
“Yet again we hope to see thee,
When death’s gloomy night has fled,
Then on earth with joy to greet thee,
Where no bitter tears are shed.”
Anne Sawyer,

Died, in St. George, of old age, Dioy Ray Perkins,
beloved wife of William G. Perkins, of St. George, Utah
Deceased was born August 12th, 1796, in Patrick Co.,
Virginia. Sister Perkins embraced the Gospel in 1840,
in MacDonald, Hancock Co., Illnois. Brother and Sister
Perkins emigrated to Utah In 1848, bringing with them
an only daughter, who is living in St. George, and who
has ever been devotedly attached to her mother, and her
constant companion through all the trying scenes of
travels, journeyings, and the sorrows of life. Aunt
Dicy, as she was affectionately styled by her friends
both young and old, was one of the most kind-hearted
and generous women that ever lived. In sickness she
was an invaluable friend; thousands in this city can
testify to her devotion in times of extreme trial, and
when sickness and death invaded the homes, Aunt Dicy
was sent for; she practiced as a midwife many, many
years, and was very sucessful indeed as a practitioner.
Warm hearted and sympathetic in her nature, tender to-
ward all, charitable and forbearing, a Latter-day Saint
in every sense of the word. Aunt Dicy died full of
integrity and rich in the faith of the new and overlast-
ing covenant; aged eighty-seven years six months and
twenty-one days. She retained her senses till the last
moment, and gave directions to her daughter Patty,
who she would Iike to have to attend to the last offi-
ces to be performed for her when she was gone. Sister
Perkins suffered much foreight weeks in her last illness,
“Blessed are the dead that die in the Lord,”


....We extend, our heartfelt sympathy to the bereaved
husband and family, and especially to the daughter who
is left so lonely by the mother’s death.
									E. B. W.

	Polly Ann Lane Greer was born in Washington Co.,
Texas, May 1st, 1849, and was married to A. V. Greer
November 2nd 1865, according to the laws of the State
in which they resided, (for time.) On the 2nd day of
July, 1878, they were sealed for time and all eternity ac-
cording to the celestial law of marriage, in the St.
George Temple. Sister Greer was baptized by Elder
Israel D. Alphin, April 18th, 1865, in Bosque Co., Texas.
Brother and Sister Greer emigrated to Utah in 1877, and
to Arizona in 1878. Sister Greer died at Amity, Apache
Co., Arizona, November 28th, 1883, at 6.20.a.m., of con-
sumption. She was a true Latter-day Saint, and re-
ceived with joy all the principles of the Gospel.
	She was set apart as President of the Primary Associa-
tion of this place November 2nd, 1882; she was an affec-
tionate and dutiful companion, a kind mother, and
truely a good neighbor. She departed in peace and full
faith of the Gospel, and an assurance of eternal life in
the Kingdom of God, where she will eventually be re-
united with her husband and little ones, and beloved
brethren and sisters who had not forsaken her in the
dark valley and the shadow of death. She was an ex-
emplary woman and beloved by all. She was the
mother of six children, three girls and three boys. Her
last words to her husband were, I want you to live your
religion and get another wife, one that will suit you.
To her eldest girl Mollie she said, Are you prepared
to give your mother up? the child sobbed no! then she
counseled her to take care of the little brothers and
sisters that would be left lonely at her death, she en-
treated her to be reconciled, and said she must be a good
girl. To her next daughter Sue, she said, I love you Sue,
and then her spirit departed so quickly it could hardly
be realized.							COM.

﻿R S., Y. L. M. I. A. AND PRIMARY
			REPORTS.
			    _____
		MESA CITY, ARIZONA.
	Some time since initiatory steps were taken
to organize a Y. L. M. I. A., and on January
12th, 1883, the organization was effected, with
the following officers, who were set apart by
President A. F. Macdonald, Bros. Richens,
Stewart and Carter, viz., President, Mrs., Zulie
Kimball; Counselors, Miss Etta Pomeroy and
Miss Beckie Richens; Secretary, Miss Gertrude
Pomeroy; Corresponding Secretary, Mrs. Jennie
Bush; Treasurer, Phoebe Shepard.
	Our society meets weekly, and from the
energetic zeal manifested we hope to attain a
higher degree of excellence in the scale of
intellect.
				JENNIE BUSH,
		Corresponding Secretary for Y. L. A.
				_____
	   WEST BOUNTIFUL PRIMARY.
	Our Association is not a large one in point
of numbers, but the little folks are striving to
improve themselves, and most of them are
much interested. We have had two Primary
concerts here for the benefit of the Association,
which were well attended. They were a great
encouragement to the children, and reflected
credit upon their endeavors. Praying that
the Lord will bless all the Primary associations
and the efforts of those who are laboring in the
good cause.
		MISS JULIA FACKRELL,
							Secretary.
				_____
		AMITY, ARIZONA.
	On November 2nd, 1882, a special meeting
was called in Amity, Arizona, and Bishop
Albinon M. Holden organized a Relief Society
with the following officers, who were unani-
mously sustained by the vote of the people:
Mrs. Martha Bigelow, President; Susan John-
son and Lucinda Holden, Counselors; Mary
Emily Marble, Secretary; Celestina Marble,
Treasurer. Nineteen members are enrolled


and four teachers have been appointed to visit
among the sisters. We hold our meetings
twice a month at 2 o'clock p.m.
	A Young Ladies’ Association has also been
organized with Julia A. Johnson, President;
Lucy Norton and Melvina Gunn, Counselors;
Ellen Bigelow, Secretary; L. Jackson, Assis-
tant Secretary; and Maria S. Holden, Treasu-
rer. There are fourteen members enrolled.
	A Primary Association was organized with
Mrs. Polly Ann Greer, President; Priscilla
Hamblin and Lora A. Brown, Counselors;
Susan Greer, Secretary; and Ella Hamblin,
Treasurer. Thirty-four members enrolled.
The Association hold their meetings every
Saturday at 2 o’clock and they are generally
well attended.
	The sisters are diligent and faithful in the
performance of their duties, the Bishop and a
few of the other brethren occasionly visit our
meetings and counsel and encourage us. Al-
ways praying for the welfare of Zion and the
advancement of mankind, including the eleva-
tion of woman, and also for the success of the
EXPONENT as the advocate of woman’s cause,
I have the pleasure to subscribe myself,
			Yours in the Gospel,
				MARY E. MARBLE,
						Secretary.


_______________________________
﻿PRINCESS MARY, DAUGHTER OF
              CHARLES I.
                      ______
          BY HANNAH T. KING.
                      ______
The royal family of Stewart were remarka-
ble for their beauty, for many fine qualities,
but most of all for their misfortunes! The
Princess Mary, daughter of Charles I, was
an exception in the catalogue, and yet little
has been historically written of her; while
other princesses’ lives have been traced on
the page of history again, and again; she had
a little sister Elizabeth, who died at Carris-
brook Castle while a child, and Queen
Victoria has had a lovely monument erected
to her memory in St. Thomas’s Church, Isle
of Wight. It will be remembered that
Charles I. married a beautiful French Prin-
cess, named Henrietta Maria, the youngest
daughter of Henry IV of France, and his
Queen Mariede Medices; she was not consider-
ed a very wise or suitable wife for an English
king, partly on account of her being a zealous
Roman Catholic, and that her training and
education unfitted her for so important a
position; but she was beautiful, and the king
loved her, and she returned his love. Her
eldest son, afterwards Charles II of England,
was born at Greenwich; his mother declared
him to be an ugly, though a very fine child,
and so solemn in his infant ways, that she felt
he was far wiser than herself. The next ar-
rival was a little daughter, born at Whitehall,
London, on the 4th of Nov., 1631, and was
warmly welcomed by both parents, but she
was a very delicate child, and so caused them
much anxiety; on this account they had her
baptized when only a few days old, by the
name of Maria, after her grandmother; they
were very poor at this time, and it suited their
circumstances to have a private christening, as
the Parliament would not vote more supplies
for them. The child was provided with a
nurse, and the Countess of Roxburgh was her
governess from the first day of her life! When
two years old, an establishment was arranged
for her, which certainly did not indicate pov-
erty; besides a nurse and three watchers, a
lady of the bedchamber, a seamstress, a tailor
and shoemaker, a gentleman usher, two pages,
a laundress, three grooms, four footmen, five
kitchen servants, a coachman, and more im-
portant than all, a good physician, Dr. Mayer,
whose skill and care were supposed to have
saved her life when quite an infant, and con-
sequently was much esteemed by the king,
her father, who was obliged to go to Scotland
soon after Mary’s birth, and so was particular-
ly anxious that her health should be strictly
attended to in his absence; the diet provided
for the little lady’s nursery was more substan-
tial than is procurable for children in this age,
being “manchet and fine bread, mutton or
chicken with all,” and the breakfast must have
been early, as it is reported the children dined
between ten and twelve, their dinner was game
and meat of various kinds, custards and sweet 
tarts, and Gascony wine; on fast days fish
instead of meat, supper the same, but not quite
so heavy. They lived at St. James’s Palace,
London, the greater part of the year, which
was a favorite place with the queen, their
mother, and she loved to walk in the beautiful
garden with the children, carrying the baby
in her arms, singing to it, and talking and
laughing with them.
  Charles I. was anxious that his children
should all speak English as their mother-
tongue, though his wife gave him some trouble
by her preference for her native  language, but
as her sons grew up and visited the French
court, they were unable to converse except in
English! The queen was, of course, a Roman
Catholic, and used to give  Mary rosaries and
crosses, and to take her into the chapel for
mass; when Mary was eight years old the king
found out what was going on, and positively
forbade her being taken there again, and in-
sisted that nothing should ever be said to her
against the protestant faith. She grew to be
a pretty child, and a devoted daughter to her
kind and good father her brother Charles
was her favorite companion.
  A very important event in Mary’s life was
brought about in consequence of her grand-
mother, Marie de Medicie, visiting England;
she came without being invited because it
happened to be convenient to her; this visit
was not an agreeable one, though the queen
was pleased to present her children to her
mother: and she told her “the king had made
her a happy wife, happy mother and a happy
queen!” But even at this time trouble was
gathering around the king, who, while this
happy reunion was taking place, was marching
at the head of his army against his Scotch
subjects, causing his family much sorrow and
anxiety; little Mary began to notice day after
day the grief on her mother's face and the
tears shed by her governess, the countess of
Roxburgh, who had been a second mother to
her, and whom she loved very much, but
whose Husband and son were fighting against
the king; and they missed their pleasant
walks at Richmond or Hampton Court, and
thought it very strange and disagreeable never
to be allowed to go out of doors without a guard
of armed soldiers; even at night her sleep was
disturbed by the tramp of the sentries at the
gates, and the poor child began to look care-
worn like the queen, her mother; at this time,
though so young, a way was already being
made for her to escape from the evils that were
coming on her father’s house; for Marie de
Medicie was the bearer of a message from the
Prince of Orange, asking her in marriage for
his son. Charles I. was very willing to listen
to such proposals for his younger daughters,
but he had more ambitious views for Mary,
besides which he loved her so much, that
he shrank from parting with her.	But
he found that large offers of wealth
were made for the bride, and that the Prince
of Orange would undertake to assist him in
his struggle with his rebellious subjects, and,
moreover, the little girl, who perhaps under-
stood what she had seen with her parents,
that difference in religion caused much misery
between husband and wife, told her father
that she should prefer being married to a


[Column 1]

﻿protestant partner. This decided the matter,
and in April, 1641, the young prince came
over, escorted by twenty vessels, and landed
at Gravesend, where he was met by a royal
equipage, and driven through London to
Whitehall where Charles and Henrietta re-
ceived him most affectionately; Mary was not
there, for she was in bed, poor child, at Somer-
set House with a feverish cold and swollen
face, but her bridegroom of sixteen begged so
hard to be permitted to see her, that the king
and queen took him thither, and the prince
was introduced to her by them, and he kissed
the little girl in spite of her swollen face, and
told his father in his first letter that she was
sweet and lovely; although young, he was very
manly in his manners and was highly accom-
plished, spoke four European languages, a good
mathematician, well acquainted with history,
both of his own country and that of others,
and had long been admitted to sit in the coun-
cils of the state, and Mary, from sympathy
with the trials of those she loved, was older
than her years. Arundel House in the Strand
had been fitted up for his reception, and he
had a private key to the garden of Somerset
House. A frequent interchange of visits took
place, and marriage was proposed and earnest-
ly insisted upon; the king objected in consid-
eration of the youth of his daughter: but, alas!
heavy clouds had long been gathering around
him and his throne, and he had much desired
to find a safe asylum for his beloved family,
for Charles was a devoted husband and father;
it was now proposed by the Prince of Orange,
the prince's father, that the marriage should
be solemnized, that the young princess should
still remain under the care and jurisdiction of
her mother for two years, that the queen
should accompany the young bride to Hol-
land, and at the expiration of the two years,
she should pass to the house of her husband.
Charles at last bowed to a dire necessity that
surrounded him and his family; the marriage
took place with the usual court ceremonies, as
soon as preparations could be made ready.
A royal vessel was duly fitted out, and then
came the cruel separation, the king was per-
fectly unmanned at parting with his almost
worshiped child, for having a mind beyond
her years she had been the solace and comfort
of his troubled life; he repeatedly clasped her
in his arms, blest her and wept bitter tears
over her; probably he had a presentiment that
their separation was final. As long as the
vessel was in sight he stood on the shore mak-
ing fond signals of adieus, he then called for
a swift horse that he might rapidly ride to a
high eminence that still commanded a view of
the ship that bore away his heart's treasures!
Never to meet again on earth! Time passed—
the final tragedy of regicide is history. Mary
mourned the sad, sad finale of her beloved
father, but her mother and her husband blest
and consoled her; the two years with her
mother was religiously kept sacred and then
she was placed in the beautiful home that had
been fondly prepared for her by the hand of
love. Alas, a very few years elapsed before
this lovely and chaste union was dispelled by
the cruel hand of death, the young prince
was seized with malignant smallpox, and left
Mary a widow at nineteen.
	At the restoration of Charles the Second,
he invited his widowed neice and her boy to
come and make her home in England, she
accepted the kindly invitation, and Whitehall,
the home of her childhood, was appointed for
her residence. (A window is still shown in
that fine building from which Charles I.
emerged to walk to the fatal block, from
whence he was ushered into eternity.)
	Mary had not resided long in the beautiful
home of her childhood, when she was attacked
by smallpox, which in a short time terminated
her life in the very bloom of womanhood,

[Column 2]

leaving her son, who in after years ascended
the throne of England by the title of William
III, having married Mary, the eldest daugh-
ter of the deposed King, James II. Parlia-
ment desired that her name should be conjoint-
ly blended with his in the government of the
kingdom, and hence they ascended the throne
unitedly as William and Mary.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


THE MARKET-PLACE AND THE PIC-
		TURES OF MUNICH.
				Munich, Oct. 7th, 1882.
	We have with us in this second visit to this
our favorite city of Germany, a dear friend
whose feet have never trod these old streets
before. The delight of the newcomer at the
place seems to quite keep pace with our own
satisfaction in showing its riches. We wax elo-
quent over the market-place; we coax our
friend through a quaint gateway, and then
lead on to the picturesque square where the old
women have set out their wares to the best
advantage. We hunt up one old woman in
particular. She has always been an especial
attraction to us. Her specialty, which she
keeps heaped up in little tubs in front of her,
is, in American parlance, “pot cheese.” Here
we buy it by the spoonful. It is one of our
luxuries. We indulge in it frequently; partly
to please the old woman, partly to please our
own taste. It reminds us of a lovely spot far
over the blue sea, a beautiful country home
where so many happy summer hours of our
early years were spent. Anything here which
recalls that home and those who were the
charm of the old house is a delight to us in
this far away land. The dear hands whose
loving ministrations wrought so unceasingly for
the comfort or the pleasure of all who had the
fortune to be gathered under her hospitable
roof, have long since ceased their labor, but
her gentle deeds and loving words follow us
even into highways and byways of the old
world.
	This market-place of Munich is particularly
quaint and attractive, large and irregular in
shape—it gives room for many a picturesque
group, which might well delight the heart of any
artist from our own shores whose eye should
fall upon the like for the first time.
	We leave the old square and its comical at-
tractions, and post off to the old Pinakothek.
How glad we are to get into it again. We
have been in Rome, in Florence, and in Venice
since we took our last look here. We have
seen a great deal that is very beautiful, but our
affection for the Munich Gallery is in no wise
shaken. There is certainly no collection of
Rubens’ works anywhere in Italy which can
equal in value and variety the one found here.
Murillo’s Italian children are like a magnet,
and draw us irresistibly to the further end of
the building. They are just as fascinating as
they were months ago. We find ourselves
among old friends. A catalogue is not in order
here for us. We can find all we want without.
We know just where to find the treasures of
Van Dych, and Titian, and Paul Veronese.
We feel as though we were wanderers who had
come home, and a great feast had been spread
for us. Rembrandt’s scenes from the passion
are as grand—grander in our eyes—than they
were before we went south. Del Sarto is as
full of sweetness in his holy family here as we
shall ever hope to find him. Wouverman,
Ruysdael, Brouer and Tenieas tempt us into
the cabinets with as eager steps as in the days
of our earlier aquaintance. Holbein, Durer,
Quinten, Matsys, are prominent in the front
rooms just as we left them—just as we must
leave them and all the treasures which come
after them—and go away soon, not to come
back again for a long, long time, if ever. It is
not so very easy to turn one’s back on the

[Column 2]
wealth of this old Pinakothek and walk quiet-
ly away, knowing in your own heart you may
never look upon its like again. Bavaria has
laid hold on many a rare treasure of art. She
keeps them with most religious care, and is a
jealous guardian of all the good gifts committed
to her charge. With reluctant feet we go out
of the doors of this famous building, grateful at
least for the small fraction of its reflected
beauty that our memory can carry over the
rest of our journey through life.
	In these, our farewell visits, we do not forget
the soldiers. We must see one more drill.
We find them “on duty,” drilling away, exactly
as we left them months ago. They are as
sturdy, strong and stalwart as ever. We act-
ually do believe if this German nation lived on
our side of the water we would be obliged to
keep a large standing army under rigorous
drill if we wanted to be sure of keeping them
at a respectful distance. The entire business of
this great kingdom seems to be the training of
soldiers. The “goose step” provokes anew to
laughter,
	In the afternoon we go to the National Mu-
seum, glad of even a hasty peep once more at
its contents. Weeks only could give one, by
patient study, a satisfactory idea of its vast col-
lections. We have become somewhat familiar
with the building and its wealth of rare antiqui-
ties, and we guide our friends on to its greatest
objects of interest in a sort of despairing con-
dition of mind because we can do but little
more than make a hasty excursion trip through
these wonderful rooms so full of the relics of
other centuries—rich mosaics, rare tapestry
from Raphael’s cartoons, Roman antiquities,
ceilings, marbles, casts, tombstones, jewels and
costumes—all showing the progress of civiliza-
tion, some from the earliest ages, others of more
recent date. In our own land, which is so new,
it would be quite out of the question for us to
understand how these aristocratic old nations
cling to their past. Their heroes have lived in
it. Their poets have sung of it. Everything
which belonged to it is sacred in their eyes.
This particular collection is almost inexhaust-
ible in interest and extent. We wander up
stairs and down, taking in what little we can
out of the great variety before us. The day
comes to an end as far as regular sight-seeing
is concerned.
	We are glad to drop into Cafe Lutz for a cup
of its excellent coffee and queer cakes. When
we are rested we go out for a stroll in the Hof-
garten, look at the historical frescoes in the ar-
cades which open out on to the garden and
walk over to the lower parade ground to see
what the soldiers are doing there, and we feel
after the day’s work that we have been here a
long time. We have renewed so many delight-
ful associations, we don’t quite credit the fact
that we have not yet slept in the old city since
our return.
	We go back to our quarters tired physically
and mentally. The good frau is waiting for us
She has one of her most tempting German
meals ready for our famished condition. She
refreshes us with a cup of tea and produces
Marie and Poots to our no small delight. They
are as bright and fresh, as cute and charming
as ever. They are a picture in themselves—
the sweetest, prettiest German children it is
possible to imagine. We were more than glad
to get hold of the dear little things again. It
was right restful to fall into the kindly frau’s
motherly care once more. The dining salon
looked as cheery as ever, and the valuable frieze,
painted by the artist son-in-law, still lent its
grace to this well-remembered room. We missed
the faces which had gathered round the table in
the winter time before. Nearly every one had
“gone on,” and we were left alone in our glory.
Many of these friends had met us more than
once during our wanderings; others shall we
hope to see again. The meetings are for the


most part better than the partings. It was
quite delightful to put our tired head on the fa-
miliar pillows once more, balance the comical
little feather bed over our feet and give our-
selves up to the contemplation of our surround-
ings. The next day we could devote to the
churches, which are more than ordinarily in-
teresting in this old catholic city. We found
our thoughts rather like dissolving views—now
one thing and now another—quaint gables,
marketwomen and costly relics faded out of
mind at last, leaving us fast asleep in Munich.
Ex.		RUTH RANDOM.


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

MEN'S RIGHTS.

	Men's rights! the reader is saying in sur-
prise, why we always supposed men not only
had rights, but the power to grant similar per-
sonal possessions to others. For men style
themselves "lords of creation," and boast on
Scriptural grounds that in their faces the
image of God dwells. And so it should.
	A maiden lady whose business had thrown
her much with that shrewd class of men called
lawyers, by whose craftiness, or her own ig-
norance, a handsome property suddenly van-
ished, bewailed the degeneracy of men and the
advantage they often took of poor, defenceless
women. "Well," answered the translator of
the law, "what's a woman that you should
make such a fuss of her?" "What's a man?"
tartly answered the aggrieved party. "A man!
a man! why a man's a man! The world couldn't
do without men; they build railroads, and
carry on great enterprises. But the women—
all they have to do is to mind the house and
be the mothers of men."
	A look of unutterable scorn, and as the in-
furiated woman departed, the final knell came,
"The mothers of men!"—looking the shrink-
ing individual from-head to foot—his narrow
soul blinking through his half shut eyes—
"what a mother you must have had!"
	And watching the faces of men, how often
are we impressed with the truth of these
words. In some the image of God—truth,
purity, mercy, justice, love, glorify them into
more than human (what a blessed mother was
theirs!); in others the image defaced by lines
of sin, selfishness and low desires. ( God pity
your mothers, poor men, for ye are legoin!)
	So we cannot be far from the truth when we
say the first right of every man is a good
mother; and as the choice of mothers depends
on the men, we add most emphatically, a good
father too. "Oh, that's an old story, we al-
ways have heard that." Old! certainly, but
none the less true: and so a rule that is only in
the primary books of the first series of the vol-
umes of human life—the rule of three. For if
two uncongenial souls can afford to battle out-
alone their miseable rexistence, choosing pan
demonium instead of paradise, the world can-
not afford for them to cast another being like
themselves—an Esau from his birth—into its
schools, its streets and its business.
	The very idea of a baby in a home suggests
a welcome, a place, loving care of both father
and mother, intelligent understanding of its
physical needs, and skill to develop its mind,
leading it always towards the good. It also
suggests plenty of bread and butter, fresh air,
sunshine, and common sense.
	Men who would not expect to gather
"grapes of brambles" will wonder why their
children are not miracles of beauty and genius,
when they marry women of small mental cali-
bre, poor creatures with no thought above the
dressing of their bodies or the obeying of the
dictates of fashion; they will wonder why their
children are peevish and puny, when their
mothers are always "under the Doctor's care,"
miserable dyspeptics, despondent and morose;
and wonder of all wonders, they are surprised if

[Column 2]

their little ones show selfishness, meanness, re-
taliation, when they themselves kept the moth-
er a very beggar upon their bounty, or by
carelessness and neglect sowed the seeds of this
bitter harvest.
	To many men this first best right has been
denied, and many a one because of the lack,
battles all his life with the unseen foes of con-
torted or misdirected impulses. Better even a
mother with a weak body, than one with a
weak mind and cringing soul, afraid of her
husband, afraid of life, and afraid of death.
	Many a man toils unceasingly to give his
children worldly possessions, when the best
heritage he could give them would be himself;
the love and care that binds a house together
and keeps its faith with God; that with un-
tiring patience and sweetness gives to each
child its best birthright—a good mother.
—Women at Work.		E. T. H.


Dr. Mary Safford, sister of ex-Governor
Safford of Arizona, has taken two hundred
acres of land near Tarpew, Florida, where she
intends to plant a colony. She has an idea of
establishing a Sanitarium in that colony to
which invalids will likely be attracted from all
parts of the States. Dr. Safford is an energet-
ic, shrewd, and brilliant woman, highly cul-
tured and exceedingly able in her profession.
She has traveled extensiveiy in foreign lands,
also among other places, and visited Utah, when
we had the pleasure of meeting and conversing
with her. Her views upon the woman ques-
tion are broad in the extreme and she intends
putting them in practice, it seems, by founding
a colony. She has similar ideas in this matter
to Mrs. Martha Coray (now deceased) pub-
lished in the WOMAN'S EXPONENT several
years ago.
