WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.

VOL. 11.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, MAY 1, 1883.	No. 23.


"LOVE PREVAILS OVER ALL"

In the fresh bloom of the apple tree,
	Robins sing merry this morn;
My hear is as happy as theirs can be,
	For love to me is just born.

The skies bend down with tenderest blue
	More golden seem the hours;
All nature takes a brighter hue,
	Deeper fragrance the flowers.

The rippling brook, and the babbling rill,
	Whisper tales of love to me.
the dashing waves bear the same sweet song,
	From ocean, or deep blue sea.

The voice of bird, and the flower's bloom,
	Mystic rustling of the pines
Breathes music, glad with my heart in tune,
	As I kneel at love's pure shrine.

The meadow is donning brightest robes,
	And down in the earth's dark mould,
Numberless agents, busy at work,
	Are producing green and gold.

Fairies are spinning gossamer threads.
	Weaving strange fabrics of art;
Love, a palace is rearing instead,
	Down in the depths of my heart.

The world is lovelier, fairer far,
	Than ever to me before;
Since in my heart has risen love's star,
	Why need I on earth crave more?

L. M. H.


PHANTASMAGORIA.

BY HANNAH T. KING.

"Illusion is the magic of real life."

Phantoms of happiness! phantoms of joy!
Phantoms of bliss without any alloy!
Phantoms alluring by day and by night!
Phantoms that bring me incessant delight!

Phantoms attending long years of my life;
Phantoms unfading through sorrow or strife,
That cast o'er my senses a mystical veil,
And through it is whispered a magical tale.

Phantoms unfading where'er I may be;
Phantoms unfading through all I may see;
If I climb the steep mountains they're close to my side,
And in the sweet valley most faithful to glide.

At midnight, when sleep has forsaken the eye,
Their presence I feel is endearingly nigh.
When I gaze on the stars they will surely be there,
And in mystical language their mission declare.

Strange that such phantoms, impalpable, sweet,
Should render life's pleasures so vastly complete.
Strange that they fade not, though life's in decline,
But as years roll along they the brighter doth shine.

Strange that young life never gave me this joy;
Strange that they never bring any alloy!
Strange that I know on this earth they will be
Immortal—beyond I'm not able to see.

Yet oft in my dreams of the future, I trace
Among the bright myriads some form or some face
That once I'd a glimpse of while dwelling on earth,
And in those brief moments gave mystical birth

To thoughts and ideas, both of heart and of brain,
Gave music as though in Æolian strain;
And as music sounds sweet from a bark on the sea,
So sweet on my ear were these strains unto me.

Strange are the mysteries that girdle our life;
Strange are the phantoms with which it is rife!
Strange are the whisp'rings that language ne'er tells,
That seem to emerge from the soul's deepest cells!


And these voices so subtle breathe soft on the ear,
Breathe into the heart with their heavenly cheer;
Breathe into the soul till it seems not of earth,
And the brain by inhaling aquires a new birth.

And we rise from the feast, as refreshed with new wine;
We go forth, and the earth in new beauties doth shine,
Our soul feels elastic—too light to retain
A foot hold on earth, or with earth to remain!

My Father! My God! these are whisp'rings of Thee!
Though of earth, I can feel—though blind—I can see.
In the phantoms I've noted, Thy hand I can trace,
Filling up the dark corners with beauty and grace.

And oh! may these phantoms forever remain,
Undimmed, and unclouded on heart, or on brain,
Till the glory of Heaven bursts full on my view,
And to earth and its magnates I waft and adieu.


MISCELLANEOUS.

	As soon as we divorce love from the occupa-
tions of life, we find that labor degenerates
into drudgery.—Whipple.

	The grave is a very small hillock, but we
can see farther from it, when standing on it,
than from the highest mountain in all the 
world.—Tholuck.

	Horace Mann once said, "if temperance
prevail education can prevail;" and Superin-
tendent Philbrick said, "the rum-shops are the
antagonists of the public schools."

	It is proposed to have ready for the 10th of
November next, the four-hundredth anniver-
sary of the birth of Martin Luther, a colossal
bronze statue of the great reformer, to be
placed in front of the Memorial Lutheran
Church in Washington.

	If women could vote in Illinois, would Con-
gressman Hitt have dared to present a resolu-
tion to difranchise the women of Utah and
Wyoming? For a fair answer to this let Mr. Hitt
move to disfranchise the negroes, the Irishmen,
Germans or some other class in some part of the
enfranchised kingdom, and see how long it
would take to drop his head into the political
wastebasket.—Ex.

	THE TRAFFIC IN VOTES.—In one of the
larger manufacturing towns of Rhode Island
is a clique of voters called the "Rounders,"
who are in the habit of watching the polls at
elections, and just at the critical moment de-
posit their votes for the party offering the
highest bid, thus often turning the scales. At
present it is reported that they are building
somewhat on the Sprague boom, and some of
them have said they expect to command twen-
ty five dollars a piece for their ballot; it has
been reported in various papers that they stand
one hundred and fifty strong in the town.
Wonder if there are one hundred and fifty
women in that place who would sell their
birthright for a mess of pottage?—Ex.


SCENES IN NAUVOO, AND INCIDENTS
	FROM H. C. KIMBALL'S JOURNAL.
			BY HELEN MAR WHITNEY.
	In our last article we mentioned the circum-
stance of an Italian brother making an offering
of all his money to the Trustee in trust to carry
on the work on the Temple.  Previous to this
the work was almost completely at a stand still
for want of means, and President Young had
urged the brethren to have faith and go on
and the Lord would provide means. On the
day when Brother Toronto laid down his
money the brethren were to meet in Council
and consider the subject of means.  President
Young walked into the council with the gold
in his pocket, when they were debating the
question of giving up the work, and he said,
"Brethren, will you go on with the work and
trust in the Lord?"  And they were doubtful.
When President Young scattered the gold
over the room the brethren were as much as-
tonished as men could possibly be.  Is not this
a proof that the Lord provides means to carry
on His work?  Brother Toronto has since ac-
cumulated means and raised a family, been to
Italy and Sicily, and in his native land,
accomplished a good work.
	In the month of July, 1845, a large caravan
came to our city.  This was a rarity in that
part of the country, and something that I had
never seen, though I remember of one passing
our house when we were living in Mendon,
State of New York, just previous to our leav-
ing there.  I was then between four and five
years old, and I teased my ma, and cried be-
cause she would not let me go with my brother
William and others to see it.  She told me
that I was too little and I would have plenty of
chances when I got older.  I never heard of
another till about a year after we came to 
Nauvoo. Then there was an opportunity for
me to go, as William (who was living with Dr.
F. G. Williams, at Upper Commerce) came to
take me to one, but found me sick with a fresh
attack of ague, and quite delirious to all around
me, so that I knew nothing of it till it was
over.  But now there seemed nothing in the
way to prevent my seeing a circus.  I was just
putting on a white dress, I remember, as the
caravan passed our house, the day being ex-
tremly warm but otherwise it was very pleasant.
My youthful anticipations were at their height,
but alas for our earthly hopes, I seemed
doomed to disappointment, as they had no
more than spread their canvas before a sudden
wind storm came and blew it to the ground,
and the rain come down in torrents, so that
they could not put it up again, and came very
near losing some of their animals.  So I had to
wait till I was considerably older before my
chances came to witness the circus, as we left
the next February for the west, and I never
again had this privilege till some years after
we came to Salt Lake; and by that time my en-
thusiasm had considerably moderated,  and I
have long since became satiated with them.
	After the storm was over,  I accompanied
father and others to see the animals, but the
change in the weather obliged us to dress in
thick clothing, and even mantles and overcoats
were neceseary.  Thus we find life in one of dis-
appointed hopes, till we have learned to res-
train ourselves and become moderate in all
things--then peace and pleasure is the natural
result.  The following interesting items I
gather from my father's journal.  "August 4th,
which was Sunday," he says, "Presidnet B.
Young preached in the forenoon,  warned
the Saints to beware  we would do well to-day to
accept and profit by the same.
	"Monday, 5th," he says, "our county elec-
tion was held.  Had a bee the same day to
get wood for the Nauvoo House;  had one

hundred and fifty teams." By the 20th, it
seems, they had got eight courses on two sides
of the Nauvoo House. But Satan became
more and more enraged as he saw the work of
God continuing to progress, and his power was
arrayed against the Saints, and on the 15th of
September, while my father and others of
his brethren were in council, at Brother
Joseph Kingsbury's, two sheriffs came after
the following men: B. Young, H. C. Kimball,
P. P. Pratt, G. A. Smith, John Taylor, W.
Richards, G. Miller, John Karns, O. Pratt, O.
Hyde, and John Page. These men said they
were requested not to serve a writ on William
Smith. They did not find any of them and soon
left the city.
	16th, father met with his brethren to consult
about what measures to enter into, and con-
cluded to stop the work on the Nauvoo House.
Adjourned at twelve o'clock, and met again at
two. "We had not been in more than ten
minutes," father says, "before J. B. Backenstoss,
a sheriff and lawyer, not a Mormon, and Porter
Rockwell came in. Learned that a mob rushed
upon them suddenly and chased them from
Warsaw. In the struggle to get away from
the mob, Porter Rockwell shot one man, and
Backenstoss one or two more." Orders were
sent to the precinct by the sheriff for them to
be in readiness. "About ten in the evening
Col. Markham went to Carthage, with fifty
men." "My Father in Heaven, wilt thou help
thy people and deliver us from our enemies, as
thou art our Father and our God." He wrote
that on the evening of the 18th several com-
panies went out to take prisoners; Bro. Red-
field came in at the same time with tidings
from Lima. On the 19th, the Twelve were in
council most of the day at Bishop Miller's.
Father mentions one young man by the name of
Tippen, being shot by accident in the camp.
He says, "Visited the camp about ten o'clock.
Tippen died about that time as we were going 
to see him."
	"20th, Saturday. Rose early in the morn-
ing, went to the council room. Two hun-
dred men went in wagons to meet Backen-
stoss at the junction. Tippen was buried;
had a short discourse by B. Young and my-
self on the public square. Most of the army
came home."
	"Monday, 22nd, held council with a com-
mittee from McDonough County, at John
Taylor's. Chose three men to meet them on
Tuesday at Mc-Comb, as follows. A. Babbit,
Bedell and Wells."
	"Tuesday, 23rd. The day pleasant. Much
rumor from Camp Creek, spent most of the
day in council, * * receiving letters from
different parts of the country. Writs out for
twelve or thirteen men,"
	"Wednesday, 24th. All the Twelve and
about thirty others went to Carthage to give
ourselves up for trial. Went before Squire
Barns and were dismissed. We went to see
the jail where Joseph and Hyrum were killed.
Most of the people had left the place; got
home about sunset and met a committee from
Quincy. Met in council at John Taylor's.
Wrote a proposition to the mob; broke up at
one o'clock in the morning."
	Friday, 26th, father speaks of attending
council at Brother Taylor's, the same day at
one o'clock, he says, met for prayer and council
at W. Richards', from thence went into the 
public square, and saw one hundred families
come in from Camp Creek and other places.
B. Young spoke to them, then we went and
laid hands upon the sick, eighteen of them."
	Saturday, he mentions being again in coun-
cil, then went with General Charles Rich to
visit the sick. At three in the afternoon he
and brethren went to the mansion. He says,
"Brother Rose came in with news from
Beardstown. He saw General Hardin with
two hundred and fifty men on his way to

[Column 2]

our city. From thence we went to the tem-
ple, then to see the troops on the square.
My health being poor, went home early."
	"Sunday, 28th, being rainy, few met at
the stand. B. Young spoke a few words, by
counsel, to get in their grain and other 
things. Went to see the sick, Sister Woolley 
and others. * * Met in Council. Bedell
got back from Springfield. The news good
from the Governor Backenstoss went to 
Carthage to meet General Hardin. The
tide of the mob is low at this time. * *
William Clayton came in with a proclama-
tion from General Hardin, and a letter.
He is now in Carthage with his troops. Dr.
Colston came in."
	"29th. At nine o'clock met in council at
Daniel Spencer's. Five Lamanites were pres-
ent. Adjourned at twelve, met at one for
prayer and council, at W. Richards'. * * Bro.
Bent and Isaac Morley met with us to present
the names of those to go west. In the evening
went down to the river. B. Young baptized
three red men, and confirmed them at the
water's edge."
	"30th. The council met at the Seventies
Hall, adjourned at twelve o'clock, as General
Hardin had come into town with his posse to
search for property and lost men. He wished
to have council with the Twelve, so we went to
his camp on the hill. He read to us his
orders, which he had received from the Gov-
ernor; after which they searched the Temple,
Masonic Hall, and the Nauvoo Barn, then
went down the river to camp. The Twelve
and Bishops met at W. Richards' for prayer
and council. We asked the Lord to frustrate
the designs of our enemies and to blind their
eyes, and cause the troops to leave our city.
Held council in the evening at John Taylor's.
Wrote another proposition to people of the
State, that we were going away—to come and
purchase our lands and houses and we would
leave."
	General Hardin, Judge Douglas McDougal,
and Col. Warren met them next day in coun-
cil at John Taylor's. Father wrote, "After
much conversation we adjourned about eleven
o'clock. Went to the Temple with them and
their army. They went all over the Temple.
Took dinner at Brother Taylor's. Me in
council in the after part of the day, with the
same men. I discovered a spirit in them not
to do much for us. Went back to their camp
at four or five o'clock. We met again in the
evening, Brother Andrew Perkins, and Dr.
Colston came in from Carthage, said there were
three hundred of the mob.
	"Oct. 1st. Engaged in council	* * and
visiting the sick. Oct. 2nd. The Twelve and 
others met in council with General Hardin's 
staff. His regiment left early in the morning, 
and the General left about eleven o'clock for 
Carthage. Then B. Young and myself went 
to visit the sick. Went also to the Temple.
They were laying the lower floor for the con-
ference." The same day he mentions attending
the funeral of Sister Daniel Spencer, and from
thence went to W. Richards' for council and
prayer. Says, "Great union prevailed."
	These historical items will be of more than
usual interest to this people at the present
period and they are calculated to instil into
them new faith and determination to perservere
in defence of the sacred principle of liberty,
which we still enjoy (thank God) in spite of
every effort that has been made by our foes,
who seem to have forgotten that persecution
and intolerance beget an increase of union,
and the same spirit and love of liberty thrills
our bosoms, which at the darkest hour roused
our country to action, and inspired the hearts
of the people to appoint for their commander-
in-chief, George Washington, and he was
filled with the spirit and power of his office,
the same as was Brigham Young at the most

[Column 3]

critical moment in the tide of our affairs in
Nauvoo; and we read that Washington was
often led to retire alone from camp, and bow
in secret prayer to that same God of Israel, in
whom we trust, not only to relieve his over-
burdened heart, but to gain His divine assist-
ance in that awful conflict. This people were
placed in a similar condition, and likely to be
scattered as sheep without a shepherd, had they
not chosen to be led by men who were inspired
by a divine power to lead them to victory.
Where if they had not been united, and had
had no other but human aid to look to, or to
interfere in their behalf, they could never have
survived through those early scenes of trial
and tribulation, much less to have carried out
the great revelations and commands of the Al-
mighty. But they knew as well as they do to-
day the necessity of humility and prayer with-
out ceasing, and that in everything they must
give thanks, knowing that "he who is faithful
shall overcome all things, and shall be lifted
up at the last day." We know that a "three 
fold cord is not quickly broken, and wisdom
strengtheneth the wise more than ten mighty
men." We have had examples enough to
show what persecution will do, and what has
been, and ever will be effected by outside pres-
sure, a practice which our foes seem determined
to keep up. No-where can they find another
such a people, whose lives and works so nearly
resemble those of the ancient followers of the
poor and despised Nazarene, who have shown
their faith by their works, and a like willing-
ness to make sacrifice of home, friends, and
all their worldly prospects (as thousands have
done) and what is of more value than all the
rest, their good name. For the last fifty years
we have been looked down upon, persecuted,
driven about and wronged, for nothing only
our religion, and still we cling to it in defiance
of all that this nation is threatening to do, and
why is it that they do not cause us to fear
and quake before them? Because we know that
our Redeemer liveth, and we still have a posi-
tive assurance of divine support if we keep
humble and united. We are aware that the 
"Mormons" as a people, have not yet become
all that they should be. Many among us are
like dead branches—are neither one thing nor
the other; and the Lord says He wants us to be
either hot or cold, for the luke-warm He would
"spew out of His mouth." If we were all in
that condition and would mix in with Babylon
we would meet with but little interference
from them, but rather let us be upon the watch
tower, for the signs of the times fore-shadow
great and mighty changes which have been
long foretold by the prophets, ancient and
modern; I remember, yes, and so do hundreds
of this people, how the solemnity of these
things rested upon my father for years previous
to his death; because he foresaw, in the vision
of his mind, the awful scenes when the judg-
ments of an offended God would be poured out
without measure upon the inhabitants of the
earth, and he beheld the fate of those who are
at ease in Zion; and it will take nothing less
than His scourging hand to wake them from
their slumber.
	Therefore, our present troubles may be
viewed in the light of a God-send, or a bless-
ing in disguise, like all the rest, for it will wake
up many and carry off the dross, and unite
His Saints more closely than they have ever
been. And none others can become heirs with
our elder Brother, to that celestial and eter-
nal glory that is awaiting all who are willing
to take up and bear their cross. Such can
safely leave the outcome to Him who stands
at the helm; but let us see that we keep inside
of the good ship, while being tossed over these
rough billows, or passing through the narrow
places, which all must do before we can ever
reach our eternal inheritance.
	To be Continued.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


A TRIBUTE

TO EMILY SCOTT AND L. M . HEMLINGS
I sang alone so hushed and low,
Fearing o'en my own voice to hear,
And overjoyed by kindly words
	From those around me, true and dear,
And little thought or listened where
The echoes died upon the air.

As pebbles cast in fountain still
	Make wid'ning circles on its breast,
Even so the echoes wandered on,
Reached north and south, far east and west,
And welcome found in many a place
Where they have never known my face.

And little dreamed I all the while,
How by the task in silence wrought,
That answering to my trembling heart
	Should loving messages be brought
To lighten all the coming days
With the soul's bright, life-cheering rays,

Sing on, my heart, and bear to those
	Returning words of gratitude, 
And bear this wish--that all may find
	In house of prayer and solitude,
The source that made this gift my own,
And learn the music round His throne.
		AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON. 
Salt Lake City, April 20th, 1883


HELEN AND VIRGINIA

[THIRD CHAPTER.]

	Not lack of love around her drew
Her isolation and restraint,
For e'en  the meanest weeds may wear
Their grace of blossoms fair to view.
So too, the human heart may bear
The soul-flower love, impure or true.

Oft like the oleander's bloom
Of richest dye, or purest white,
That fascinate the gazer's eye
And sways the sense with perfumed breath;
Yet taste its petals—all delight
Is lost in agony and death.

Alas, that hearts to their own kind
May poison treachery extend!
And that the helpless, trusting walk
Beside the strong and may not know
If those on whom their hopes depend
Shall prove their soul's good friend or foe

And her still face would oft incite
In hearts of these dark birds of prey
A purpose swift, and strong and deep
To know her and win assuring smile;
With power greater than e'er they
Had known by artifice and guile.

And such, unnoticed or repelled,
Turn in still wrath to wreak their power—
"What power have such?"  O, ask of these
Who toil for bread day after day,
How false, brief words, in one short hour,
May snatch a girl's fair name away

And love, though pure, may not be right
To have and hold against God's laws;
And looking over all the world
We find a mystery solved herein—
A broken law the primal cause
Of human misery and sin.

When flattery and wealth are vain
The lonely toiler to allure,
A stronger shaft the tempter brings;
Two souls that else were all complete,
Meet the world's verdict stern and sure,
And joy or honor must retreat.

And thus defrauded the soul's right,
The laws man made, his spirit weighs;
The scales swing all unevenly,
His eyes the written law behold,
And still his spirit swing and sways
Twixt law of man and law of soul.

Amid these 'wild'ring paths of life
Virginia walked as one who knew
A time would come when all might find
Truth, Justice and the way of right;
And ever with this faith in view
Kept her pure soul's lamp filled and bright.


Yet often o'er the silken robes
	Her shining needle wrought upon,
Her face drooped lower, and the tears
	Rose in mute pity for the lot
Of the sad wearer, who had won
	Fame, and a heart where love was not.

Far from her own loved Italy,
The child of song had followed him
O'er paths that reached forever on;
	The vales of peace lay far below,
The phantom hope, was faint and dim,
And still beyond o'er wastes of snow.

And yet night after night she sand,
She sang for love, she sang for gold,
That she might win by lavish gift
	Wrought from her aching heart, the love
That once was found, and now was cold;
	Her voice rang all applause above.

For her, as o'er her sleeping babe
	Honora's gaze in sadness bent,
Virginia's spirit rose in prayer,
Her voice bore strength and tenderness,
As one with gift by heaven lent,
To serve, protect her and to bless.
			AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.


[Column 1]
﻿	REFLECTIONS ON PROPHECY

	In reading our Sabbath School lesson this
morning, (the fourth Chapter of Daniel) I
could not help being forcibly struck with the
similarity of the position of King Nebuchad-
nezzar, and that of the people of this nation,
of which we are a part.  It seems that this
same king had had many testimonies that
there was a God in heaven, who was able to
deliver those who trusted in Him; he had wit-
nessed the deliverance of the three Hebrew
children from the fiery furnace without even
the smell of fire upon their garments.  And
when the Lord sent a dream to him, and Dan-
iel was called to show him the interpretation
thereof, and it was made known to him that
he should be driven out from the midst of men,
and dwell with the beasts of the field until he
should know that the Most High ruled in the
kingdoms of men, and giveth it to whomsoever
He will; and Daniel said to the king, O,
King, let my counsel be acceptable unto thee
and break off thy sins by righteousness, and
thine iniquities by showing mercy to the poor,
if it may be a lengthening of thy tranquility.
Still after this the king walked in his palace
twelve months; and he spake and said, "Is not
this great Babylon that I have built for the
house of the kingdom, by the might of my
power, and for the honor of my majesty."
While the word was in the king's mouth, there
fell a voice from heaven, saying, "O King
Nebuchednezzar, to thee it is spoken, the king-
dom is departed from thee."  The same hour
was the thing fulfilled upon Nebuchednezzar,
he was an outcast from among men, until the
time had been fulfilled that Daniel had pre-
dicted, and his reason returned unto him, and
he blessed the Most High, and praised and
honored him that liveth forever, whose domin-
ion is an everlasting dominion, and His king-
dom from generation to generation.
	And all the inhabitants of the earth are re-
puted as nothing, and He doeth according to
his will in the army of heaven, and among the
inhabitants of the earth, and none can stay His
hand, or say unto Him, what doest thou?  And
those that walk in pride he is able to abase.
To me there seems a similarity in the position
of King Nebuchednezzar and the people of
this nation in which we live; they say, "Behold,
we are fifty millions of people, and how can a
people so few in numbers expect to withstand
the pressure brought upon them?"  Our an-
swer to them is:  The God of the Prophet
Daniel still lives, and He is our God, and we
are His people; and if this nation does not re-
pent and cease to fight against His kingdom,
which He has decreed shall be established upon

			[Column 2]
the earth for the last time, no more to be
thrown down; they will find to their sorrow
and everlasting shame that "Those that walk
in pride He is able to abase."  Oh!  why is it
they will not repent of their sins, and turn
unto the true and living God, who loves all
His children, even the workmanship of His
hands, and is merciful and kind.  They call us
(the women of Utah) slaves!  we were free until
they began to make laws against us.  The only
oppression we feel comes from them.  May
heaven save me from such deliverance as they
propose to give us.  Excuse me, I would
sooner live and die encircled by the chains
which they talk so much about, honored and
respected by some of the "noblest works of
God,"  honest men and women, and beloved
and cared for by God himself, than turn traitor
to God and my conscience, and deny the
knowledge that He has given me.  Our deliver-
ance will come soon.  The darkest hour is just
before the dawn.  We need only to put our
trust in Him, who is able to save, and who
holdeth the destinies of all men and all nations
in His hands, who created the earth and all
that dwelleth upon it, and who setteth up
kingdoms, and who pulleth them down, and
who heareth even the ravens when they cry.
							RUTH.
	October 29th, 1882.


A PLEASANT PARTY. 

	On the second of April the Young Ladies'
Improvement Association of Glenwood ar-
ranged a party in honor of the birthday of
Sister Lucy Heppler. She has been very
energetic in assisting the young ladies in this
place in temporal as well as spiritual things.
She has taught us many varieties of ornamen-
tal, fancy needle work, which has enabled us
to get means in our Association, and also to
adorn our homes with the work of our own
hands. Our President, Rozina Powell, with
her second Counselor, conducted Sister Hep-
pler to the Relief Society House (which has
just been completed.)  On entering the room
she was greeted by the young ladies singing,
"Sweet hour of prayer." She was then pre-
sented with a beautiful hair wreath made by
the girls, each member making a flower of her
own hair, as a token of love and esteem. Sis-
ter Heppler, in a few words, thanked the Asso-
ciation for the present and exhorted them to
continue to be as united as they were at the
present time; said that she could not express
her feelings as she wished, as she was so over-
come with the surprise of the party. Brother
Heppler spoke for a short time, encouraging
the young ladies to continue in well doing.
We then all partook of a good supper, twenty-
eight were seated around the table; after sup-
per a few songs were sung. Everything passed
off harmoniously. In the evening there was a
conjoint session of the Y. M. and Y. L. I. As-
sociations, making in all a very pleasant time.
								TINIA.
	Glenwood, Sevier Co., April 9th, 1883.


Things which never could have made a man
happy develop a power to make him strong.
Strength and not happiness, or rather only
that happiness which comes by strength, is the
end of human living. And with that test and
standard the best order and beauty reappears.
—Phillips Brooks.

	Whenever the truth of God—to whom all
sovereignty, majesty, and power belong—and
the commandment of men come in conflict, we
must say, as the apostle said, "We ought
rather to obey God than man." And when
this is done, it must not be called disobedience
to superiors; but it is obedience to them,
because it is obedience to God, who is above
them.—Alexander Henderson, 1638.

SALT LAKE CITY,	MAY 1, 1883.


[Column 1]
LOVE OF NATURE.

	THE editorial columns of a paper are looked up-
on as being characteristic of the force and power
of the editor to give the public, some ideas of
the topics of the day in general terms, and pre-
sent them in a brief comprehensive and readable
style. Occasionally one may be inclined to in-
dulge in a strain not so prolific of news as of sen-
timent, and give expression to some special
taste in the field of literature. However, people
are always more or less disappointed in the news-
papers of the day, as they seldom can give just
the sort of thing each reader wants, and indeed
it is astonishingly rare to suit everybody, and
therefore an editor ought now and then to be ex-
cused, if he writes upon a theme which pleases
his own fancy.
	The Spring is the season for the indulgence of
poetic taste, so understood all the world over.
The awakening of nature from its long wintry
slumber, the bursting forth of leaf and bud, is
in itself not only wonderful, but delightful to all
admirers of creative or perpetuating power.
There are so many charming sights and sounds,
that one might be always occupied in their en-
joyment. The greatest writers of poetical sen-
timent have rejoiced in the beauties of Spring-
time. It inspires and cherishes a nearness to
nature's heart. The commonest flower of the
field or meadow, and even the dandelion posses-
ses a charm because of its early bloom; then
what shall we say of those so surpassingly love-
ly as to be famed far and wide for beauty and
fragrance? Flowers are a part of literature, so
closely are they associated in the mind with poe-
try and history. The lily, the rose, the sham-
rock, the blue bells and heather and other varie-
ties are so interwoven in poetry, music and his-
tory, as to make them of special interest, and
give them a prestige almost human, yet so gen-
tle in effect, touching the finest perceptions of
the soul, that they awaken only the most deli-
cate sensibilities.
	It has been remarked by standard authors,
that to love nature deeply and truly, one must
be born and raised in the country. Be this as it
may, the lanes, the orchards, the groves, the
broad fields and byways, outside the turmoil
and bustle of city precincts are the places where
one comes in actual contact with surroundings
that arouse the most agreeable sensations, and
the heart leaps out of its narrow depths into the
glowing regions of light and love.
	The birds form a pleasing part of the enjoy-
ment of Spring, the chattering and twittering,
the nesting, the musical notes echoing and re-
echoing, is a most charming element in the
faculty of happiness, and they convey to the
imaginative much more than the mere sound.

[Column 2]
One writer tells us they are simple lullabies, sooth-
int and tenderly caressing, and gently efface
the sad and awful impression which many other
sounds of wild things make upon us; such as
the screeching of owls and the croaking of ra-
vens, the wild cry of plovers, and similar birds.
Certainly bird songs lighten labor; the plowman
in the field often whistles to the tune of the
birds, and imitates their notes. Emotions are
awakened in a ramble through nature's haunts,
that otherwise might have lain dormant forever,
at least during the period of earthlife. One
drinks in more real beauty and feels more keen-
ly the intensity of sympathy between himself
and nature outside of humanity, than in any
other commingling or association. It is con-
sidered a pastime, but it is a real one, that gives
tone to the labor afterwards to be performed.
The voices of nature that are only perceptible
in a true sense to the poetical soul, the hum of
insects, the murmur of water, the sighing of
the long grass and reeds, the low subdued whist-
ling of the breeze, or the song of the trees, the
flutter of the silken leaves, and above these the
singing and melodious notes of the birds, the
lowing of cattle, the bleating of lambs' animal
and vegetable life all astir, and as it were, put-
ting forth fresh vigor, speak most forcibly to the
soul of man.
	There is no boundary to the imagination in
elaborating upon these subjects, and when
one has made his grandest effort the field is just
as broad, and the landscape just as prolific with
food for the poet's soul as though the songs had
never been written or set to music. There is no
need to mourn for the faculty of expressing that
which is beautiful, grand and glorious at once,
for there is an eternity stretching out ever far
away where the mind will be able to grasp
greater truths and give them golden touches,
and where the yearning for expression and de-
lineation of that which one feels but cannot
convey in language, music or painting, will
surely find its realization and fulfilment.
	Come away into the fields among the hills
when the daisies and lilies bloom in their fresh-
ness, while the early morning dew is glistening
in the petals of the lily bells. The lily of the
valley we find in its rare beauty as we "go a
Maying," and what is there sweeter or purer, or
that can call up dearer recollections? The poe-
try that has been handed down as history, says
"the poet, lay on the earth all through the sunny
days dreaming and doting in the flowers," and
the hero "knight sheathed in complete steel
crushed them under the hoofs of his war horse."
What a contrast, and what a lesson may be
drawn from it. But we were out for a ramble,
what thoughts, what memories are aroused, and
how soothing are the voices we hear all around.
The perfume of the apple blossoms is heavy in
the air, dainty pink flowers, so fragile they fall
apart if you breathe upon them; how the soft
winds scatter them over the clover and in at
the open windows. The groves are ringing
with melody, the merry songs of gladsome
birds, and the young saplings bend with the
weight of the feathered songsters as they sway
up and down with their loud, prolonged notes.
The rapture of the open air brings a happiness
that attracts to the infinite, the source, the
motor power that produces the germ of all these
forces of creation, and we are led to exclaim
"Great and wonderful are thy ways, O Lord,
and past finding out." And so we may revel
in the charms of nature and grow familiar with
flowers, and trees, and birds, and sunshine, and
moonlight, and still there is the same unuttera-
ble, intense longing to approach a higher degree
of attainment in all that is beautiful and exalt-
ing, which nothing but the immortal can ever
satisfy.


HOME AFFAIRS.

	WE have for sale in this office "The Alphabet
in Rhyme," on cards for children, one cent
each, also a song "The Word of Wisdom," pub-
lished on cards two cents each. These cards are
to help the Primary, and are so reasonable that
all the Associations can afford to purchase a sup-
ply. Orders from the country will be promptly
attended to.

	ON Wednesday, April 19th, the Primary As-
sociation of the Twelfth Ward held its annual
meeting, Mrs. Ellen Clawson presiding. Sisters
Eliza R. S. Smith, E. B. Wells and other ladies
were present, also Bishop H. B. Clawson. The
minutes read by the Secretary, Miss Hardy,
were a very great credit both in the manner of
writing and reading. The yearly report was
very good. The programme was well selected.
Dialogue, Alice and Lucy Hardy; Song,
Ruby Clawson; Recitation, Edna Wells;
Recitation, Willie Evans; Questions and An-
swers by the members; Recitation, Roy Cun-
ningham; Song, Dex Pyper; Comic Recitation,
Bryant Wells; Essay, Minnie Clawson; Bible
Questions and Answers, by the Association;
Song, "I'll be a little Mormon;" Primer Ques-
tions and Answers; Song by the children, "Word
of Wisdom." Remarks were made by Sisters
Eliza R. S. Smith, E. B. and L. A. Wells, F. Y.
Thatcher and Bishop Clawson. The meeting was
exceedingly interesting, and well conducted.

	IT is to be hoped that the sisters will bear in
mind the proposition that has been made them
by Dr. Barney in regard to students and the op-
portunities before them for the study of medi-
cine, and use all necessary diligence to help
complete the number called for. By corres-
ponding with the Dr. she may be able to ar-
range matters so as to remove any apparent dif-
ficulty. We would like to see such an under-
taking succeed, for truly, there is a lack of effi-
cient, skillful help in cases of sickness. And the
offer that is made, with the facilities to carry it
out, cannot be equaled, and with the great
amount of interest that is taken in education,
that this one branch should not be neglected
and have only a minor place, but the subject of
physiology, health and hygiene should precede
all other branches, in order to prepare the
mind and the body for more active exercise in
other directions. Write to Dr. Barney, Box 555,
Salt Lake City, and get a complete understand-
ing if you are in doubt on any point in question
in regard to the proposition.

	A new edition of the "Correspondence of the
Palestine Tourists" has lately been issued, con-
taining the steel engravings of Pres. George A.
Smith and Apostle Lorenzo Snow. These are
excellent likenesses of the men they represent.
The book is a series of letters, written in 1872
and 1873, by George A. Smith, Lorenzo Snow,
Paul A. Shettler and Eliza R. Snow, most of
them while traveling in Europe, Asia and Af-
rica, and afterwards compiled by E. R. Snow,
very carefully, for the benefit of the reader.
They are true and interesting discriptions of
places and events worthy of note, during the
journey. This book ought to be in all our young
people's libraries, and is certainly an attractive
and useful compilation. Sister Eliza, determined
to place the book in the hands of the people, has
reduced the prices very materially, and now
offers the book for sale at two dollars for
leather and gilt binding, one dollar and a half
for plain leather, and one dollar and a quarter
bound in cloth. It is a volume of nearly four
hundred pages, and includes seventy-five letters;
it is in plain, clear type, which recommends
it to people in years. One can scarcely fail to be
charmed with these letters, containing such a


[Column 1]
vast deal of information of places in the old
world, of antiquities, museums, and art collec-
tions as well as the country which has become
hallowed ground because of its association. It
is written in a style that brings you directly,
among the people and scenes that are described
although historical in fact. To any one expect-
ing to travel over the ground, it would be very
valuable, and to those who remain at home a
useful and pleasing companion. The book is
very suitable for presentation as a prize or gift,
and ought to be widely circulated. For sale at
this office, and the DESERET NEWS.

	ON Friday, April 21st, the Primary Associa-
tion of the Thirteenth Ward held its fourth
annual meeting, Mrs. Lydia Ann Wells, who has
had charge of the Association just one year,
presiding. In this connection we feel it only
just to say, Sister Wells has been very much
aided by her Counselor Miss Georgie Spencer,
who has been constant in attendance and effi-
cient in her labors, especially with the singing.
Bishop Attwood and Counselor Empey
were present also sisteres R. R. Grant,
B. W. Smith, Ellen C. Clawson, E. B.
Wells, and other ladies. The exercises were
good and well rendered, some in a very notice-
able manner. Programme: Address, Victor
Thornberg; Song, Wilhelmina Lardon, "Lost on
the Lady Elgin;" a wonderful execution for a
child four years old; Dailogue on fashion, Misses
Mortensen and Merril; Address, "Little Boy's
First Recitation," Carlos Badger; Song, "Whip-
poor-will," by four members, was very good in-
deed. Select reading, Orlie Childs, "Let us be
Saints;" Alphabet in rhyme, by five members;
Song, Annie Stromberg, "Scenes that are
brightest," this little songstress deserves recog-
nition and encouragement; Bible Questions
answered by members; Dialogue, "The
dream and its fulfilment;" Song, "The little
gray-bird," Lida Stromberg; Essay, Annie Strom-
berg, "Let us be useful;" Recitation, little Roy
Grant, "Butterflies;" Recitation, "Articles of
Faith," very well done, Adelaide Mortensen;
Organ solo, "Primary March," Orlie Childe; ex-
ceedingly well performed. Sister Susan String-
am was appointed as a Counselor to Sister Wells.
Remarks were made by Sisters Clawson, E. B.
Wells, and R, Attwood, Bishop Attwood and
Counselor Empey. Meeting was adjourned for
one year, and the regular appointment for the
next week was announced. After the close of
the meeting cake and oranges were served to
all in the room.

	In the evening there was a social entertain-
ment for the Primary, which was very enjoy-
able indeed.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 181


OBITUARY.

	Died, at Kaysville, August 19th, 1882, Clara Burton,
wife of Frederic Burton, and daughter of Mark and
Hannah Beazer, born October 11th, 1860, at Kaysville,
Davis County, Utah.
	Sister Burton has lived and died a faithful Latter-day
Saint. She has left a husband and one child, besides
many friends by whom she will be greatly missed. She
has been a faithful member of the Y. L. M. I. A. ever
since its organization, in 1876, she has ever been on
hand to do good, and attend her meetings, for duty
never seemed a task to her. She was, in every sense of
the word, an example to the young ladies worthy of
imitation.
	TESTIMONIAL OF RESPECT.—Inasmuch as it has
pleased our Heavenly Father to take from our midst
another of our dear sisters, we, the young ladies of the
M. I. A., resolve to manifest our appreciation and res-
pect to our departed sister.
	Resolved, That we will try to follow the example of
our departed sister in our lives.
	Resolved, That we condole with each other in the
great loss we sustain in our bereavement, and tender a
token of respect and sympathy to her husband, parents,
and family in this their hour of trial, hoping we will
ever emulate her worth.
	Resolved, That we present a copy of these resolu-
tions to her bereaved family, and publish a copy of the
same in the WOMAN'S EXPONENT, and place a copy on
our record. MARY A. S. SNOW, President.
						MARIAM BARNES,
						SARAH H. SHEFFIELD,
									Couselors.

	Died, in Nephi, March 26th, 1883, of a paralytic
stroke, Mary Hannah Miller, wife of Thomas Miller.
Deceased was born April 14th, 1834, Derby England.
	At our last Relief Society meeting the following pre-
amble and resolutions were unanimously adopted.
	Whereas, It has pleased God our Heavenly Father to
release from her sufferings our much beloved sister,
Therefore,
	Resolved, That we do recognize in the death of Sis-
ter Mary Hannah Miller, the loss of a faithful teacher
in the Nephi Relief Society, a friend to the needy, a
comforter in the hour of trouble, a devoted wife and
mother, and a faithful member of the Church of Jesus
Christ of Latter-day Saints.
	Resolved, That we esteem her faithful labors and
devotion to the interests of Zion, as worthy the emula-
tion of every member in our Society.


Resolved, that we condole and deeply sympathize
with the bereaved husband, children,  relatives and
friends, in the loss of a faithful wife, mother, and con-
fiding friend.
	Resolved, That these resolutions be spread upon the
records of the Nephi Relief Society, and that we publish
the same in the WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
						M. PITCHFORTH, President.
						M. E. TEASDALE, Secretary.


GONE TO HER REST.

Tribute of respect to the late Huldah Meriah Ballan-
iyne.

	The funeral services over the remains of Sister Huldah
Mariah Ballantyne, wife of Elder Richard Ballantyne,
were held in the Fourth Ward schoolhouse, on Thurs-
day afternoon, April 5th, 1883. The building was filled
to its utmost capacity with friends who went there to
pay their last tribute of respect to the memory of their
departed friend.
	The services were conducted by Bishop Edwin Strat-
ford. The choir sang:
		"Mourn not the dead who peaceful lay
			Their wearied bodies down."
	Prayer was then offered by Elder Joseph Parry. The
choir then sang:
		"Sister, thou wert mild and lovely."
After which David M. Stewart addressed the assembly
for a few minutes. He said he had been acquainted
with Sister Ballantyne for twenty years. He knew she
was a good, kind-hearted lady, and a faithful Saint of
God. She was always ready, willing, and took a delight
in administering to the afflicted and supplying comfort,
consolation and material help to those who were in
need.
	These kind offices were not confined to those of her
own household, but were extended to the whole neigh-
borhood in which she resided. Many will miss her, and
by her death will lose a kind friend who acted towards
them like an administering angel. But outside of her
own family circle none will realize the loss of her socie-
ty and good offices more than the Fourth Ward Ladies'
Relief Society, with whom she had been associated, and
whose best interests she had labored many years to pro-
mote. She has made many friends who mourn her de-
parture, and whose hearts are filled with blessings for
her and her posterity, who will perpetuate her name
and memory. She has been a woman of integrity; she
has fought the good fight. She has kept the faith, and
has secured for herself an eternal inheritance in the
Kingdom of God.
	President O. F. Middleton was the next speaker. He
fully endorsed all that had been said by Elder Stewart,
as he had been long acquainted with the deceased, and
knew that every word that had been uttered concerning
her was true. He sympathized with the bereaved hus-
band and family in their affliction. He knew, of course,
that we are all appointed to die, and it is but a matter
of time when each one of us will be called upon to
pass through this ordeal. But the Gospel is full of
promises to the faithful Saints of God, and those who
remain true to the end of their lives will receive a re-
ward in the resurrection that will fully compensate
them for all their sufferings for the Gospel's sake. He
prayed that God would bless all present with His Spirit
and enable us so to live that when at last our end shall
come it may be like that of our departed sister. She
lived the life of the righteous, she died in the Lord, and
her eternal reward is sure.
	President N. C. Flygare next spoke. He said it fell
to his lot to be with Sister Ballantyne during the last
hours of her sickness. She had been a very great suffer-
er. Her body was worn out in the services of God and
His people. But she had borne it all with much pa-
tience and fortitude, until the Lord relieved her by
death. Her sufferings are over; she is now free from all
her sorrows and has gone to her rest. He then spoke of
her long, faithful, and efficient labors in the Relief So-
ciety in the Fourth Ward, over which she presided for
about four years. Her mission on earth is done and she
has earned a name that will never perish.
	Elder Lorin Farr next briefly addressed the meeting.
He said he had been acquainted with Sister Ballantyne
for some thirty-eight years, and never saw her during
that time when she was not ready to impart of her sub-
stance to administer to those who were in distress. He
never knew her otherwise than an honorable, upright
woman, faithful wife, affectionate mother, and true Lat-
ter-day Saint. He was in full sympathy with all that
had been said of her by the former speakers.


Bishop Stratford made a few appropriate remarks,
after which the choir sang:
		"Unveil thy bosom, faithful tomb,
		Take this new treasure to thy trust."
	The benediction was pronounced by Bishop F. A.
Hammond.
	The cortege then took up its march to the cemetery
where the remains of Sister Huldah Meriah Ballantyne
were laid to rest.
	Deceased was the daughter of Gardner and Electa
Clark. She was born at Genesce, New York, October
26th, 1823. She was baptized into the Church of Jesus
Christ of Latter-day Saints in 1840, by Elder Orson
Hyde—by whom she was also confirmed—at Geneva, 
Illinois. She gathered with her parents and family to
Nauvoo, in 1842. She remained there until 1846, when 
she was expelled from her home, in connection with 
thousands of others who were driven away by the hands
of a ruthless mob. She went to Winter Quarters, 
where, on February 17th, 1847, she was married to Rich-
ard Ballantyne by President Heber C. Kimball.
	In the fall of 1848, she arrived with her husband in
Salt Lake City. In 1860 she moved to Weber County.
For six years she resided in Eden, Ogden Valley. The 
remainder of the time since that period she has lived in
this city.
	On the 4th of January, 1879, she was appointed Presi-
dent of the Fourth Ward Relief Society, over which she
has continued to preside until her demise. She was the
mother of nine children, four sons and five daughters.
One boy and girl are dead. She has also twelve grand-
children. Sister Ballantyne has been sick with general
debility for about one year, during which time she was
a great sufferer. She retained the use of all her mental 
faculties to the last moment of her life, and died sur-
rounded by all her loving children, with whom she
shook hands and left with them her parting blessing.
	On the casket bearing the remains was the following, 
encircled by a beautiful white wreath:
	"A tribute of Respect in memory of Sister H. Ballan-
tyne, the first President of the 4th Ward Relief Society.
Presented by the officers and members of the Society.
Absent but not forgotten."
	Ogden HERALD, April 6th, 1883.


182	WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. AND PRIMARY
REPORTS.

BEAVER.
	A Conference of the Ladies' Relief Society
of Beaver Stake, was held in the Meeting
House at Minersville, on March 2nd, Sister
Lucinda Howd presiding. Sister Howd's 
Counselors and the Presidency of the different
wards were present. There was quite a good
attendance of the sisters, and a good repre-
sentation of the brethren. The presidents of
the several wards gave verbal reports, showing
them to be in a prosperous condition. Bishop
McKnight, of Minersville, expressed himself
as being well pleased with the reports he had
heard, and gave the sisters good instructions.
Mrs. S. M. Dell read an extract from a sermon
by Joseph Smith, published in the Contributor
of August last, calculated to give the people a 
proper understanding of the vexed question,
whether the sisters have a right to lay on
hands for the recovery of the sick. Remarks
were made by a number of the brethren and
sisters, that were instructive and pleasing, and
the conference throughout was thoroughly en-
joyable. The young ladies held their confer-
ence in the evening of the same day; on the
day following the Primary conference was
held, Mrs. M. A. Tolton presiding.
	ELIZABETH FARNWORTH.

LONDON.
	The Ninth Anniversary of the White-
chapel Branch Relief Society, was held March
4th, in Orson's Assembly Rooms, New Road,
Mile End, London. This hall is now the regu-
lar meeting place of the Whitechapel Branch,
and is well adapted for holding meetings or 
concerts. It is large and well appointed; the
walls are hung with mirrors and pictures of
considerable value. Its acoustic properties are

[Column 2]

good; and in fact is considered the best hall
in the European Mission. It was in this place, 
thus briefly described, that the Relief Society
celebrated by a concert, or entertainment,
what proved to be a most successful anni-
versary. The programme for the evening was
varied and extensive, and contained over
thirty names. Bishop O. F. Whitney, at pres-
sent acting as President of the London Confer-
ence during the temporary absence of Presi-
dent J. A. West, was present, and assisted, and, 
as is well know, is a host in himself. Elders
Nye and King likewise assisted. The concert
opened with prayer by Bishop Whitney, who
prayed that nothing might be said or done but
that which the Lord might approve.
	President Garner, the Branch President, who
presided, then called upon the children of the
Sunday School to sing a greeting song. Then
Sister Ann Gell, the efficient Secretary of the
Whitechapel Branch for the year ending
March 4th, 1883, being the Ninth Anniver-
sary, having been organized March 4th, 1874,
under the Presidency of Bishop Burton. The
Society consists of a President, two Counselors,
Secretary, and six Teachers. Number of 
meetings held during the year, fourteen; Visits
paid, ninety-six; Cash collected from all sources
£7. 16. 9; Disbursements, £5. 18. 11; On hand,
£1. 17. 10. The sisters have been greatly 
blessed in their endeavors, and have enjoyed
a bountiful supply of the Holy Spirit. Pray-
ing that we may be faithful to the end,
through Jesus Christ. Amen.
	Signed	ELIZABETH YOUNG, President.
			ANN GELL, Secretary.
	President Garner then called upon Elder
A. R. Clark to give a recitation from Homer's 
Odyssy, which was well received. Of course
it would almost seem invidious to draw com-
parisons, when all did their best to add to the 
sum total of the evening's enjoyment; but
marked excellence seems to call for a little
special recognition. A recitation by Bishop
Whitney, entitled, "The Old Hat," charmed
the audience and excited much laughter. A
duet by Elder King and Sister Angie Cross,
was given  taste and feeling. Elder Nye
gave "The Moneyless Man," the sentiments
embodied in which were well suited to further
the object of the Society. Lines composed by
President Garner on "The Ca[-]t" were recited
in a sprightly manner by Sister Annie Davies.
The nature, habits, etc. of that feline species,
were delineated in a style that charmed and
delighted both young and old. Some 
verses on the Priesthood, composed by Elder
Garner, and which he recited himself, were
well received; they not only interested, but 
instructed as well. A song, "Timothy," by
Sister Brain, was well sung, as was a song by
Sister Mary A. Spillman. A duet by Presi-
dent S. Spillman and Sister Sarah Edgley,
proved a success; as did a song of Sister Gell's,
entitled, the "Old Maid." Sister Young, of 
the Relief Society, sang "The Merry Mor-
mons," and Sister Whitpin, "The Son of God 
will come." A recitation by a little girl seven
years of age, the daughter of President Gar-
ner, deserves to be noticed; it was given with
such charming coolness and precision. Sister
Mary Garner, the wife of the respected Presi-
dent of the Whitechapel Branch, gave a reci-
tation, and President F. Garner sang a song
with a chorus that the audience seemed to take
up with considerable vigor. At a late hour a 
vote of thanks was moved by President S.
Spillman, seconded, and carried unanimously,
to the Relief Society, and to all those who had
assisted in bringing to a successful issue this
the  ninth anniversary of the Whitechapel
Relief Society.
	The concert was largely  attended by the
Saints from other branches, with a fair sprink-

[Column 3]

ling of strangers, who thus testified by their
presence, their sympathy for, and their desire
to aid so good a cause.
	Signed	ALEXANDER R. CLARK,
		Branch Secretary.

	MOAB, EMERY CO.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Feeling almost sure you have never heard
from this part of the Lord's vineyard, we
thought a few words from here would be of in-
terest to the sisters, as we are all engaged in
the same work. There is but one copy of the
EXPONENT taken here at present, but through
it we are able to learn that many of our sisters
are far ahead of us in the Societies and Asso-
ciations, having the great  privilege from time 
to time of getting instruction from those great
and noble men and women that we away out
here, on the outskirts as it were, so much de-
sire to see and hear.  It seems to me that those
who have these privileges cannot fully realize
the great blessing unless they have been at
some time deprived of it. Nevertheless we do
not feel to shrink or give up trying to learn 
and improve in the way that we should walk
to become great and noble and find favor with
our Heavenly Father. 
	The sisters of this ward were organized into
a Relief Society on February 19th, 1883, by
President C. G. Larsen and others of the 
Emery Stake of Zion, with the following offi-
cers: Mary Jenson, President; Sarah Burnham
and Priscilla Warner, Counselors; Amanda
Taylor, Secretary; and Sarah J. Stewart,
Treasurer. Fifteen members were enrolled,
and I can truly say we had a time of rejoicing.
Meetings were held here by these brethren
four days in succession, and we received just 
such counsel as we needed. I think, and know
in fact, that the Relief Society will help
to unite the sisters, to strengthen and encour-
age them in well-doing, as some of them have
lived here four years without any of these or-
ganizations. We realize the good instructions
that we get through the EXPONENT from time
to time, for although we have a stake organiza-
tion we could not expect the sisters to come
and see us at present, as they would have to
travel by wagons and over rough roads 
one hundred and twenty miles and expose them-
selves. Ever praying for the spread of the
Gospel and the success of the EXPONENT, we
remain your sisters in the everlasting covenant,
	MARY JENSEN,
	SARAH BURNHAM.
April 14th, 1883.


VISIT TO VIRGEN CITY.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	At the request of our worthy President, I
attempt a brief sketch of a visit to Virgen City
for the purpose of holding a semi-annual confer-
ence of the Relief Society of this Stake of Zion,
and also to visit other settlements along the
Rio Virgen. Our company consisted of Apos-
tle E. Snow, Minerva W. Snow, President of 
Relief Society, Mary B. Eyring, President of 
Silk Association, Mary Whitehead and Arte-
misia Segmiller, Counselors to Elizabeth S. 
Ivins, President of Y. L. M. I. A., Julia
Ivins, President of Primaries, and your corres-
pondent.
	Left St. George Friday morning, 2nd inst;
took dinner at Leeds; arrived at Toquerville at
five p.m.; held meeting at seven, R. S. and Y. 
L. M. combined. 3rd. At nine a.m. meeting of
primaries; thence to Virgen City; met with the
Primaries at two and the Y. L. M. at seven p.m.
	Sunday, 4th. Conference of R. S. convened 
at eleven a.m., a general attendance from the
different settlements; the verbal reports of
the different wards showed the Societies in
good condition; we had much good instruction


from Minerva W. Snow and others. Apostle
E. Snow, President of this Southern Mission,
spoke on the duties and privileges of Latter-
day Saints, combining fatherly counsel with
gentle reproof, that all might be guided by the
admonition of the good Spirit and be ad-
vanced in the general interest of the King-
dom.
	At two p.m. a general meeting was ad-
dressed by President E. Snow, touching on
various subjects, speaking quite lengthy on the
evils of drink, censuring severely those who
sold wine and intoxicating liquors, but left his
blessing with the congregation. After meet-
ing, drove to Grafton and held joint meeting of
the Societies and stopped for the night. 5th.
Drove to Rockville; met with the Primaries at
eleven, and the Relief Society at two p.m. A
general meeting in the evening was addressed
by President Snow and others. 6th. Parted
with President Snow and lady, who went on to
Kanab, the others returning came to Duncan
and held two short meetings; an excellent
feeling prevailed; then to Toquerville and
held evening meeting. 7th. Came to Leeds,
and held meeting at eleven; thence home.
We had a pleasant time and enjoyed ourselves
in meeting and associating with the people,
and found a peaceable happy spirit prevailing,
and felt to say, truly these are a people of
God, every way worthy the name of Latter-day
Saints.				F. C. BROWN,
							Secretary R. S.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 183


[Column 1]
A VENERABLE WOMAN.

PRESIDENIA LATHROP KIMBALL.

[Continued.]
	Sister Presendia, in connection with her hus-
band, received all the ordinances and blessings
in the Temple, that those are entitled to who
enter into holy covenants in the order of celes-
tial marriage. She remained in Nauvoo until
the work in the Temple was finished, and as
she was well fitted out with a good substantial
carriage and span of horses, and had her two
boys with her, she was advised to remain, when
she could be in readiness to leave with the
Saints when they started west "into the wilder-
ness." She took her children and team, jand
crossed the Mississippi river into Iowa, and
stayed with a family by the name of Jordan.
However, some of her friends who were opposed
to her going west, and also opposed to the Gos-
pel, heard of her intention, followed her into
the retreat where she was waiting, and succeed-
ed by stratagem in effecting her return to Lima.
On her way she passed through Nauvoo, saw
President Brigham Young and had some con-
versation with him about her circumstances,
and what course would be best to pursue.
Her brother William and his wife went with
her to Lima, and remained a week, and then
she was left alone among a people bitter against
the Saints, and filled with the spirit of persecu-
tion. Time passed and the Saints had nearly
all left for the west, Sister Presendia felt as if
she was at the mercy of the mob, and indeed
plans were laid to destroy her. One night a
subterfuge was invented to decoy her out of
doors in the dark; she felt a great fear come
over her, and resisted, but the tenant at the
mill close by, hearing the disturbance, came
out and set his dogs on the mob, and they went
back through the orchard to the woods. Sister
Presendia says, "then I fully realized the dan-
ger that surrounded me, not a Mormon near me;
I prayed for help and my prayer was answered;
my brother William sent a messenger, telling
me to leave all my worldly possessions and
come; my house was full of good things for my
comfort, but I walked out of it, leaving all be-
hind, on the 2nd day of May, 1846, with my

[Column 2]
little boy seven years old, who had just been
sick with the measles, and not able to be up;
but I was flying for my life; I knew I must go
before the mill closed for the night, and with
the help of my son George, then sixteen years
of age, I succeeded in getting away, taking
Oliver, the youngest, with me. We traveled
all night on the old road to Nauvoo, and
reached Dr. Spurgeon's at day break. There
I took some refreshment, and then knowing I
should be pursued, and that the mob would be
on my track, I went away to the woods with
my little boy, and stayed all day long, fasting
and praying for deliverance. I picked flowers
to amuse him, for it was Spring-time, and I
gave him water from a stream running down
the bluffs into the Mississippi. The Doctor's
house was full of company all day long, but
when they had gone, at evening his daughter
came out to the woods and I went back with
her for the night, sleeping with my sick boy
on a little feather bed on the floor. I real-
ized the great contrast in my situation, no
home, no bed to rest in, nothing to live
upon but my trust in God and my friends.
The next day I got in a wagon and hid my-
self; when we arrived at Nashville, the place
to cross the river, the teams being taken to
the Iowa side, I saw a man I knew was
looking for me; I kept out of his sight until
he had past, and then retired again to the
woods for safety. However, I got some ti-
dings from home through a messenger, and
learned that the mob had been sent, as I antici-
pated, and were searching Nauvoo for me, and
to take my child away from me. My brother
Dimick Huntington had not yet left Nauvoo,
but he sent his sons to take charge of me, and
see me safely out of Illinois, and I went on to
Sugar Creek. This was the first camping
place of the Saints who left Nauvoo. I stayed
in a deep ravine, secluded from everybody, un-
til I could have a few things brought to me,
which I had previously left with my father. I
slept on a buffalo robe at night, on the ground,
with my little child. No tongue can tell what
my feelings were during those days of severe
trial. But I had considered well, and weighed
the matter beforehand, and I felt I would
rather go with the Saints and suffer and die
with them, than live in Babylon, as I had lived
before. When it was safe to travel we started
out on the route the Saints had taken. We
arrived at Bonaparte, Brother Moses Clawson
helped me to make some purchases, as I had
a little money with me, and he carried my
flour for me. The excitement I had gone
through, and the exposure to cold, lying upon
the ground and not having proper food or rest
gave me cold—fever set in and I was very
ill."
	"Brother Joseph Coolidge came along and he
gave me some medicine, which benefited me
very much. This was when we were near
Garden Grove. We arrived safely at Mt.
Pisgah; there I found my father, and my sister
Zina and her children. They were living in a
log house without any chimney and not any
floor. It was a sickly location, nearly every family
had some sick, and many died there on Grand
River. It was thought best for me to remain
there awhile with my father. Very soon after
arriving there, men were sent by the govern-
ment of the United States, to get volunteers to
march to Mexico—from among the Mormons,
to fight for a government that had suffered us
to be driven out at the point of the bayonet.
Men and teams were called for to take these
volunteers to Col. Sarpee's trading post on
the Missouri River. Charles C. Rich and my
father, Brother Huntington, presided at Mt.
Pisgah. They considered my case, and decided
to send me in Father Bird's team to the bluffs
on the Missouri River, and then I saw the five-
hundred men enrolled as volunteers and take
up the line of march to Mexico. My brother

[Column 3]
Dimick, brave-hearted and strong, with his
family, was among the number. His wife
Fanny wintered in Pueblo, where she had a
daughter born under the most trying and pain-
ful circumstances. I was left behind, alone as
it were; I remained at what was then known as
Cutler's Park. I went to keep house for Cap-
tain James Brown, in connection with Joseph
Woodmansee. However, we passed the winter
in the place known as Winter Quarters, now
designated Florence, on the famous Missouri
River. Before leaving Cutler's Park I was
very ill; it was feared that I would die. At
the same time my father was at Pisgah, also
my sister Zina, my brother Dimick in Mexico,
my brother William in St. Louis, my brother
Oliver on a mission in Europe, and anon came
the tidings that my veteran father had died at
Pisgah, my friend, my counselor, my own dear
parent, to whom I looked for counsel and ad-
vice for the future that stretched out before me
like a great unknown and undiscovered desert
unrelieved and barren. My grief was deep
and heartfelt; I had only my Heavenly Father
left, and I reached out in faith to the one
above to open the heavens for me, and aid me
in my loneliness. I was in a new and wild
country without means, and this was indeed a
great change; but I knew the Lord would pro-
vide, and He always has. Joseph Woodmansee
and his brother Henry were building a log
cabin, and they wanted me to keep house for
them, and superintend their home; but I was
no sooner established there, than their father
wrote for them to come back home to winter,
and I was left in charge of their goods and
home. After they left I started a school, which
was a great blessing to the children. It was
very hard times for all the Saints, sickness was
very prevalent, diseases that were brought on
for want of proper food, and from hardships
incidental to the journey."

To be Continued.


THOUGHTS.

BY HANNAH T. KING.

	My thoughts are legion at the present mo-
ment; would it be possible to concentrate them
into a certain forcii let me try; the first
thought that pioneered the residue, was a
conviction of what a blessing a peaceful, con-
tented mind is to the possessor, and also to all
whom it may come in contact, or be associated
with; and who has no undue craving for that
which is not within its reach; one who can be-
hold the grandeur that wealth bestows, yet
feel no envy, but can even rejoice in the pros-
perity of another; that can see others lauded,
and even preferred before themselves, yet feel
no heart burning, have no regrets; one who
can daily retire into their own home, although
it may be humble, but they ever find it "swept
and garnished," with pure thoughts and sweet
influences, and the spirit of peace prevading it,
and they rejoice therein. Some cannot realize
that true happiness is more often found in the
"sequestered vale," than in the halls of pleas-
ure; if happiness dwells in our own bosom we
carry it with us wherever we may be; we do
not look outward or around for it but feel that
is an individual possession; then, life in all its
phases is enjoyable. It is a general impression
that riches bring happiness and enjoyment;
they may—or they may not; the way in which
they are used decides the question; they certain-
ly give the possessor a grand opportunity of
blessing themselves, by blessing those from
whom wealth had been withheld; and 'tis more
blessed to give than to receive; there is a nobil-
ity in giving, and most certainly a nobility in
receiving a gift. Strange as it may seem; there
are those that it is impossible to bless; if it
would be attempted, they would throw back


[Column 1]
﻿the offered blessing as though they had been
insulted, and would seem to expect an apology
for the offering, fearing it should be known
that they are poor! and they call this dignity
and proper pride—and in this pride and digni-
ty they fold themselves up, and feed upon
bitterness and complaining, and envy and re-
gret, and call themselves noble!—such perver-
sion of feeling is strange yet true; some minds
are easily made happy, even by the common
occurrences of life; the Creator has caused
them to see through a lens, that makes every-
thing in life look charming—more or less—has
given them a faculty to receive quickly beauti-
ful impressions; and to them nature herself
looks heavenly; everything within and without
them seems magnified beauty, by the rich ap-
preciation with which they are gifted; the tree,
the flower, the murmuring brook, the song of
birds, and indeed the universal world has
charms for them; even poverty brings to them
an accompanying blessing, from the hand of
love and friendship, that the wealthy know
not; it is sweet to be fed when we are hungry—
the rich know nothing of hunger. 'Tis sweet
to have a new garmemt brought to us when we
absolutely need it—a full wardrobe can never
give this blessing. 'Tis sweet to labor, it un-
loads the brain of too much spirituality—the
luxurious know nothing of such rewards.
Bread eaten by labor, how sweet it is! Nature
has worked herself hungry, and plain whole-
some food is then sweet to the taste. Poverty
is generally despised, and the poor bear the
stigma; yet one beautiful writer calls it "holy"
—it is certain the Savior gave the poor a
peculiar blessing, both in the present and in
the grand restoration of all things. His words
were "Blessed are the poor, for they shall in-
herit the earth;" and He chose that class for
His associates on earth;" yet many that are rich
are "poor in spirit," and estimate their wealth
at its real value—and their heart and their
hand and their purse are one!—God bless all
such, and long may they possess wealth and
long life to dispense it, and bless their fellow
creatures.
	Happiness is the goal for which we all pant,
yet how few really possess it! Why? generally
because they imagine it to be something which
they do not possess, and which others have at-
tained. Happiness ought to be in the possession
of every human being. The great Creator
forms His creatures for happiness, the fault
then lies with the children of earth, the ma-
terial for happiness is all around us—every-
where present. Heaven itself is around and
about us, in the very air we breathe, in all we
taste, touch, or behold; why do we not realize
it? because nature is perverted. Can we not
see it? Can we not remedy the evil? Yes, we
could do much towards it, if we would be more
simple-minded, more child-like, more individu-
alized—but instead of studying ourselves, and
finding out what would really make us happy
to-day, we study other people, fancy that what
they possess would make us happy, when proba-
bly we grasp at a shadow, and while doing
that we lose the substance! Let us study our-
selves, and endeavor to become fully acquainted
with the cravings of our own heart and brain,
take self into our studio—placing it upon the
easel, and see where we can retouch the sketch,
(for human nature is but a sketch at present)
and see where we can make it a more perfect
representation of what the Maker intended it
to prefigure—"Only a little lower than the
angels" was the motto sent forth with mankind
upon the earth. "Alas! how are the mighty
fallen!" Still we need not despair; men and
women are yet upon the earth, that portray the
high destiny from whence they came and to
which in due time they shall be restored,
renewed and regenerated, the posterity of the
gods! The ideas I have just written places all
on the highest pinnacle of the ladder of intel-

[Column 2]
ligence, which gives that knowledge which
alone is power! wealth does not make men and
women great; no, it is work, labor, the sweat of
the brow, the sweat of the brain, and the
stretch of the heart-strings; from such come
intelligence, discipline, appreciation, wisdom,
understanding, courage, fortitude and endur-
ance—of such giants are formed. Who are the gi-
ants of any age? the laborers, the workers, have
we not seen it with our own eyes in our own day?
Who have been the master-minds in the age in
which we have lived upon the earth? Need I
name them? No, they stand before the world
as "beacons set upon a hill that cannot be hid,"
but one, the last, I must name—his ashes have
not yet lost their "wonted fires," one from the
people—born, lived, and died of the people!
a son of a provincial tradesman, possessing no
wealth, yet raised himself to be the first
statesman of Europe, and of importance to-day,
Though he was all this, and poor was still the
entertainer, and the entertained of kings and
princes. The Prince of Wales was a great ad-
mirer of Gambetta, liked his society, and cor-
dially invited him to visit England as his guest
assured him he would be well received by
the English people. There are few that could
have declined such a tempting honor, but
Gambetta probably felt the offer very agreeable,
but not acceptable by him—he shrank from
compromising his position, which he himself
had acquired as the first statesman of Euro-
pean importance, and as the man among men!
It lays bare to my view the true and sterling,
nobility of his character, and his immense
individuality!
	George Eliot also was of humble origin,
though of highly respectable and enlightened
parentage. "The rank is but the guinea stamp,"
'tis the gold that tells the worth. These two
are but types of a vast multitude—bless and
thank the Lord there have been in all ages,
and there are to-day, men and women that
have stood out, and stand out to-day as types of
the grand original, "only a little lower than
the angels." May we as a people leave the
"rank of the guinea stamp," and determine to
be the gold, and if we must have the alloy to
render us current—so be it. "Hands off"—
God is our Father—Christ is our Captain, and
if we are true soldiers victory is sure, indi-
vidually and collectively. Amen.
	Salt Lake City, April 19th, 1883.

﻿Maria Mitchell, Professor of Astronomy in
Vassar College, has received the degree of L.L.
D. from Hanover College, Madison, Ind., the
first time this degree has been conferred upon a
woman.
	A pair of Sevres vases ordered for Queen
Victoria's birthday are mounted upon a pedes-
tal of Mexican onyx, decorated in French
bronze and surmounted by a crown of flowers
of the same material, in rare workmanship;
the handles are likewise of bronze. The
designs upon the pair, on a groundwork of old
blue enamel, represents Venus rising from the
sea and the fable of Europe.
